If $G_{\text{ff}}(s) = 1/G_p(s)$ (perfect inverse model), then $Y(s) = 0$ for all $d(t)$ — **perfect disturbance cancellation**. In practice, the body uses *approximate* inverse models (learned responses).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Formula Table — Homeostatic Control**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Deviation from set point |
| 2 | Closed-loop TF (NFB) | $\dfrac{Y}{R} = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$ | Negative feedback system |
| 3 | Closed-loop TF (PFB) | $\dfrac{Y}{R} = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 - G_c G_p H}$ | Positive feedback system |
| 4 | Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} sE(s)$ | Long-term deviation |
| 5 | Type-0 $e_{ss}$ (step) | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | Proportional control error |
| 6 | Type-1 $e_{ss}$ (ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Integral control error |
| 7 | Feed-forward compensation | $G_{\text{ff}}^{\text{ideal}} = -\dfrac{1}{G_p}$ | Perfect disturbance rejection |
| 8 | Settling time (1st order) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | Time to reach 2 % of set point |
| 9 | Loop gain | $L(s) = G_c G_p H$ | Determines stability & error |
| 10 | Stability (NFB) | $\vert L(j\omega) \vert < 1$ at phase = $-180°$ | Bode/Nyquist criterion |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Concepts

- **Dialysis machines** use PID (proportional-integral-derivative) feedback to maintain patient fluid balance.
- **Insulin pumps** (artificial pancreas) combine feed-forward (meal announcement) with negative feedback (continuous glucose monitoring).
- **Ventilators** use feedback control to maintain tidal volume / airway pressure.
- **Prosthetic limbs** with EMG sensors use feed-forward control of pre-grasp posture.
- **Cell biology** uses the same control principles to explain signal transduction cascades (e.g., MAPK pathway with feedback loops).

## 2.8 Animal Cell — Organelle Reference Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Organelle | Structure | Primary Function | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nucleus** | Double membrane, nucleolus | Houses DNA, controls cell activities | Control room |
| **Mitochondria** | Double membrane, cristae | ATP production (oxidative phosphorylation) | Power plant |
| **Rough ER** | Membrane + ribosomes | Protein synthesis & modification | Assembly line |
| **Smooth ER** | Membrane only | Lipid synthesis, detoxification | Chemical plant |
| **Golgi Apparatus** | Stacked cisternae | Packaging, sorting, shipping | Post office |
| **Ribosomes** | rRNA + protein | Protein synthesis | Assembly robots |
| **Lysosomes** | Membrane, hydrolytic enzymes | Intracellular digestion | Waste/recycling unit |
| **Peroxisomes** | Membrane, catalase | Fatty acid oxidation, H₂O₂ breakdown | Detox unit |
| **Centrioles** | Microtubule bundles | Cell division (spindle formation) | Logistics hub |
| **Cytoskeleton** | Microtubules, microfilaments, intermediate filaments | Shape, movement, transport | Building scaffold |
| **Cell Membrane** | Phospholipid bilayer + proteins | Selective barrier, signalling | Perimeter security |

## 2.9 Fluid Mosaic Model of the Plasma Membrane

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:**
> Proposed by **S. J. Singer & G. L. Nicolson (1972)**, the fluid mosaic model describes the plasma membrane as a *phospholipid bilayer* in which proteins float laterally like icebergs in a sea of lipids.

**Key Components:**
- **Phospholipid bilayer** — hydrophilic heads face aqueous environments; hydrophobic tails face inward.
- **Integral proteins** — span the bilayer; transport, receptors, channels.
- **Peripheral proteins** — loosely attached; signalling, structural.
- **Cholesterol** — modulates membrane fluidity.
- **Glycoproteins / glycolipids** — cell recognition, adhesion.
- **Cytoskeletal attachments** — maintain shape.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a **layered cake** floating on a sea of olive oil, with **nuts and raisins (proteins)** embedded in it. The cake is fluid (lipids can move laterally), and the raisins can drift (proteins float). This is exactly what the plasma membrane looks like under an electron microscope.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

We begin with the standard block diagram where the comparator subtracts the feedback signal from the reference.

**Step 1 — Define the error signal:**

$$e(t) = r(t) - H(s) \cdot y(t)$$

**Step 2 — Controller output (efferent command):**

$$u(s) = G_c(s) \cdot e(s)$$

**Step 3 — Plant response:**

$$y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot u(s) + G_d(s) \cdot d(s)$$

where $G_d(s)$ is the disturbance-to-output path transfer function.

**Step 4 — Substitute $u(s)$ from Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$y(s) = G_p(s) G_c(s) e(s) + G_d(s) d(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $e(s)$ from Step 1:**

$$y(s) = G_p(s) G_c(s) [r(s) - H(s) y(s)] + G_d(s) d(s)$$

**Step 6 — Expand the bracket:**

$$y(s) = G_p(s) G_c(s) r(s) - G_p(s) G_c(s) H(s) y(s) + G_d(s) d(s)$$

**Step 7 — Collect $y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$y(s) + G_p(s) G_c(s) H(s) y(s) = G_p(s) G_c(s) r(s) + G_d(s) d(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $y(s)$:**

$$y(s) [1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)] = G_c(s) G_p(s) r(s) + G_d(s) d(s)$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $Y(s)/R(s)$ (with $d(s) = 0$):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 10 — Solve for the disturbance transfer function $Y(s)/D(s)$ (with $r(s) = 0$):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_d(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Both the reference-tracking transfer function and the disturbance-rejection transfer function share the **same denominator** $1 + L(s)$, where $L(s) = G_c G_p H$ is the **loop gain**. Increasing $L(s)$ reduces the effect of disturbances on the output — *this is precisely why negative feedback is so central to physiology and engineering.*

## 3.2 Steady-State Error Derivation for a Type-0 System (Proportional Control)

**Step 1 — Let $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback) and $G_c(s) = K_p$, $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s}$.**

Then:

$$G_c(s) G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{1 + \tau s}$$

**Step 2 — The error in Laplace domain is:**

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a unit-step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:**

$$E(s) = \frac{1/s}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{1 + \tau s}} = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \tau s + K_p}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Final Value Theorem:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \tau s + K_p}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limit:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \tau s + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Interpretation:**
> For **proportional control** (which dominates in physiology — the magnitude of corrective action is proportional to the size of the error), a small but non-zero steady-state error always exists. This is the **load error**, and is the reason why, e.g., blood glucose can drift slightly above baseline after a meal even when insulin is secreted.

## 3.3 Derivation of Positive Feedback Instability Condition

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop transfer function for positive feedback (denominator has a *minus* sign):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 - G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Routh-Hurwitz / Nyquist stability criterion. For first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \tau s}$ and $G_c(s) = K_c$, $H(s) = 1$:**

The characteristic equation is:

$$1 - G_c G_p H = 0 \implies 1 - \frac{K_c K}{1 + \tau s} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the pole:**

$$1 + \tau s - K_c K = 0 \implies \tau s = K_c K - 1 \implies s = \frac{K_c K - 1}{\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Stability requires the real part of $s < 0$:**

$$\frac{K_c K - 1}{\tau} < 0 \implies K_c K < 1$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Positive feedback is stable ONLY when loop gain $K_c K < 1$. When $K_c K \ge 1$, the system pole crosses into the right-half plane and the system becomes unstable — the output grows exponentially. In biology, this is harnessed to drive a process to a *rapid* endpoint, but if it does not terminate, the outcome is pathological.**

## 3.4 Derivation of Feed-Forward Disturbance Cancellation

**Step 1 — Plant output with both feedback and feed-forward control:**

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) U_{\text{fb}}(s) + G_d(s) D(s) + G_p(s) U_{\text{ff}}(s)$$

**Step 2 — Choose $U_{\text{ff}}(s) = -G_{\text{ff}}(s) D(s)$ to cancel the disturbance term:**

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) U_{\text{fb}}(s) + [G_d(s) - G_p(s) G_{\text{ff}}(s)] D(s)$$

**Step 3 — For perfect cancellation:**

$$G_d(s) - G_p(s) G_{\text{ff}}(s) = 0 \implies G_{\text{ff}}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = \frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Biological Realisation:**
> The body cannot compute a perfect inverse model in real time. Instead, it uses **learned associations** (Pavlovian conditioning) and **pre-set neural templates** to approximate the inverse model. This is why, e.g., the *cephalic phase* insulin response is a *partial*, not perfect, anticipatory compensation for an upcoming meal.

## 3.5 Cell Membrane Transport — Fick's Law of Diffusion

For passive transport across the plasma membrane, the flux $J$ of a solute is given by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

where:
- $J$ — diffusive flux (mol · m⁻² · s⁻¹).
- $D$ — diffusion coefficient (m² · s⁻¹). For O₂ in water at 37 °C, $D \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-9}$ m² · s⁻¹.
- $\Delta C$ — concentration difference across the membrane (mol · m⁻³).
- $\Delta x$ — membrane thickness ($\approx 7 \text{ nm} = 7 \times 10^{-9}$ m for the lipid bilayer).

**Resting Membrane Potential (Nernst Equation):**

For a single ion species, the equilibrium potential $E_X$ is:

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{\text{out}}}{[X]_{\text{in}}}$$

- $R = 8.314$ J · mol⁻¹ · K⁻¹ — universal gas constant.
- $T$ — absolute temperature (K). At 37 °C, $T = 310$ K.
- $z$ — valence of the ion.
- $F = 96485$ C · mol⁻¹ — Faraday's constant.
- $[X]_{\text{out}}, [X]_{\text{in}}$ — extracellular and intracellular concentrations.

**For K⁺ at body temperature:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_{\text{out}}}{[K^+]_{\text{in}}} \text{ mV}$$

Using typical values $[K^+]_{\text{out}} = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_{\text{in}} = 140$ mM:

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{4}{140} = 61.5 \log_{10}(0.0286) = 61.5 \times (-1.544) \approx -94.9 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The Nernst equation explains the **resting membrane potential** of excitable cells (neurons, muscle). The actual resting potential ($\approx -70$ mV) is calculated using the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation**, which considers multiple ion permeabilities.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — Type-0 Steady-State Error

**Problem:** A proportional controller in a blood-pressure regulation loop has $K_p = 9$. The plant is a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 5$ s. Find the steady-state error to a unit-step disturbance of magnitude $D_0 = 10$ mmHg. Also find the settling time (within 2 %).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Steady-state error to a step disturbance (with $r(t) = 0$):**

$$E_{ss} = -\lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{G_d(s) D(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

Assuming $G_d(s) = 1$ (disturbance directly alters output) and $D(s) = D_0/s$:

$$E_{ss} = -\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{D_0}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{1 + \tau s}} = -\frac{D_0}{1 + K_p} = -\frac{10}{1 + 9} = -1 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p / \tau}{s + (1 + K_p)/\tau} = \frac{9/5}{s + 10/5} = \frac{1.8}{s + 2}$$

**Step 3 — Time constant of the closed loop:**

$$\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K_p} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Settling time (2 % criterion):**

$$t_s \approx 4 \tau_{cl} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2 \text{ s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** With proportional gain $K_p = 9$, the blood-pressure deviation settles to within 2 % of the disturbance value in just 2 seconds, with only a 1 mmHg residual error. Increasing $K_p$ further reduces steady-state error and speeds up response, but risks instability (oscillation) — a classic control engineering trade-off mirrored in physiology.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Negative Feedback Homeostatic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point r of t] --> CMP{Comparator in Hypothalamus}
    SENS[Sensor e g Thermoreceptor] --> AFF[Afferent Pathway]
    AFF --> CMP
    CMP -->|Error e of t| CTRL[Integrating Center Controller]
    CTRL --> EFF[Efferent Pathway Motor Hormone]
    EFF --> ACT[Effector e g Sweat Gland]
    ACT --> PLANT[Plant Body Tissue y of t]
    PLANT --> SENS
    DIST[Disturbance d of t] -.->|Perturbs| PLANT
```

**Interpretation:**
- The loop is **closed** — the output $y(t)$ is sensed and compared with $r(t)$ continuously.
- Disturbance $d(t)$ enters at the plant; the loop counteracts it.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Positive Feedback (Childbirth Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Head Pressure on Cervix] --> B[Stretch Receptors Fire]
    B --> C[Hypothalamus Releases Oxytocin]
    C --> D[Stronger Uterine Contraction]
    D -->|More| A
    D -->|Until| END[Delivery of Baby and Loop Breaks]
    A --> END
```

**Interpretation:**
- The output (uterine contraction) **amplifies** the input (head pressure) in a self-reinforcing cycle.
- The loop is **broken externally** by the biological endpoint (delivery).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Feed-Forward Control (Cephalic Phase Insulin Release)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSE[Sensory Cue Sight Smell of Food] --> LEARN[Learned Neural Template]
    LEARN --> FF[Feed-Forward Controller Vagus Nerve]
    FF -->|Insulin Released Early| PLANT[Blood Glucose]
    MEAL[Food Intake Disturbance] -->|Later| PLANT
    PLANT -.->|FB Correction| LEARN
```

**Interpretation:**
- The feed-forward branch operates *before* the disturbance arrives.
- Feedback (dashed line) still operates in parallel for fine-tuning.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined Feed-Forward + Negative Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r] --> SUM{Adder}
    D[Disturbance d] --> FF[Feed-Forward Compensator Gff]
    FF -->|uff| SUM
    Y[Output y] --> H[Feedback Sensor H]
    H --> NEG[Negative Sign]
    NEG --> SUM
    SUM --> E[Error e]
    E --> Gc[Controller Gc]
    Gc --> Gp[Plant Gp]
    Gp --> Y
    D -->|Also perturbs| Gp
```

**Interpretation:**
- This is the *engineering gold standard* of physiological control: a *parallel* feed-forward + closed-loop feedback structure.

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Transfer Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R(s) Reference] --> SUM1{+}
    SUM1 --> Gc[Gc(s) Controller]
    Gc --> Gp[Gp(s) Plant]
    Gp --> Y[Y(s) Output]
    Y --> H[H(s) Sensor]
    H --> NEG[Negative Sign]
    NEG --> SUM1
    D[D(s) Disturbance] --> Gd[Gd(s) Disturbance Path]
    Gd --> SUM2{Adder}
    SUM2 --> Y
    Gp --> SUM2
```

**Interpretation:** Standard 2-input, 1-output closed-loop block diagram with additive disturbance.

## 4.6 Mermaid Schematic — Animal Cell Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CELL[Animal Cell]
    CELL --> NM[Plasma Membrane Phospholipid Bilayer]
    CELL --> CYTO[Cytoplasm]
    CELL --> NUC[Nucleus]
    CELL --> MITO[Mitochondria]
    CELL --> RER[Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum]
    CELL --> SER[Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum]
    CELL --> GOL[Golgi Apparatus]
    CELL --> RIBO[Free Ribosomes]
    CELL --> LYS[Lysosomes]
    CELL --> PER[Peroxisomes]
    CELL --> CYTOS[Cytoskeleton Microtubules Microfilaments]
    CELL --> CENT[Centrioles]

    NUC --> DNA[DNA and Nucleolus]

```

**Interpretation:** Hierarchical map of the animal cell's principal organelles, the standard "labels diagram" asked in KTU board exams.

## 4.7 Mermaid Process Flow — Thermoregulation (Negative Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Body Temperature Rises Above 37C] --> B[Thermoreceptors in Skin and Hypothalamus Detect Rise]
    B --> C[Afferent Signals to Hypothalamic Integrating Centre]
    C --> D[Comparator Computes Error Positive]
    D --> E[Efferent Signals to Effectors]
    E --> F1[Vasodilation of Skin Blood Vessels]
    E --> F2[Sweat Gland Activation and Evaporative Cooling]
    E --> F3[Decreased Metabolic Rate and Skeletal Muscle Activity]
    F1 --> G[Heat Loss Increases]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Body Temperature Returns to 37C]
    H --> I[Thermoreceptors Signal Reduced Firing]
    I --> J[Negative Feedback Loop Closes]
```

**Interpretation:** Complete end-to-end physiological process flow showing the homeostatic correction.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Homeostasis. Explain the role of a homeostatic control system with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Homeostasis is the self-regulating process by which the internal environment of the body is maintained in a dynamic steady state, despite variations in the external environment. A homeostatic control system consists of a **set point**, a **sensor (receptor)**, an **integrating centre (control center)**, and an **effector**. The system operates primarily through **negative feedback**, where the response opposes the stimulus to restore the variable to its set point.

**Example:** Regulation of body temperature around **37 °C** by the hypothalamus. When body temperature rises above the set point, thermoreceptors send signals to the hypothalamus, which initiates vasodilation and sweating to dissipate heat; when it falls below, vasoconstriction and shivering are triggered to generate heat.

*(Marking: 1 mark for definition; 1 mark for the components of HCS; 1 mark for the temperature regulation example with correct set point value.)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between positive and negative feedback mechanisms with one physiological example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Negative Feedback | Positive Feedback |
|---|---|---|
| Effect on stimulus | Opposes / reverses it | Amplifies / reinforces it |
| Direction from set point | Restores equilibrium | Moves away from set point toward an endpoint |
| Stability | Stabilising | Destabilising (if not terminated) |
| Prevalence | ~99 % of physiological control | Rare, used for rapid, finite processes |
| Example | Blood glucose regulation by insulin/glucagon | Childbirth — oxytocin-driven uterine contractions |
| Example | Thermoregulation by hypothalamus | Blood clotting cascade |

*(Marking: 1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for example OR 1.5 marks for the comparison + 1.5 marks for examples. Either breakdown is acceptable per the KTU marking scheme.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse feed-forward with negative feedback.** Feed-forward *anticipates* a disturbance; negative feedback *corrects* an existing deviation. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Always mention the set point value** in numerical examples (e.g., 37 °C for temperature, 90–110 mg/dL for blood glucose, 7.35–7.45 for blood pH). Vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Draw the block diagram** in feedback questions — a missing diagram costs 2–3 marks even if the written explanation is perfect.
> 4. **In cell structure questions, label BOTH structure and function** of every organelle. One-line answers are penalised.
> 5. **Use proper biological nomenclature:** *Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum* (not just "ER"); *Mitochondria* (singular: mitochondrion); *Oxytocin* (not "the contraction hormone").

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Homeostatic Control Systems `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the negative feedback mechanism of thermoregulation. Draw the block diagram of the homeostatic control system and label each component.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Negative feedback is the principal mechanism by which the body maintains homeostasis. The output of the system opposes the input, restoring the controlled variable to its set point. Body temperature is regulated by a negative feedback loop centered in the **hypothalamus** with a set point of **37 °C**.

**2. Components of the Homeostatic Control System (2 marks):**

| Component | Biological Role in Thermoregulation |
|---|---|
| Set Point | Hypothalamic thermostat (≈ 37 °C) |
| Sensor | Peripheral thermoreceptors in skin + central thermoreceptors in hypothalamus |
| Afferent Pathway | Spinothalamic tracts to hypothalamus |
| Integrating Centre | Preoptic area (POA) and posterior hypothalamus |
| Efferent Pathway | Sympathetic (autonomic) nerves to skin, sweat glands, muscles |
| Effector | Sweat glands, cutaneous blood vessels, skeletal muscles |
| Controlled Variable | Core body temperature |
| Disturbance | Ambient temperature, exercise, fever |

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw the same flowchart as Section 4.7: Set Point → Comparator → Integrating Centre → Effector → Plant, with feedback to the Sensor.)*

**4. Mechanism of Correction (2 marks):**

- **Heat-gain response** (body too cold): Vasoconstriction of cutaneous vessels → reduced heat loss; shivering thermogenesis in skeletal muscle → increased heat production; behavioural responses (curling up, putting on clothes); thyroid hormone release to increase BMR.
- **Heat-loss response** (body too hot): Vasodilation of cutaneous vessels → increased radiative heat loss; sweating → evaporative cooling; decreased BMR; behavioural responses (seeking shade, fanning).

Each corrective action reduces the error signal, and once the variable returns to the set point, the response is terminated. The hypothalamus thus acts as a precise biological proportional controller with an effective loop gain high enough to keep core temperature within **±0.5 °C**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the feed-forward control mechanism. How is it different from negative feedback? Give two physiological examples.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition of Feed-Forward Control (2 marks):**
Feed-forward control is an **anticipatory** regulatory mechanism in which the body uses sensory or learned cues to predict a future disturbance and initiates a corrective response *before* the controlled variable deviates from the set point. The corrective signal is computed from the expected disturbance, not from the error signal.

**2. Difference from Negative Feedback (3 marks):**

| Feature | Negative Feedback | Feed-Forward |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Detected deviation (error) | Anticipated disturbance |
| Timing | *After* disturbance | *Before* disturbance |
| Compensation | Based on past error | Based on predicted disturbance |
| Steady-state error to disturbance | Non-zero (small residual) | Zero (if model is perfect) |
| Robustness to model errors | High (self-correcting) | Low (no error-sensing) |
| Combined use | Standalone | Always paired with feedback in biology |

**3. Two Physiological Examples (2 marks):**

- **Cephalic phase insulin release:** The mere sight, smell, or taste of food triggers vagal stimulation of pancreatic β-cells, releasing insulin *before* blood glucose rises. This pre-empts postprandial hyperglycaemia.
- **Anticipatory thermoregulation:** When a person steps from a warm room into a cold corridor, peripheral thermoreceptors signal the hypothalamus *before* core temperature drops. Vasoconstriction and shivering begin in advance, minimising the drop in core temperature.
- **Conditioned salivation (Pavlovian reflex):** Salivation at the sound of a bell is a learned feed-forward response that pre-emptively prepares digestion.
- **Exercise pre-warming:** Heart rate and ventilation increase *before* the onset of strenuous exercise, anticipating the metabolic demand.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Cellular Control and Feedback `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Describe the fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane. List any four functions of membrane proteins.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The **fluid mosaic model** of the plasma membrane, proposed by **Singer and Nicolson (1972)**, describes the cell membrane as a **phospholipid bilayer** in which a mosaic of proteins floats in a fluid lipid sea. The membrane is approximately **7–10 nm thick**.

**2. Components of the Model (3 marks):**

- **Phospholipid bilayer:** Two layers of phospholipids with **hydrophilic (polar) heads** facing the aqueous exterior and interior, and **hydrophobic (nonpolar) tails** facing inward. Provides the basic structural framework and acts as a selectively permeable barrier.
- **Integral (transmembrane) proteins:** Span the bilayer; examples include channels, carriers, and receptors.
- **Peripheral proteins:** Loosely attached to the inner or outer surface; involved in signalling and structural support.
- **Cholesterol molecules:** Inserted between phospholipids; regulate membrane fluidity and permeability.
- **Glycoproteins and glycolipids:** Carbohydrate chains attached to proteins/lipids on the extracellular face; involved in cell recognition, adhesion, and protection.
- **Cytoskeletal attachments:** Spectrin, ankyrin, etc., maintain membrane shape and anchor proteins.

**3. Four Functions of Membrane Proteins (3 marks, 0.75 each):**

1. **Transport:** Channel proteins (e.g., aquaporins, Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) and carrier proteins (e.g., GLUT glucose transporters) facilitate the movement of ions, water, and nutrients across the membrane.
2. **Reception:** Receptor proteins (e.g., GPCRs, insulin receptor) bind specific signalling molecules (hormones, neurotransmitters) and trigger intracellular signal transduction.
3. **Enzymatic activity:** Membrane-bound enzymes (e.g., adenylyl cyclase, ATPases) catalyse specific reactions at the membrane surface.
4. **Cell adhesion and recognition:** Glycoproteins and adhesion molecules (e.g., integrins, cadherins) mediate cell–cell and cell–extracellular matrix interactions, essential for tissue formation and immune recognition.
5. **Anchoring:** Proteins like ankyrin and spectrin link the membrane to the cytoskeleton, maintaining cell shape.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain positive feedback with the example of childbirth. Why is positive feedback considered "unstable" in engineering terms, and how does the body overcome this instability?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition of Positive Feedback (1 mark):**
Positive feedback is a control mechanism in which the response **amplifies** the original stimulus, driving the system further away from its initial state. The output is *added* to the input rather than subtracted.

**2. Mechanism of Childbirth (3 marks):**

- The baby's head presses against the **cervix** of the uterus.
- **Stretch receptors** in the cervix are stimulated and send afferent signals to the **hypothalamus**.
- The hypothalamus signals the **posterior pituitary** to release **oxytocin** into the bloodstream.
- Oxytocin causes **stronger uterine contractions**, which push the baby's head harder against the cervix.
- Increased pressure → more stretch receptor firing → more oxytocin release → even stronger contractions.
- The cycle continues with escalating intensity (**uterine contraction → pressure → oxytocin → stronger contraction**).
- The loop is **broken** when the baby is delivered — the pressure on the cervix is removed, stretch receptors stop firing, and oxytocin release ceases. The placenta is then expelled due to a similar positive feedback mechanism.

**3. Engineering Instability of Positive Feedback (2 marks):**

In control engineering, positive feedback produces a closed-loop transfer function with a *minus* sign in the denominator:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c G_p}{1 - G_c G_p H}$$

The system is **stable** only when $\vert G_c G_p H \vert < 1$ at all frequencies. If loop gain exceeds unity, the closed-loop pole crosses into the right-half plane, and the system output grows without bound — *mathematical instability*. In an electrical amplifier, this is **howl/oscillation**; in a chemical reactor, it is **thermal runaway**.

**4. How the Body Overcomes Instability (1 mark):**
The body **terminates** the positive feedback loop *externally* by reaching a **biological endpoint** (delivery, clot formation, action potential repolarisation). The instability is therefore *finite* and *purposeful* — once the endpoint is reached, the loop is broken mechanically or biochemically, and the system returns to a normal feedback-controlled state. Without such termination, pathological runaway (e.g., **malignant hyperthermia**, **disseminated intravascular coagulation**) occurs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 1**

### A. Homeostatic Control System
- [ ] **Homeostasis = dynamic equilibrium** (not static), maintained around a set point.
- [ ] Walter Cannon coined the term; Claude Bernard proposed *milieu intérieur*.
- [ ] **Components:** Set point, sensor (receptor), afferent pathway, integrating centre, efferent pathway, effector, controlled variable, disturbance.
- [ ] The hypothalamus is the master integrating centre for temperature, hunger, thirst, circadian rhythm.

### B. Negative Feedback
- [ ] **Output opposes input** — most common (~99 % of physiological control).
- [ ] Reduces error, improves stability, but cannot eliminate steady-state error in Type-0 systems.
- [ ] Examples: thermoregulation (37 °C), blood glucose (90–110 mg/dL), blood pressure (~120/80 mmHg), pH (7.35–7.45), Ca²⁺ (~9–10.5 mg/dL).
- [ ] Closed-loop TF: $\dfrac{Y}{R} = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$.

### C. Positive Feedback
- [ ] **Output reinforces input** — drives system away from equilibrium to a *defined endpoint*.
- [ ] Examples: childbirth (oxytocin), blood clotting, action potential (Na⁺ influx → depolarisation), ovulation (LH surge).
- [ ] Mathematically unstable (loop gain > 1) unless terminated externally.
- [ ] Must NOT be confused with pathology; it is a *purposeful*, *finite* mechanism.

### D. Feed-Forward Control
- [ ] **Anticipates** a disturbance based on cues (sensory or learned).
- [ ] Zero error to a *known, modelled* disturbance; cannot handle unmeasured disturbances.
- [ ] Examples: cephalic phase insulin release, anticipatory thermoregulation, Pavlovian salivation, exercise pre-warming.
- [ ] Always paired with negative feedback in real biological systems.
- [ ] Ideal compensator: $G_{\text{ff}} = G_d / G_p$ (perfect disturbance cancellation).

### E. Comparison Table — Feedback vs. Feed-Forward
- [ ] **Negative feedback** = corrective, *reactive*, error-driven.
- [ ] **Positive feedback** = amplifying, *progressive*, drives to endpoint.
- [ ] **Feed-forward** = anticipatory, *predictive*, model-driven.

### F. Cell Structure — Must-Know Organelles
- [ ] **Nucleus** → DNA, nucleolus, nuclear envelope with pores.
- [ ] **Mitochondria** → "powerhouse," ATP via oxidative phosphorylation, own circular DNA, double membrane with cristae.
- [ ] **Rough ER** → ribosome-studded, protein synthesis & modification.
- [ ] **Smooth ER** → lipid synthesis, detoxification, Ca²⁺ storage.
- [ ] **Golgi apparatus** → cis-medial-trans cisternae, packaging & sorting.
- [ ] **Ribosomes** → 80 S in eukaryotes (60 S + 40 S), site of translation.
- [ ] **Lysosomes** → hydrolytic enzymes (acid hydrolases), pH ~4.5, autophagy.
- [ ] **Peroxisomes** → catalase, fatty acid β-oxidation, H₂O₂ breakdown.
- [ ] **Cytoskeleton** → microtubules (25 nm, tubulin), microfilaments (7 nm, actin), intermediate filaments (10 nm).
- [ ] **Centrioles** → 9 + 0 microtubule arrangement, organise spindle during mitosis.

### G. Plasma Membrane (Fluid Mosaic Model)
- [ ] **Phospholipid bilayer** (7–10 nm thick), hydrophilic heads + hydrophobic tails.
- [ ] **Cholesterol** modulates fluidity.
- [ ] **Integral proteins** = transmembrane; **peripheral proteins** = surface-attached.
- [ ] **Glycocalyx** (glycoproteins + glycolipids) for cell recognition.
- [ ] Selective permeability: small nonpolar (O₂, CO₂) pass freely; ions and large molecules need channels/carriers/ATP.

### H. Key Formulas (Must Memorise for KTU)
- [ ] $e_{ss}$ for Type-0 step input: $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$
- [ ] Nernst equation: $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{\text{out}}}{[X]_{\text{in}}}$
- [ ] Fick's law: $J = -D \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$
- [ ] Settling time: $t_s \approx 4\tau$
- [ ] Closed-loop TF (NFB): $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$

### I. Engineering ↔ Physiology Crosslinks (Examiner Favourites)
- [ ] Insulin pump = proportional + integral control with CGM as sensor.
- [ ] Pacemaker = feedback regulation of heart rate.
- [ ] Prosthetic limb with EMG = feed-forward control.
- [ ] Action potential = regenerative positive feedback (Na⁺ channels).
- [ ] Signal transduction cascades (e.g., MAPK) = biological negative feedback control of cell growth.
- [ ] Body's baroreceptor reflex is mathematically a *proportional controller* with time delay (~2 s).

### J. Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks If You…)
- [ ] Forget to mention the **set point value** (37 °C, 7.4 pH, 5.5 mM glucose, etc.).
- [ ] Confuse feed-forward with feedback.
- [ ] Call positive feedback "abnormal" or "pathological" — it is *purposeful*.
- [ ] Omit the block diagram in feedback questions.
- [ ] Write only one function per organelle in cell structure questions.
- [ ] Mix up smooth vs. rough ER functions.
- [ ] State "ER" or "Golgi" without the full term.
- [ ] Forget units in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane and Transport Mechanisms. RMP - Action Potentials - Ionic Basis of Generation - Nernst Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Membrane and Transport Mechanisms. RMP - Action Potentials - Ionic Basis of Generation - Nernst Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cell Membrane — Structural Foundation

The **cell membrane (plasma membrane)** is a **~7–10 nm** thick selectively permeable barrier that separates the intracellular fluid (ICF) from the extracellular fluid (ECF). According to the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972), it is composed of a **phospholipid bilayer** embedded with integral and peripheral proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrate moieties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The cell membrane is *not* a passive wall — it is a dynamic, electrically active, and selectively permeable interface that governs all cellular transport and bioelectrical signaling.

**Structural Composition:**

| Component | Approximate % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Phospholipids | **~55%** | Bilayer scaffold, hydrophobic core |
| Proteins | **~40%** | Transport, receptors, enzymes, channels |
| Cholesterol | **~5%** | Fluidity regulation, stability |
| Carbohydrates | Trace | Cell recognition, glycocalyx |

### 1.2 Membrane Transport Mechanisms — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of the cell membrane as a **high-security apartment complex lobby**. 
> - **Small, uncharged people (O₂, CO₂)** walk straight in (simple diffusion).
> - **VIPs with badges (glucose via carriers)** get escorted by doormen (facilitated diffusion).
> - **Large items (Na⁺/K⁺ pumps)** require a dedicated maintenance crew actively working against the crowd (active transport).

**Two Master Categories of Transport:**

1. **Passive Transport** — Movement *down* the electrochemical gradient. **No ATP required.** (Diffusion, Osmosis, Facilitated Diffusion)
2. **Active Transport** — Movement *against* the electrochemical gradient. **Requires ATP.** (Primary and Secondary Active Transport)

### 1.3 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** The steady-state voltage difference (~ **−70 mV** in a typical neuron, with the inside negative relative to the outside) that exists across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is *not* firing an action potential.

### 1.4 Action Potential — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action Potential (AP):** A rapid, transient, all-or-none depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential (peaking near **+30 to +40 mV**) that propagates along an excitable cell (neuron, muscle) as a bioelectrical signal.

### 1.5 Nernst Potential — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nernst Potential (Equilibrium Potential, E_ion):** The precise membrane voltage at which the electrical force pulling an ion across the membrane exactly balances the chemical (concentration) force pushing it — i.e., the ion's **net electrochemical flux is zero**.

### 1.6 Physical Constants (Memorize These!)

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol** (≈ **96,500 C/mol**)
- **Absolute Temperature (T):** For 37 °C body temperature, T = **310 K**
- **RT/F** at 37 °C = **26.71 mV** (frequently rounded to **~26.7 mV**)
- **2.303 RT/F** at 37 °C = **61.54 mV** (frequently rounded to **~61.5 mV**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Transport Mechanism Classification

**A. Passive Transport (Down gradient, no ATP)**

1. **Simple Diffusion**
   - Mechanism: Lipid-soluble, small, uncharged molecules (O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones) dissolve through the lipid bilayer.
   - Rate obeys **Fick's Law of Diffusion**.

2. **Facilitated Diffusion**
   - Mechanism: Carrier proteins or channel proteins shuttle polar/charged molecules (glucose, ions).
   - **Saturation kinetics** — follows Michaelis-Menten behavior.

3. **Osmosis**
   - Net movement of **water** across a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration region.

**B. Active Transport (Against gradient, requires ATP)**

1. **Primary Active Transport**
   - Direct ATP hydrolysis powers the pump.
   - **Classic example: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, 1 ATP consumed).
   - **Ca²⁺-ATPase** (SERCA pump in ER).

2. **Secondary Active Transport (Cotransport)**
   - Uses the electrochemical gradient (of Na⁺) created by primary pumps as energy.
   - **Symport** (same direction, e.g., Na⁺-glucose cotransporter SGLT1).
   - **Antiport** (opposite direction, e.g., Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger, NCX).

### 2.2 Why RMP is ~ −70 mV? — The Ionic Logic

- ICF has **high K⁺ (~140 mM)**, **low Na⁺ (~10 mM)**, **high Cl⁻ (~4 mM)**, and **impermeant anions (proteins, A⁻)**.
- ECF has **high Na⁺ (~145 mM)**, **low K⁺ (~4 mM)**, **high Cl⁻ (~120 mM)**.
- The membrane at rest is **~25× more permeable to K⁺ than Na⁺** due to **leak K⁺ channels**.
- K⁺ diffuses OUT → leaves behind impermeable A⁻ → membrane polarizes with **inside negative, outside positive**.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (electrogenic: 3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in) actively restores ionic gradients, contributing **~−3 to −5 mV** to RMP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Nernst-Planck framework is the foundation of **biosensor design**, **patch-clamp amplifiers**, **EEG/ECG signal acquisition**, and **implantable electrode interfaces**. Bioelectric potential measurement is essentially measuring Nernst/Goldman-derived voltages.

### 2.3 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

Because the membrane is permeable to **multiple ions** simultaneously, the true resting potential is a *weighted average* of the Nernst potentials of the permeant ions, weighted by their **relative permeabilities (P)**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Formula | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Nernst (log₁₀ form) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Nernst at 37 °C (log) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV |
| 4 | Nernst at 37 °C (ln) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{26.7}{z} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV |
| 5 | GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Fick's Law (Diffusion flux) | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | mol/(m²·s) |
| 7 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$ | Pascals (Pa) |
| 8 | Nernst-Planck (full flux) | $J = -D\left(\dfrac{dC}{dx}\right) - \dfrac{zF}{RT} \, D \, C \, \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | mol/(m²·s) |
| 9 | Conductance / Current | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | Amperes (A) |
| 10 | Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP | — |
| 11 | Body Temperature Conversion | T(K) = 273.15 + 37 = **310 K** | K |
| 12 | RT/F at 310 K | = 26.71 mV | mV |
| 13 | 2.303 RT/F at 310 K | = 61.54 mV | mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for z (valency):** 
> - For **cations** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺): use **+1, +2**.
> - For **anions** (Cl⁻): use **−1** in numerator of the log form — but more commonly, write Cl with concentrations INVERTED so that z = +1 always. See Section 3 derivation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Full Rigor)

**Starting premise:** At electrochemical equilibrium, the **electrical work** moving one mole of ions across the membrane must equal the **chemical work** of diffusing down the concentration gradient.

**Step 1: Chemical (osmotic) work for moving 1 mole of ions from [ion]_in to [ion]_out**

$$
W_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

*Logic:* This is the standard free energy change of moving 1 mole from low to high concentration.

**Step 2: Electrical work for moving 1 mole of ions across a potential difference E**

For an ion with valence z, moving across potential E_ion:

$$
W_{elec} = zFE_{ion}
$$

*Logic:* Each mole of z-valent ions carries charge zF, multiplied by voltage E gives work.

**Step 3: At equilibrium, the two works are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction**

$$
zFE_{ion} = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

The negative sign appears because the electrical force opposes the chemical force at equilibrium. Rearranging:

$$
E_{ion} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Convention — Invert the log argument so the equation is conventionally positive**

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the inversion?** Some textbooks write the ratio as [out]/[in]. Both forms are correct; *you must know which convention your course uses.* KTU examiners typically use the **[in]/[out] form** (gives positive E for K⁺). Be consistent.

**Step 5: Convert natural log to log base 10 (engineering-friendly form)**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical constants at 37 °C (T = 310 K)**

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.06154 \text{ V} = 61.54 \text{ mV}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{E_{ion}(\text{mV}) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-style)

#### **Example 1: Calculate E_K (Nernst potential for K⁺)**

Given: $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM, T = 37 °C, z = +1.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{4}\right)
$$

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(35)
$$

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times 1.5441
$$

$$
\boxed{E_K = -94.96 \text{ mV} \approx -95 \text{ mV}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Recap:** We used [in]/[out] convention. For K⁺, [in] >> [out], so log is **positive** — but z = +1 gives the result as **negative**, meaning the inside must be **~95 mV negative** to balance K⁺ efflux. This is consistent with biology.

---

#### **Example 2: Calculate E_Na (Nernst potential for Na⁺)**

Given: $[Na^+]_{in} = 10$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, T = 37 °C, z = +1.

$$
E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{10}{145}\right)
$$

$$
E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.06897)
$$

$$
E_{Na} = 61.5 \times (-1.1614)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{Na} = +71.43 \text{ mV} \approx +60 \text{ mV}}
$$

This is the **driving force pulling Na⁺ INTO the cell** during depolarization.

---

#### **Example 3: Calculate E_Ca (Nernst potential for Ca²⁺)**

Given: $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 10^{-4}$ mM (100 nM), $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2$ mM, z = +2, T = 37 °C.

$$
E_{Ca} = \frac{61.5}{+2} \log_{10}\left(\frac{10^{-4}}{2}\right)
$$

$$
E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times (-4.3010)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{Ca} = -132.26 \text{ mV} \approx +132 \text{ mV (using [in]/[out])}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Wait — recheck sign:* $\log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-5}) = -4.3010$. With z = +2 in denominator, 30.75 × (−4.3010) = **−132.26 mV**. The huge negative value means the inside must be very negative to hold Ca²⁺ out. This is why [Ca²⁺]_i is kept at nanomolar levels.

---

#### **Example 4: E_Cl (using inverted convention for anions)**

Given: $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$ mM, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 120$ mM. For anions, **invert the ratio** OR use z = +1 with [out]/[in] (KTU's preferred clarity method).

$$
E_{Cl} = -\frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[Cl^-]_{in}}{[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)
$$

$$
E_{Cl} = -61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{120}\right)
$$

$$
E_{Cl} = -61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0333)
$$

$$
E_{Cl} = -61.5 \times (-1.477)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{Cl} = +90.84 \text{ mV}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Equilibrium Potentials at 37 °C

| Ion | [in] mM | [out] mM | z | E_ion (mV) | Biological Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 140 | 4 | +1 | **−95** | Dominates RMP |
| Na⁺ | 10 | 145 | +1 | **+60** | Drives depolarization |
| Ca²⁺ | 10⁻⁴ | 2 | +2 | **+132** | Triggers exocytosis/contract. |
| Cl⁻ | 4 | 120 | −1 | **−90** (≈ E_Cl ≈ Vm in some cells) | Stabilizes RMP |
| HCO₃⁻ | 12 | 24 | −1 | **−18** | pH buffering |

---

### 3.4 Action Potential — Phases & Ionic Basis (KTU High-Yield)

The action potential has **5 canonical phases** in a ventricular myocyte (slight variation in neurons):

| Phase | Voltage Change | Dominant Current | Channel / Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization** | −90 → +30 mV (very fast upstroke) | **I_Na (inward Na⁺)** | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open |
| **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization** (notch) | +30 → 0 mV | **I_to (transient K⁺ efflux)** | Voltage-gated K⁺ channels (fast) + Na⁺ channel inactivation |
| **Phase 2 — Plateau** (cardiac only) | ~0 mV, sustained | **Slow Ca²⁺ influx** (L-type Ca²⁺ channels) balanced by K⁺ efflux | L-type Ca²⁺ channels vs. delayed rectifier K⁺ |
| **Phase 3 — Repolarization** | 0 → −90 mV | **I_K (outward K⁺)** dominates | Delayed rectifier K⁺ channels; L-type Ca²⁺ closes |
| **Phase 4 — Resting / RMP** | Stable at ~ −90 mV | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores gradients | Leak K⁺ channels, Na⁺/K⁺ pump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neuronal AP note:** Neurons do **NOT** have a Phase 2 plateau. They go 0 → +30 (Phase 0) → rapid repolarization (Phase 3) → hyperpolarization (afterhyperpolarization) → return to RMP.

**Key Properties of Action Potential (KTU Favorite):**
- **All-or-None Law:** Once threshold (~ **−55 mV**) is reached, AP fires at full amplitude.
- **Threshold:** ~ **−55 mV** for Na⁺ channel activation.
- **Refractory Periods:**
  - **Absolute Refractory Period** — no new AP possible (Na⁺ channels inactivated).
  - **Relative Refractory Period** — stronger-than-normal stimulus needed.
- **Propagation:** Continuous (unmyelinated) or **Saltatory** (myelinated — faster, less energy).

---

### 3.5 Nernst-Planck Equation (Advanced Note for 14-mark Q)

The Nernst-Planck equation gives the **total ionic flux** J (mol m⁻² s⁻¹) under combined diffusion and electromigration:

$$
J = -D \left(\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{zF}{RT} D \, C \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\right)
$$

**Term 1:** Fickian diffusion flux.
**Term 2:** Electromigration (drift) flux.

At **electrochemical equilibrium**, J = 0 for that ion → solving for V across the membrane gives the Nernst equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, 3 marks]**

> **Define Resting Membrane Potential (RMP). Mention its typical value and the ion primarily responsible for it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** It is the steady-state potential difference that exists across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is not excited, with the inside of the cell electrically negative with respect to the outside.

- **Typical value:** ~ **−70 mV** (neurons), **−90 mV** (ventricular cardiomyocytes).
- **Primary ion responsible:** **Potassium (K⁺)** — the high resting permeability to K⁺ (via leak K⁺ channels) and the resulting K⁺ efflux down its concentration gradient is the dominant determinant of RMP.
- **Supporting contributor:** The electrogenic **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in) directly contributes about −3 to −5 mV.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark value + 1 mark ion & mechanism]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, 3 marks]**

> **State the Nernst equation. What does it calculate and what are the standard values of constants used at 37 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nernst equation** calculates the **equilibrium (reversal) potential** for a single ion — i.e., the membrane voltage at which there is no net electrochemical flux of that ion.

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

Or in log₁₀ form:

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

**Constants at body temperature (T = 37 °C = 310 K):**
- R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- F = **96,485 C/mol**
- 2.303 RT/F = **61.54 mV** (or 61.5 mV)
- RT/F = **26.71 mV**

Therefore the working formula is:

$$
\boxed{E_{ion} \text{ (mV)} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)}
$$

**[3 marks: 1 mark statement + 1 mark constants + 1 mark boxed formula]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model QP Style]

**Q. (a)** Define and derive the **Nernst equation** for an ion. List the standard constants used and explain its significance in determining the membrane potential of excitable cells. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Calculate the **Nernst potential for K⁺** at 37 °C given: $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. Comment on the biological significance of the result. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Definition [1 mark]:**
The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential of a single ion across a membrane — the voltage at which the chemical (concentration) force and the electrical force on the ion are exactly balanced, resulting in **zero net ionic flux**.

**Derivation [4 marks]:**

At electrochemical equilibrium, the work done by the electrical field equals the chemical (osmotic) work:

Chemical work for 1 mole moving from [ion]_in to [ion]_out:

$$
W_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

Electrical work moving 1 mole of ions of valence z across voltage E_ion:

$$
W_{elec} = zFE_{ion}
$$

Equating magnitudes at equilibrium (with a negative sign since they oppose):

$$
zFE_{ion} = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

Rearranging and inverting the log:

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

Converting to log base 10:

$$
E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

**Constants [1 mark]:**
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, T = 310 K (37 °C)
- 2.303 RT/F at 37 °C = **61.54 mV**

**Significance [1 mark]:**
The Nernst equation is the foundation of all bioelectric phenomena. It explains why K⁺ (the most permeant resting ion) generates a negative RMP, and why Na⁺ (high outside, low inside) drives depolarization in Phase 0 of the action potential. It is the basis for understanding ECG, EEG, EMG, and neural signaling.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

Given: $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, T = 37 °C, z = +1.

**Step 1:** Apply the Nernst equation (log₁₀ form):

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{150}{5}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{150}{5} = 30
$$

**Step 4:** Take the log:

$$
\log_{10}(30) = 1.4771
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply:

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times 1.4771
$$

$$
E_K = +90.84 \text{ mV}
$$

Wait — sign correction. With [in]/[out] convention and z = +1, the formula gives:

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(30) = +90.84 \text{ mV}
$$

But biologically the inside is **negative** at rest. The sign convention: many textbooks write the equation with the **ratio as [out]/[in]** in the natural log form, which flips the sign. Using:

$$
E_{ion} = -\frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)
$$

we get $E_K = -61.5 \times \log_{10}(5/150) = -61.5 \times (-1.4771) = -90.84$ mV.

$$
\boxed{E_K \approx -90.84 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Biological Significance [1 mark]:**
The value of $E_K \approx -91$ mV represents the voltage at which K⁺ flux is zero. Since the actual RMP (~ −70 mV) is **less negative** than E_K, there is a small but continuous K⁺ efflux through leak channels at rest, which (combined with the Na⁺/K⁺ pump) sustains the RMP. The closeness of E_K to the resting potential confirms K⁺ is the principal determinant of RMP.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model QP Style]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **ionic basis of action potential generation** in a **neuron**, with a neat labeled diagram of the various phases and the underlying ion channels involved. Mention the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** State and explain the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation**. Why is it more physiologically realistic than the simple Nernst equation? Compute the resting membrane potential given: $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 10$ mM, $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM, $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM; with relative permeabilities $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ionic Basis of Action Potential [7 marks]:**

The neuronal action potential is a transient, all-or-none wave of membrane depolarization that propagates along the axon, driven by voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels. The **resting membrane potential (~ −70 mV)** is maintained primarily by K⁺ leak channels and the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase.

**Phase 0 — Depolarization [1.5 marks]:**
When a stimulus depolarizes the membrane to the **threshold (~ −55 mV)**, **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open rapidly. Na⁺ rushes in down its steep electrochemical gradient (E_Na ≈ +60 mV), driving V_m toward E_Na. The membrane potential reverses, peaking at about **+30 to +40 mV**.

**Phase 1 — Repolarization [1.5 marks]:**
At peak depolarization, Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (fast inactivation gate closes) and **voltage-gated K⁺ channels** (delayed rectifier) open. K⁺ efflux drives V_m back toward E_K (~ −95 mV). Some neurons show a brief hyperpolarization (afterhyperpolarization) before returning to RMP.

**Role of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase [2 marks]:**
The pump uses **1 ATP** to export **3 Na⁺** and import **2 K⁺** per cycle. It is **electrogenic** (net export of +1 charge per cycle), contributing ~ −3 to −5 mV to RMP. It restores the ionic gradients after each AP, ensuring the cell is ready to fire again. Without it, gradients would slowly dissipate.

**Threshold & All-or-None [1 mark]:**
Subthreshold stimuli produce only local (graded) potentials. Once −55 mV is crossed, an AP of fixed amplitude is triggered. The absolute refractory period (during Na⁺ inactivation) limits the maximum firing frequency.

**Diagram [1 mark]:** (See Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for reference)

---

**(b) Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation [7 marks]:**

**Statement [1 mark]:**

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

Or equivalently in mV at 37 °C:

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

**Why GHK is more realistic [2 marks]:**
- The Nernst equation considers only **one ion** in isolation.
- Real membranes are permeable to **multiple ions** simultaneously.
- GHK is a **weighted average** of Nernst potentials, weighted by **relative permeabilities (P)**.
- When P_K ≫ P_Na, the membrane potential naturally skews toward E_K, explaining the resting state.
- During an AP, when Na⁺ channels open, P_Na transiently exceeds P_K, and the GHK equation correctly predicts the shift in V_m toward E_Na.

**Numerical Computation [4 marks]:**

Given permeabilities $P_K = 1$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$.

**Step 1:** Compute numerator (N):

$$
N = P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i
$$
$$
N = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)
$$
$$
N = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator (D):

$$
D = P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o
$$
$$
D = (1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(120)
$$
$$
D = 140 + 0.4 + 54 = 194.4
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{N}{D} = \frac{11.6}{194.4} = 0.05967
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the log:

$$
\log_{10}(0.05967) = -1.2243
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by 61.5:

$$
V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.2243)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_m = -75.29 \text{ mV} \approx -75 \text{ mV}}
$$

This closely matches the typical neuronal RMP of ~ **−70 mV**, validating the GHK model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mix-up:** Forgetting whether z = +1 or −1 for anions causes sign errors. **Pro tip:** Always use z = +1 and **invert the log ratio** for anions. Alternatively, write Cl⁻ concentrations in inverted order in the GHK equation (see Step 1 above).
> 2. **Temperature Mix-up:** Examiners may give T = 27 °C (300 K) instead of 37 °C. Recompute RT/F accordingly: at 27 °C, 2.303 RT/F = **58.16 mV**.
> 3. **Log vs. Ln:** Some students lose 2 marks by writing $\ln$ without the **2.303 conversion factor**.
> 4. **Forgetting to use [in]/[out] vs. [out]/[in]:** Be consistent. State your convention explicitly in the answer.
> 5. **GHK computation errors:** Permeability weights are commonly **mis-typed** (e.g., 0.04 vs 0.4). Recheck the Q's data.
> 6. **Units:** Always convert mM to M (or keep mM throughout — the ratio is dimensionless, so units cancel). **Final V_m must be in mV**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU 2024 Module 1 Bioelectricity Essentials**

- [ ] **RMP = −70 mV (neuron), −90 mV (myocyte)**; K⁺ is the chief determinant.
- [ ] **E_K ≈ −95 mV**, **E_Na ≈ +60 mV**, **E_Ca ≈ +132 mV**, **E_Cl ≈ −90 mV** — at 37 °C.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation (final form):** $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$ mV (or 58.1 mV at 27 °C).
- [ ] **Constants to memorize:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, T (body) = 310 K.
- [ ] **Na⁺/K⁺ pump:** 3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per ATP; **electrogenic** (net +1 outward charge).
- [ ] **Threshold for AP:** **−55 mV**; peak: **+30 to +40 mV**.
- [ ] **Phase 0:** Na⁺ influx (fast Na⁺ channels). **Phase 3:** K⁺ efflux (delayed rectifier). **Phase 2 (cardiac):** Ca²⁺ plateau via L-type Ca²⁺ channels.
- [ ] **All-or-None Law** + **Absolute/Relative Refractory Periods** are KTU favorites.
- [ ] **GHK equation** weighs ions by **permeability**, not just concentration — it is the *real* model.
- [ ] **Nernst-Planck** = Fickian diffusion + Electromigration; reduces to Nernst at zero net flux.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** ECG/EEG/EMG electrodes, defibrillators, pacemakers, neural stimulators, biosensors — all rely on these bioelectric principles.
- [ ] **Sign convention tip:** Write down whether you are using [in]/[out] or [out]/[in] — and stick to it for the entire answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation:** A quantitative biophysical expression that determines the **steady-state resting membrane potential** ($V_m$) of a biological cell by accounting for the simultaneous diffusion of multiple ionic species (typically $Na^+$, $K^+$, and $Cl^-$) across a **uniformly polarized lipid bilayer**, weighted by their **relative membrane permeabilities**. Mathematically, the GHK equation (also called the **constant field equation**) is expressed as: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_{K}[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right]$$ Where: - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin (**310 K at 37 °C body temperature**) - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - $P_X$ = Permeability of the membrane to ion *X* (in m/s) - $[X]_o$ and $[X]_i$ = Extracellular and intracellular concentrations of ion *X* (in mM) At physiological body temperature (**37 °C**), the prefactor $2.303 \, RT/F$ evaluates to a convenient **constant of 61.5 mV**, allowing the equation to be rewritten in base-10 logarithm form: $$V_m = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_{K}[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right] \;\;\text{(in mV)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The GHK equation is a direct extension of the **Nernst Equation** (which considers only a single ion). It is a *cornerstone* of bioelectricity and is mandatory for the **Module 1** unit on *Cell Membrane Potentials and the Origin of Bioelectric Signals*. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banquet Hall Turnstile" Model Imagine a **packed banquet hall** (the intracellular space) with a **single turnstile door** (the cell membrane) leading out to a **street** (the extracellular space). Three types of guests are trying to leave: 1. **Potassium guests ($K^+$)** — Fast movers, light baggage. They slip through the turnstile easily. 2. **Sodium guests ($Na^+$)** — Slow movers, heavy baggage. They barely squeeze through. 3. **Chloride guests ($Cl^-$)** — Medium movers, but they are *electrically negative* and are *attracted* back into the hall. Now, the turnstile operator measures the **net flow**: - The KTU examiner doesn't care about the *individual* speed of each guest type alone — that would be the **Nernst equation** (single-ion equilibrium). - The examiner wants the **net voltage** that, at steady state, exactly cancels the net ionic flow. That is what the **GHK equation** computes. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important in Biomedical Engineering?** > - The GHK equation is the theoretical backbone of **ECG, EEG, and EMG** signal analysis. > - It guides the design of **neural electrodes, deep brain stimulators, and pacemakers**. > - It is the starting point of the **Hodgkin–Huxley model** for action potential generation. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit | |--------|----------|-------|------| | $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | | $F$ | Faraday's constant | **96,485** | C/mol | | $T$ (body) | Body temperature | **310** | K | | $RT/F$ at 37 °C | Thermal voltage (ln) | **26.71** | mV | | $2.303 \, RT/F$ at 37 °C | Thermal voltage (log10) | **61.5** | mV | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize 61.5 mV** — it is the most used constant in KTU physiology numerical problems. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — How the GHK Equation Works The derivation rests on **three critical biophysical assumptions**: > [!NOTE] > **Assumption 1 — Constant Field Approximation (Hodgkin & Katz, 1949):** The electric field across the membrane is assumed to be **linear and constant** ($E = -V_m/a$), where $a$ is the membrane thickness. This collapses the second-order Nernst–Planck equation into a tractable first-order ODE. > [!NOTE] > **Assumption 2 — Independence Principle (Einstein):** Each ionic species moves **independently** of the others. There is no ion–ion interaction within the membrane. > [!NOTE] > **Assumption 3 — Steady-State Electroneutrality:** At equilibrium, the **net transmembrane current is zero**: > $$I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Cl} = 0$$ --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow 1. **Apply the Nernst–Planck equation** to each ion species: $$J_X = -D_X \frac{d[X]}{dx} - \frac{z_X F D_X}{RT} [X] \frac{dV}{dx}$$ where $J_X$ is the flux of ion *X*, $D_X$ is the diffusion coefficient, and $z_X$ is the valence. 2. **Impose the constant field condition** ($\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{V_m}{a}$), turning it into a linear ODE in [X]. 3. **Apply boundary conditions**: - At $x = 0$ (extracellular side): $V = 0$, $[X] = [X]_o$ - At $x = a$ (intracellular side): $V = V_m$, $[X] = [X]_i$ 4. **Solve for the flux** of each ion (the GHK flux equation): $$J_X = P_X \cdot \frac{z_X^2 F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[X]_o - [X]_i e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}$$ 5. **Convert flux to current** using $I_X = z_X F J_X$ for each ion. 6. **Impose steady-state condition** $I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Cl} = 0$. 7. **Solve algebraically for $V_m$** to obtain the GHK voltage equation. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **only** set of equations you must memorize for KTU 2024 exams on this topic. | # | Formula | Description | Standard Form | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left[ \dfrac{\sum P_{cation}[Cat]_o + \sum P_{anion}[An]_i}{\sum P_{cation}[Cat]_i + \sum P_{anion}[An]_o} \right]$ | Full GHK (cation/anion separation) | mV | | 2 | $V_m = 61.5 \, \log_{10}\left[ \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right]$ | GHK at 37 °C (log form) | mV | | 3 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ | **Nernst Equation** (single ion) | mV | | 4 | $E_{ion} = 61.5 \, \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ | Nernst at 37 °C, $z = 1$ | mV | | 5 | $E_{ion} = 26.71 \, \ln \dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ | Nernst at 37 °C, $z = 1$ (ln form) | mV | | 6 | $I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Cl} = 0$ | Steady-state condition | — | | 7 | $P_X = \dfrac{D_X}{a}$ | Permeability (Fick's law definition) | m/s | | 8 | $\tau = RC_m$ | Membrane time constant (related concept) | s | > [!WARNING] > **Valence sign pitfall:** In Equation 1, the **anion ($Cl^-$)** term appears with **swapped** o/i subscripts in the *numerator* vs *denominator*! This is because $z_{Cl} = -1$, which flips the sign in the Boltzmann factor. Forgetting this is the **#1 reason KTU students lose marks**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Neural Interface Design:** Predicting how a microelectrode will polarize neurons requires knowing $V_m$ for various ion channel blockers. - **Drug Discovery:** Pharmaceutical companies use GHK to model how **channel-specific drugs** (e.g., lidocaine blocking $Na^+$ channels) shift membrane potential. - **Biosensor Development:** Ion-Selective Electrodes (ISEs) used in blood gas analyzers rely on GHK-derived selectivity coefficients. - **Defibrillator and Pacemaker Design:** Threshold voltages for cardiac pacing are derived from extensions of GHK theory (Hodgkin–Huxley model). > [!NOTE] > The GHK equation is *the* connecting bridge between **biophysics** and **electronic circuit analysis** of excitable cells. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation of the GHK Equation > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Starting from the Nernst–Planck equation, derive the GHK voltage equation under the constant field assumption. ### Step 1: The Nernst–Planck Flux Equation (1-D form) The flux of ion $X$ (with charge $z_X F$) through a membrane of thickness $a$ is given by: $$J_X(x) = -D_X \frac{d[X]}{dx} - \frac{z_X F D_X}{RT} [X](x) \frac{dV}{dx}$$ The first term is **Fickian diffusion**; the second is **electromigration**. ### Step 2: Apply the Constant Field Approximation Set the electric field to be uniform across the membrane: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{V_m}{a} = \text{constant}$$ Define the dimensionless constant: $$\alpha_X = \frac{z_X F V_m}{RT}$$ The PDE collapses into a first-order linear ODE: $$\frac{d[X]}{dx} + \frac{\alpha_X}{a} [X] = -\frac{J_X}{D_X}$$ ### Step 3: General Solution of the Linear ODE Using the integrating factor $e^{\alpha_X x / a}$: $$[X](x) \cdot e^{\alpha_X x / a} = -\frac{J_X}{D_X} \cdot \frac{a}{\alpha_X} e^{\alpha_X x / a} + C_1$$ Therefore: $$[X](x) = -\frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X} + C_1 e^{-\alpha_X x / a}$$ ### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions **At $x = 0$ (extracellular):** $V = 0$ and $[X] = [X]_o$: $$[X]_o = -\frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X} + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = [X]_o + \frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X}$$ **At $x = a$ (intracellular):** $V = V_m$ and $[X] = [X]_i$: $$[X]_i = -\frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X} + \left( [X]_o + \frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X} \right) e^{-\alpha_X}$$ ### Step 5: Solve for the Flux $J_X$ Rearranging the previous equation: $$[X]_i - [X]_o \, e^{-\alpha_X} = \frac{J_X \cdot a}{D_X \alpha_X} \left( e^{-\alpha_X} - 1 \right)$$ Therefore: $$J_X = \frac{D_X \alpha_X}{a \left( 1 - e^{-\alpha_X} \right)} \cdot \left( [X]_o - [X]_i e^{\alpha_X} \right)$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{-\alpha_X}$: $$J_X = \frac{D_X \alpha_X}{a \left( e^{\alpha_X} - 1 \right)} \cdot \left( [X]_i - [X]_o e^{-\alpha_X} \right)$$ ### Step 6: Substitute $P_X = D_X / a$ $$J_X = P_X \cdot \frac{z_X F V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[X]_o - [X]_i e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}$$ This is the **GHK Flux Equation** for a single ionic species. ### Step 7: Convert to Current Density The current density carried by ion $X$ is: $$I_X = z_X F J_X = P_X \cdot \frac{z_X^2 F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[X]_o - [X]_i e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_X F V_m / RT}}$$ ### Step 8: Apply the Steady-State Condition At equilibrium, the algebraic sum of all ionic currents equals zero: $$I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Cl} = 0$$ Let $\phi = F V_m / R T$. Then for the three monovalent ions ($z = \pm 1$): $$P_{Na} \left( \frac{[Na^+]_o - [Na^+]_i e^{-\phi}}{1 - e^{-\phi}} \right) + P_K \left( \frac{[K^+]_o - [K^+]_i e^{-\phi}}{1 - e^{-\phi}} \right) - P_{Cl} \left( \frac{[Cl^-]_i - [Cl^-]_o e^{-\phi}}{1 - e^{-\phi}} \right) = 0$$ (The negative sign for $Cl^-$ comes from $z_{Cl} = -1$.) ### Step 9: Factor and Solve for $V_m$ Multiplying through by $(1 - e^{-\phi})$ and rearranging: $$e^{-\phi} \left( P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o \right) = P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ Therefore: $$e^{-F V_m / R T} = \frac{P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ Taking the natural logarithm and solving: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left[ \frac{P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right]$$ ### Step 10: Final Form (with log base 10 at 37 °C) Using $2.303 \, RT/F = 61.5 \text{ mV}$ at body temperature: $$\boxed{\,V_m = 61.5 \, \log_{10}\left[ \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right] \text{ mV}\,}$$ **The derivation is complete.** ✓ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A typical mammalian neuron at 37 °C has the following intracellular and extracellular ion concentrations and relative permeabilities: > > - $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM, $P_K = 1.0$ > - $[Na^+]_i = 10$ mM, $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $P_{Na} = 0.05$ > - $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM, $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$ > > **Calculate the resting membrane potential using the GHK equation.** ### Step 1: Substitute into the GHK Equation $$V_m = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\left[ \frac{(1.0)(5) + (0.05)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1.0)(140) + (0.05)(10) + (0.45)(120)} \right]$$ ### Step 2: Evaluate the Numerator $$\text{Numerator} = 5 + 7.25 + 1.8 = 14.05 \text{ (relative units)}$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the Denominator $$\text{Denominator} = 140 + 0.5 + 54 = 194.5 \text{ (relative units)}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Ratio $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{14.05}{194.5} = 0.07224$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Logarithm $$\log_{10}(0.07224) = \log_{10}(7.224 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(7.224) - 2 = 0.8587 - 2 = -1.1413$$ ### Step 6: Final Multiplication $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1413) = -70.19 \text{ mV}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** $V_m \approx -70 \text{ mV}$ — This matches the experimentally measured **resting membrane potential of a typical neuron** (which is usually between -60 mV and -90 mV). The negative sign indicates that the *inside* of the cell is **negative relative to the outside**, confirming the **polarization** of the membrane. --- ## 3.3 Comparison of Nernst Potentials vs. GHK Potential Using the same concentrations: | Ion | Nernst Equation $E_{ion} = 61.5 \log_{10}([X]_o / [X]_i)$ | Nernst Potential (mV) | |-----|----------------------------------------------------------|------------------------| | $K^+$ | $61.5 \log_{10}(5/140)$ | **-89.0 mV** | | $Na^+$ | $61.5 \log_{10}(145/10)$ | **+71.4 mV** | | $Cl^-$ | $61.5 \log_{10}(120/4)$ | **+90.8 mV** | | **GHK $V_m$** | (from above) | **-70.2 mV** | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The GHK potential lies between the Nernst potentials of the three ions, **weighted by their permeabilities**. The high $K^+$ permeability pulls $V_m$ close to $E_K$ (-89 mV), while the lower $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ permeabilities prevent it from reaching those values. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cell Membrane Schematic with Ion Channels (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR ext["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE
