## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the three divisions of the brain stem and state **one** specific function of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three divisions of the brain stem, from superior to inferior, are:

1. **Midbrain (Mesencephalon):** Contains the superior and inferior colliculi of the corpora quadrigemina. The superior colliculi mediate **visual reflexes** (e.g., coordinated head and eye movement toward visual stimuli), while the inferior colliculi mediate **auditory reflexes** (startle response to sudden sound). It also houses the **red nucleus** and the dopaminergic **substantia nigra**.

2. **Pons:** Serves as a bridge connecting the cerebrum and cerebellum via the **middle cerebellar peduncles** (transverse pontine fibres). It also contains the **pneumotaxic and apneustic centres** that modulate the rate and depth of respiration.

3. **Medulla Oblongata:** Houses the **cardiovascular centre** (regulates heart rate and blood pressure), the **medullary rhythmicity centre** (sets the basic rhythm of breathing), the **vasomotor centre** (controls blood vessel diameter), and the **vomiting and swallowing centres**. It is the site of **pyramidal decussation** — the crossing of the corticospinal motor tracts.

*Marking distribution (KTU key):* 1 mark for naming each division correctly, 1 mark for the function of the first two divisions (½ mark each), and 1 mark for the function of the third. *(3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a **spinal reflex**. Draw the labelled diagram of a reflex arc and name any **two** clinical tests used to assess spinal reflexes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A spinal reflex is a rapid, involuntary, stereotyped motor response to a specific stimulus, integrated entirely at the level of the spinal cord through a neural pathway called the **reflex arc**, without the requirement of conscious cortical processing.

**Reflex Arc Diagram (1½ marks):**

The five components in their correct sequence are:
**Receptor → Sensory (Afferent) Neuron → Integration Centre (Spinal Cord) → Motor (Efferent) Neuron → Effector (Muscle/Gland).**

**Two Clinical Tests (½ mark each):**

1. **Knee-jerk (Patellar) Reflex Test** — tapping the patellar tendon below the kneecap stretches the quadriceps muscle, stimulating muscle spindles; the resulting monosynaptic reflex causes the lower leg to jerk forward. Tests **L2–L4** spinal levels.

2. **Ankle-jerk (Achilles) Reflex Test** — tapping the Achilles tendon elicits contraction of the gastrocnemius muscle; tests **S1–S2** spinal levels.

*(Other acceptable answers: Biceps reflex C5–C6, Triceps reflex C7–C8, Plantar reflex L5–S2.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **internal structure of the spinal cord** with a neat, labelled cross-sectional diagram. Explain the **location and function** of the major ascending and descending tracts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **structure of a typical multipolar neuron**. Discuss the **mechanism of nerve impulse conduction** along a myelinated axon (saltatory conduction). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Internal Structure of the Spinal Cord (7 marks)**

The spinal cord on cross-section displays a characteristic **butterfly-shaped (H-shaped) inner core of grey matter** surrounded by **outer white matter**. The grey matter is organized into:

- **Dorsal (posterior) horns** — receive sensory afferent fibres entering through the dorsal roots.
- **Ventral (anterior) horns** — contain motor neuron cell bodies whose axons exit via the ventral roots.
- **Lateral horns** — present from T1 to L2 (sympathetic preganglionic neurons) and S2–S4 (parasympathetic preganglionic neurons).

The grey matter surrounds a tiny **central canal** filled with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), continuous with the 4th ventricle of the brain.

The white matter is divided by the dorsal and ventral horns into three paired funiculi (columns): **anterior (ventral)**, **lateral**, and **posterior (dorsal)** funiculi. Each funiculus contains ascending and descending tracts.

**Major Ascending (Sensory) Tracts:**

| Tract | Function |
|---|---|
| Dorsal column–medial lemniscus (Fasciculus gracilis + cuneatus) | Fine (discriminative) touch, vibration, conscious proprioception |
| Spinothalamic tract (anterior & lateral) | Pain, temperature, crude touch |
| Spinocerebellar tracts (anterior & posterior) | Unconscious proprioception to cerebellum |

**Major Descending (Motor) Tracts:**

| Tract | Function |
|---|---|
| Lateral & anterior corticospinal tracts | Voluntary motor control of limbs |
| Rubrospinal tract | Facilitates flexor muscle tone |
| Tectospinal tract | Reflex head turning in response to visual/auditory stimuli |
| Vestibulospinal tract | Postural reflexes, balance |
| Reticulospinal tracts | Modulates muscle tone, autonomic functions |

*Mark split (KTU typical):* 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for grey matter description, 1 mark for white matter description, 1 mark each for ascending & descending tract tables (≈ total 7 marks).

---

**(b) Structure of a Multipolar Neuron & Saltatory Conduction (7 marks)**

**Structure of a typical multipolar neuron** *(Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.3)*:

A multipolar neuron has multiple dendritic processes and a single axon. The major structural components are:

1. **Dendrites** — branched cytoplasmic projections from the soma that serve as the principal **input zone** for synaptic contacts from other neurons. They increase the surface area available for receiving signals.

2. **Cell body (soma / perikaryon)** — contains the nucleus with a prominent nucleolus, **Nissl bodies** (rough endoplasmic reticulum with ribosomes for protein synthesis — characteristic of neurons), numerous mitochondria, neurofibrils (microtubules and neurofilaments for cytoskeletal support and axonal transport), and Golgi apparatus.

3. **Axon hillock** — the cone-shaped region of the soma where the axon originates. It is the **trigger zone** of the neuron — the site where action potentials are initiated because it has the highest density of voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels and the lowest threshold for depolarization.

4. **Axon** — a long (up to 1 m in humans) cylindrical process that conducts action potentials away from the soma. It contains axoplasm, neurofibrils, and microtubules.

5. **Myelin sheath** — segments of concentric lipid-rich membrane wrapped around the axon by **Schwann cells** (in the peripheral nervous system) or **oligodendrocytes** (in the central nervous system). Each Schwann cell forms one internodal segment; gaps between segments are the **Nodes of Ranvier**.

6. **Axon terminals (synaptic boutons / telodendria)** — bulbous endings that form synapses with the next cell. They contain **synaptic vesicles** loaded with **neurotransmitter** (e.g., ACh, glutamate, GABA) and numerous mitochondria for ATP-dependent vesicle cycling.

*Mark split:* 1 mark for diagram + 3 marks for labelling (4 marks total for structure).

**Mechanism of Saltatory Conduction (3 marks):**

Because the myelin sheath acts as an **electrical insulator**, the action potential cannot regenerate beneath the myelinated regions. Instead, the depolarizing current flows passively (electrotonically) through the axoplasm from one Node of Ranvier to the next.

1. At an active node, voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels open → Na$^+$ influx → depolarization → action potential is regenerated.
2. The local circuit current flows longitudinally through the axoplasm to the next node (passive spread, very fast).
3. At the next node, the membrane is depolarized to threshold → voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels there open → **action potential "jumps" to the next node**.
4. The previous node enters its refractory period.
5. This process repeats, giving the appearance of the action potential **leaping (Latin: *saltare* = to leap)** from node to node.

**Advantages over continuous conduction (unmyelinated axons):**
- **Speed:** Conduction velocity increases dramatically (up to ~120 m/s in large myelinated axons vs ~2 m/s in unmyelinated).
- **Energy efficiency:** Ionic exchange (Na$^+$/K$^+$ pump activity) occurs only at nodes — far less ATP consumption.
- **Compactness:** Faster conduction is achieved with a smaller axon diameter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **structure and functions of the medulla oblongata**. Explain why damage to the medulla is almost always fatal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify neurons based on **structure and function**. With diagrams, explain **unipolar, bipolar, multipolar, and pseudounipolar neurons** giving one example location for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Medulla Oblongata — Structure, Functions, and Clinical Significance (7 marks)**

**Location & Gross Structure (2 marks):**
The medulla oblongata is the most inferior portion of the brain stem, approximately **3 cm long**, extending from the lower border of the pons superiorly to the **foramen magnum** inferiorly, where it is continuous with the spinal cord. Its anterior surface bears two **pyramids** flanking the **anterior median fissure** (housing the corticospinal tracts), and at its caudal end these fibres undergo the **pyramidal decussation** (motor fibres cross to the opposite side). The posterior surface shows the **gracile** and **cuneate** tubercles (relay nuclei for the dorsal column sensory pathways).

**Internal Structure (1 mark):**
The central canal of the spinal cord expands into the **fourth ventricle** at the level of the medulla. The interior contains extensive **reticular formation**, cranial nerve nuclei, and the vital reflex centres listed below.

**Vital Functions (3 marks):**

| Centre | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cardiovascular centre** | Regulates heart rate (cardioaccelerator + cardioinhibitor zones) and arterial blood pressure |
| **Medullary rhythmicity centre** (ventral & dorsal respiratory groups) | Generates the basic rhythm of breathing (inspiration–expiration cycle) |
| **Vasomotor centre** | Controls diameter of blood vessels → regulates BP via sympathetic tone |
| **Vomiting (emetic) centre** | Coordinates vomiting reflex in response to toxins, GI irritation, increased intracranial pressure |
| **Swallowing (deglutition) centre** | Coordinates the complex sequence of muscles involved in swallowing |
| **Coughing, sneezing, hiccupping centres** | Protective respiratory reflexes |
| **Salivation & lacrimation centres** | Autonomic control of glandular secretions |

**Cranial Nerve Nuclei Located in the Medulla (1 mark):**
CN IX (glossopharyngeal), CN X (vagus — parasympathetic to thoracic & abdominal viscera), CN XI (spinal accessory — motor to sternocleidomastoid & trapezius), and CN XII (hypoglossal — motor to tongue muscles).

**Why Medullary Damage is Usually Fatal (Bonus / Boxed note):**
The medulla houses all the **autonomic reflex centres essential for life** — heart rate, respiration, and vasomotor tone. Direct injury (e.g., high cervical fracture dislocating the odontoid process, brain-stem stroke, or compression by a cerebellar tonsillar herniation) disrupts these centres. **Cessation of breathing and cardiac arrest occur within minutes** unless artificial ventilation and cardiovascular support are immediately instituted. This is the anatomical basis of the clinical dictum: *"No medulla, no life."*

---

**(b) Classification of Neurons (7 marks)**

*(Refer to the diagrams in SECTION 4.4.)*

**A. Structural (Morphological) Classification (4 marks):**

| Type | Diagram Features | Example Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Unipolar** | Single process emerging from the cell body that branches into peripheral and central arms | Found mainly in invertebrates; rare in adult mammals (embryonic stages only) |
| **Pseudounipolar** | A single process that bifurcates into a peripheral branch (going to a receptor) and a central branch (entering the spinal cord); cell body lies off the main axon path in the dorsal root ganglion | **Dorsal root ganglion sensory neurons** (touch, pain, proprioception) |
| **Bipolar** | Two distinct processes — one dendrite and one axon — extending from opposite poles of an oval cell body | **Retinal bipolar cells**, **olfactory receptor neurons**, **vestibular and cochlear ganglia** of CN VIII |
| **Multipolar** | One axon and multiple dendrites projecting from a star-shaped or pyramidal cell body | **Motor neurons of the ventral horn**, **Purkinje cells of cerebellum**, **pyramidal cells of cerebral cortex** |
| **Anaxonic** | No clearly distinguishable axon; small neurons | **Amacrine cells of retina**, some interneurons |

**B. Functional Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Sensory (Afferent) Neurons:** Transmit impulses from peripheral receptors (skin, muscle, viscera) **towards** the CNS. Mostly pseudounipolar. Form the dorsal roots.

2. **Motor (Efferent) Neurons:** Transmit impulses **away from** the CNS to effectors (skeletal muscles, smooth muscle, glands). Mostly multipolar. Cell bodies in ventral horn or autonomic ganglia.

3. **Interneurons (Association / Connector Neurons):** Lie entirely within the CNS; connect sensory and motor neurons in neural pathways. By far the most numerous type. Mostly multipolar or anaxonic. Responsible for higher functions like memory, learning, and integration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Conflating "reflex" with "reaction":** A reflex is **involuntary and stereotyped**, while a reaction involves conscious cortical processing. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you describe a withdrawal reflex as "the person consciously pulling away their hand."
>
> 2. **Reversing sensory and motor roots:** Students frequently mix up dorsal (sensory — afferent) and ventral (motor — efferent) roots. Memorize: **"Dorsal = aDoor = sensory input"** and **"Ventral = eVentricle / eXit = motor output."**
>
> 3. **Calling the brain stem "just a connector":** The brain stem is **not** a passive cable — it contains the **vital centres**, **cranial nerve nuclei**, and the **reticular activating system** (RAS). Listing only its "connector" function without these roles will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing pseudounipolar with bipolar:** In pseudounipolar neurons, the cell body sits *off* the axon on a single short stem; in bipolar, the cell body has **two truly separate processes**. Diagrams must clearly distinguish these.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the axon hillock** in neuron diagrams — it is the **trigger zone** and examiners specifically look for it.
>
> 6. **Mixing up cranial nerve numbers:** CN I and II are **not** brain stem nerves — they attach directly to the cerebrum (olfactory bulb and diencephalon respectively). Only CN III–XII are brain stem nerves. This single error costs full marks in some questions.
>
> 7. **Saying "myelin is made of fat":** Be precise — "myelin is a **lipoprotein** membrane wrapped concentrically around the axon by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS)."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Brain stem = midbrain + pons + medulla** (superior → inferior). It contains cranial nerve nuclei **III to XII**.
- [ ] **Midbrain** has corpora quadrigemina (superior colliculi = visual reflex, inferior colliculi = auditory reflex), red nucleus, substantia nigra.
- [ ] **Pons** is the "bridge" — transverse fibres connect the two cerebellar hemispheres; contains pneumotaxic and apneustic respiratory centres.
- [ ] **Medulla** houses the **vital centres** (cardiovascular, respiratory rhythmicity, vasomotor). Damage → death. Contains pyramidal decussation.
- [ ] **Spinal cord** is **42–45 cm** long, ends at **L1–L2** (conus medullaris) in adults. Contains **31 pairs** of spinal nerves.
- [ ] Internal anatomy: **butterfly-shaped grey matter** (dorsal horn = sensory, ventral horn = motor, lateral horn = autonomic T1–L2 & S2–S4) inside; **white matter** (funiculi with ascending & descending tracts) outside.
- [ ] **Spinal reflex arc** = receptor → afferent neuron → integration centre (spinal cord) → efferent neuron → effector.
- [ ] **Stretch reflex (knee-jerk) is monosynaptic**; **withdrawal reflex is polysynaptic**.
- [ ] **Withdrawal reflex** uses reciprocal inhibition (flexor contracts, extensor relaxes) + crossed extensor reflex (opposite limb extends to support body).
- [ ] **Neuron** has 4 functional zones: input (dendrites), integration (soma), trigger (axon hillock), conduction (axon), output (synaptic boutons).
- [ ] **Nissl bodies** = rough ER; characteristic of neurons. **Axon hillock** = lowest threshold = AP initiation site.
- [ ] **Myelin** in **PNS = Schwann cells**; in **CNS = oligodendrocytes**. Nodes of Ranvier enable saltatory conduction.
- [ ] **Structural types of neurons:** unipolar, pseudounipolar (DRG sensory), bipolar (retina, olfactory, vestibulocochlear), multipolar (motor, Purkinje, pyramidal), anaxonic.
- [ ] **Functional types:** sensory (afferent), motor (efferent), interneurons.
- [ ] **Saltatory conduction** = action potential jumps node-to-node. Speed ∝ axon diameter (Hursh: v ≈ 6·d m/s for myelinated mammalian fibres).
- [ ] **Resting potential ≈ −70 mV**; **threshold ≈ −55 mV**; **Na$^+$ equilibrium +60 mV**; **K$^+$ equilibrium −90 mV**.
- [ ] **Absolute refractory period** ensures unidirectional propagation; limits max firing frequency to ~2500 Hz.
- [ ] **Nernst equation** and **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz equation** are examinable formulae — know their forms and the meaning of every variable.
- [ ] **Demyelinating diseases:** Multiple sclerosis (CNS), Guillain-Barré syndrome (PNS) — block saltatory conduction → severe motor/sensory deficits.
- [ ] **Neuromuscular junction** uses **acetylcholine** acting on **nicotinic** receptors — site of action for many neuromuscular-blocking drugs and myasthenia gravis autoantibodies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms of Nerve Impulses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mechanisms of Nerve Impulses ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nerve Impulse (Action Potential):** A rapid, transient, self-propagating electrical and chemical signal that travels along the plasma membrane of a neuron, characterized by a brief reversal of the resting membrane potential from approximately **−70 mV** to about **+30 mV**, followed by a return to the resting state. It is the fundamental functional unit of information transfer in the nervous system. **Mechanism of Nerve Impulse** refers to the entire sequence of biophysical and biochemical events — involving ion channel gating, ionic flux, and membrane potential changes — that convert a stimulus into an electrical signal and propagate it along a nerve fiber. The mechanisms include: - **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) Maintenance** – via the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump - **Depolarization** – opening of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels - **Repolarization** – opening of voltage-gated K⁺ channels - **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** – delayed closure of K⁺ channels - **Propagation** – local circuit currents in unmyelinated (continuous) and myelinated (saltatory) fibers - **Synaptic Transmission** – chemical signaling between neurons --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Water Tower and Pipe" Model of a Neuron** Think of a neuron as a long water pipe filled with charged "water" (ions). In its resting state, the inside of the pipe has more **negative ions (Cl⁻, proteins)** and less **positive sodium (Na⁺)**, while the outside has more **positive sodium (Na⁺)** and **potassium (K⁺)**. The "pressure difference" between inside and outside is the **Resting Membrane Potential (−70 mV)** — a stored energy reservoir. When a stimulus (like tapping a nerve) acts like **opening a pressure-release valve** (Na⁺ channels snap open), positive Na⁺ ions rush inside, flipping the polarity to **+30 mV** — this is the **action potential (nerve impulse)**. Just as quickly, a second valve (K⁺ channel) opens to push positive charge back out, restoring the resting pressure. This wave of valve-opening cascades down the pipe — that cascade is the **propagation of the nerve impulse**. In myelinated fibers, the "valves" only exist at gaps called **Nodes of Ranvier**, so the signal **jumps** from node to node (like a kangaroo) — this is **saltatory conduction**, making it 50–100× faster. --- ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard Biophysical Constants for Nerve Impulse Calculations:** > - Resting Membrane Potential (Vₘ) = **−70 mV** (typical neuron) > - Peak Action Potential = **+30 mV to +40 mV** > - Intracellular [K⁺] = **140 mM**, Extracellular [K⁺] = **5 mM** > - Intracellular [Na⁺] = **10–15 mM**, Extracellular [Na⁺] = **145 mM** > - Threshold potential = **−55 mV** > - Body temperature = **37 °C = 310 K** > - Universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - Faraday's constant **F = 96,485 C/mol** > - Conduction velocity: **0.5 to 120 m/s** (depending on fiber type) --- ### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping | KTU CO | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | What This Topic Covers | |---|---|---| | **CO2** | Understand / Apply | Explain the biophysical basis of nerve signal generation and propagation | | **CO3** | Analyze | Relate ion channel behavior to membrane potential changes | | **CO5** | Apply | Correlate nerve mechanisms with biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG, EMG) | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) — Foundation Stage The neuron at rest maintains a **potential difference** across its plasma membrane, with the **inside negative** relative to the **outside positive**. This is established by: 1. **Differential ion distribution** maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in — electrogenic pump contributing **−4 mV to −10 mV**). 2. **Higher membrane permeability to K⁺** at rest (due to leak K⁺ channels) — K⁺ diffuses out down its concentration gradient, leaving the inside negative. 3. **Large anionic proteins** trapped inside the cell that cannot cross the membrane. --- ### 2.2 Nernst Equation (Equilibrium Potential for a Single Ion) The **Nernst Equation** calculates the membrane potential at which there is no net flow of a specific ion across the membrane. $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ Where: - **E_ion** = Equilibrium (Nernst) potential for the ion (in Volts) - **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **T** = Absolute temperature in Kelvin - **z** = Valence (charge) of the ion - **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - **[ion]_out**, **[ion]_in** = Extracellular and intracellular concentrations **Common Equilibrium Potentials in Neurons (at 37 °C):** - **E_K = −90 mV** (the resting potential approaches this) - **E_Na = +60 mV** (peak of action potential approaches this) - **E_Cl = −70 mV** --- ### 2.3 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation Because the membrane is permeable to multiple ions at rest, the actual resting potential is given by the **Goldman Equation**: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$ Where **P** is the **permeability coefficient** of the membrane for each ion. > [!TIP] > **KTU Key Insight:** At rest, P_K >> P_Na >> P_Cl, so Vₘ ≈ **E_K = −70 to −90 mV**. During an action potential, P_Na becomes briefly dominant, shifting Vₘ toward **E_Na = +60 mV**. --- ### 2.4 The Action Potential — Phases The action potential is generated when a stimulus depolarizes the membrane to the **threshold potential (−55 mV)**. This triggers a stereotyped sequence of phases: | Phase | Voltage Change | Ion Channel Activity | Direction of Ion Flow | |---|---|---|---| | **Resting State** | −70 mV (stable) | Leak K⁺ channels open; Na⁺/K⁺ pump active | K⁺ leaks out | | **Depolarization** | −70 mV → +30 mV (rising phase) | Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open** rapidly | Na⁺ rushes **INTO** cell | | **Repolarization** | +30 mV → −70 mV (falling phase) | Na⁺ channels **inactivate**; voltage-gated **K⁺ channels open** | K⁺ flows **OUT** of cell | | **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** | Below −70 mV (≈ −80 mV) | K⁺ channels close slowly; membrane briefly more negative | Excess K⁺ efflux | | **Return to Resting** | Returns to −70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores ionic gradients | 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in | --- ### 2.5 Threshold & All-or-None Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Threshold Potential = −55 mV**: The critical voltage at which the regenerative Na⁺ channel opening becomes self-sustaining. - **Subthreshold stimulus** → Local graded potential; does NOT propagate - **Threshold/suprathreshold stimulus** → Full action potential fires - **All-or-None Law:** Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at **full amplitude** regardless of stimulus strength. Stronger stimuli increase **frequency** of firing, not amplitude. --- ### 2.6 Refractory Periods | Period | Duration | Cause | Functional Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Absolute Refractory Period** | ~1–2 ms | Na⁺ channels are inactivated; no stimulus can trigger another AP | Limits maximum firing frequency; ensures unidirectional propagation | | **Relative Refractory Period** | ~3–4 ms | K⁺ channels still open; membrane is hyperpolarized | A stronger-than-normal stimulus can trigger AP | --- ### 2.7 Propagation of Nerve Impulses **Two modes of propagation exist:** **(A) Continuous Conduction (Unmyelinated Fibers)** - Local circuit currents spread **step-by-step** along the entire membrane - Slow conduction velocity (**0.5 to 2 m/s**) - Found in: Pain fibers (C-fibers), autonomic postganglionic fibers **(B) Saltatory Conduction (Myelinated Fibers)** - Action potential "jumps" from one **Node of Ranvier** to the next - Myelin sheath (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) acts as an **electrical insulator** - Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are **clustered at Nodes of Ranvier** - Fast conduction velocity (**up to 120 m/s**) - Found in: Motor neurons, sensory fibers (A-alpha, A-beta, A-delta) > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Saltatory conduction is faster AND more energy-efficient because Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase only needs to restore ion gradients at the nodes, not along the entire axon. --- ### 2.8 Conduction Velocity Calculation For a myelinated fiber, conduction velocity depends on axon diameter and internodal distance: $$v \approx k \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\ln\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)}$$ Where: - **v** = Conduction velocity - **d** = Axon inner diameter - **D** = Outer diameter (with myelin) - **k** = Constant (depends on conditions) Empirically, for mammalian myelinated fibers: $$v \approx 6 \times d \quad (\text{m/s, with } d \text{ in μm})$$ --- ### 2.9 Synaptic Transmission **Synapse** = Junction between two neurons (or neuron and effector cell) | Type | Mechanism | Speed | Location | |---|---|---|---| | **Electrical Synapse** | Direct ionic current flow through **gap junctions** | Very fast (no delay) | Cardiac muscle, some CNS neurons, retina | | **Chemical Synapse** | Neurotransmitter release from presynaptic terminal | Slower (~0.5 ms synaptic delay) | Most neurons in CNS/PNS | **Chemical Synaptic Transmission Steps:** 1. Action potential arrives at presynaptic terminal 2. Voltage-gated **Ca²⁺ channels** open; Ca²⁺ influx 3. Synaptic vesicles fuse with presynaptic membrane (**exocytosis**) 4. **Neurotransmitter** (e.g., ACh, glutamate, GABA, dopamine) released into synaptic cleft (~20–40 nm wide) 5. Neurotransmitter binds to **receptors** on postsynaptic membrane 6. Opens ion channels → **EPSP** (excitatory) or **IPSP** (inhibitory) 7. Neurotransmitter degraded or reuptaken --- ### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Nernst equilibrium potential | Volts (V) | | 2 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{0.0592}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Nernst at 37 °C (log form) | Volts (V) | | 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation | Volts (V) | | 4 | $C_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Membrane capacitance | Farads (F) | | 5 | $I = g \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$ | Ionic current (Ohm's law for ion channels) | Amperes (A) | | 6 | $v \approx 6 \times d$ | Conduction velocity vs axon diameter (mammalian myelinated) | m/s (d in μm) | | 7 | $\tau = R_m \cdot C_m$ | Membrane time constant | Seconds (s) | | 8 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Length constant (signal spread) | Meters (m) | --- ### 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters for Biomedical Engineers:** > - **ECG/EKG Machines:** Measure summed action potentials of cardiac myocytes (similar Na⁺/K⁺ mechanism) > - **EEG Systems:** Detect synchronized postsynaptic potentials of cortical neurons > - **EMG & Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS):** Clinically measure conduction velocity to diagnose demyelinating diseases (e.g., Multiple Sclerosis, Guillain-Barré) > - **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Require understanding of action potential encoding (frequency coding, population coding) > - **Local Anesthetics (Lidocaine):** Block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels → prevent nerve impulse > - **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS):** Modulates aberrant firing patterns in Parkinson's disease ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation (From First Principles) **Step 1 — Start with electrochemical equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the electrical force pulling the ion one way exactly balances the chemical (concentration) force pushing it the other way. The work done in moving **1 mole** of ions from inside to outside against the concentration gradient is: $$W_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ **Step 2 — Work done against the electrical gradient** in moving 1 mole of a z-valent ion across a potential difference ΔV (= V_in − V_out) is: $$W_{elec} = zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = -zF E_{ion}$$ (The sign is negative because the membrane potential E_ion is conventionally defined as outside-minus-inside.) **Step 3 — At equilibrium**, total work = 0: $$W_{chem} + W_{elec} = 0$$ $$RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} - zF E_{ion} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for E_ion:** $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ > **This is the Nernst Equation.** It gives the equilibrium potential for a single permeant ion. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation at Body Temperature (37 °C) **Step 1 — Substitute T = 310 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol:** $$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 — Convert natural log to log base 10** using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$: $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \times 26.71}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} \text{ (mV)}$$ **Step 3 — Most textbooks use the rounded form:** $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{0.0592}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} \quad \text{(Volts, at 37 °C)}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Calculating E_K for a Typical Neuron **Given:** [K⁺]_in = 140 mM, [K⁺]_out = 5 mM, z = +1, T = 310 K **Step 1 — Apply Nernst Equation:** $$E_K = \frac{0.0592}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{140}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:** $$\frac{5}{140} = 0.0357$$ **Step 3 — Take the log:** $$\log_{10}(0.0357) = -1.447$$ **Step 4 — Multiply:** $$E_K = 0.0592 \times (-1.447) = -0.0857 \text{ V} \approx -85.7 \text{ mV}$$ **Result: E_K ≈ −86 mV**, which closely matches the resting membrane potential of a neuron (since K⁺ permeability dominates at rest). --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Calculating E_Na for a Typical Neuron **Given:** [Na⁺]_in = 15 mM, [Na⁺]_out = 145 mM, z = +1 **Step 1 — Apply Nernst Equation:** $$E_{Na} = \frac{0.0592}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{145}{15}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:** $$\frac{145}{15} = 9.667$$ **Step 3 — Take the log:** $$\log_{10}(9.667) = 0.985$$ **Step 4 — Multiply:** $$E_{Na} = 0.0592 \times 0.985 = +0.0583 \text{ V} \approx +58.3 \text{ mV}$$ **Result: E_Na ≈ +58 mV to +60 mV**, which is the theoretical peak of the action potential. --- ### 3.5 Worked Example: Verifying the Resting Membrane Potential via Goldman Equation **Given:** - [K⁺]_in = 140, [K⁺]_out = 5 - [Na⁺]_in = 15, [Na⁺]_out = 145 - [Cl⁻]_in = 9, [Cl⁻]_out = 125 - P_K = 1.0, P_Na = 0.04, P_Cl = 0.45 (typical at rest) **Step 1 — Numerator (extracellular cations + intracellular anions):** $$P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ $$= (1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(9)$$ $$= 5 + 5.8 + 4.05 = 14.85$$ **Step 2 — Denominator (intracellular cations + extracellular anions):** $$P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o$$ $$= (1.0)(140) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(125)$$ $$= 140 + 0.6 + 56.25 = 196.85$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Goldman equation (at 37 °C, using the form with 61.5 mV for monovalent ions):** $$V_m = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \frac{14.85}{196.85} \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4 — Compute ratio and log:** $$\frac{14.85}{196.85} = 0.0754$$ $$\log_{10}(0.0754) = -1.123$$ **Step 5 — Final calculation:** $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.123) = -69.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Result: V_m ≈ −69 mV**, which closely matches the experimentally observed **−70 mV** resting potential. ✓ --- ### 3.6 Worked Example: Estimating Conduction Velocity **Given:** A myelinated motor neuron axon with inner diameter **d = 12 μm**. **Apply the empirical relation:** $$v \approx 6 \times d = 6 \times 12 = 72 \text{ m/s}$$ **Result:** Conduction velocity ≈ **72 m/s** (typical for A-alpha motor fibers, range 70–120 m/s). --- ### 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Action Potential vs Graded Potential | Feature | Action Potential | Graded Potential | |---|---|---| | **Amplitude** | All-or-none, fixed (~100 mV) | Variable (proportional to stimulus) | | **Threshold** | Yes (−55 mV) | No threshold | | **Propagation** | Active, self-regenerating | Passive, decremental (decays with distance) | | **Refractory period** | Yes (absolute + relative) | No | | **Location** | Axon hillock & axon | Dendrites and soma | | **Function** | Long-distance signaling | Local integration of inputs | | **Direction** | Unidirectional (orthodromic) | Bidirectional spread | --- ### 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Types of Nerve Fibers (Erlanger-Gasser Classification) | Fiber Type | Myelinated? | Diameter (μm) | Conduction Velocity (m/s) | Function | |---|---|---|---|---| | **A-alpha (Aα)** | Yes | 12–20 | 70–120 | Motor, proprioception | | **A-beta (Aβ)** | Yes | 5–12 | 30–70 | Touch, pressure | | **A-gamma (Aγ)** | Yes | 3–6 | 15–30 | Muscle spindle (intrafusal) | | **A-delta (Aδ)** | Thin myelin | 1–5 | 5–30 | Fast pain, temperature | | **B** | Yes | <3 | 3–15 | Autonomic preganglionic | | **C** | No (unmyelinated) | 0.5–1.5 | 0.5–2 | Slow pain, postganglionic autonomic | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Action Potential Phases & Ion Channel States ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RESTING STATE
Vm = -70 mV
K+ leak channels open
Na+/K+ pump active"] B["STIMULUS APPLIED
Local depolarization begins
graded potential forms"] C["THRESHOLD REACHED
Vm = -55 mV
Voltage-gated Na+ channels open"] D["DEPOLARIZATION
Vm rises to +30 mV
Na+ rushes INTO cell"] E["PEAK OF AP
Vm = +30 mV
Na+ channels INACTIVATE
K+ channels open"] F["REPOLARIZATION
Vm falls toward -70 mV
K+ flows OUT of cell"] G["HYPERPOLARIZATION
Vm = -80 mV
K+ channels close slowly"] H["RETURN TO RESTING
Na+/K+ pump restores gradients
Vm = -70 mV"] I["ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY
No new AP possible
Na+ channels inactivated"] J["RELATIVE REFRACTORY
Stronger stimulus needed
K+ channels still open"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H H -.->|new stimulus| B E --> I I --> J J --> H ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Saltatory Conduction in Myelinated Axon ```mermaid flowchart LR N1["NODE 1
Vm = +30 mV
AP firing here
Na+ in"] N2["NODE 2
Vm = -55 mV
reaching threshold
next to fire"] N3["NODE 3
Vm = -70 mV
resting
waits for signal"] M1["MYELIN
insulating sheath
Schwann cell"] M2["MYELIN
insulating sheath
Schwann cell"] INT["INTERNODAL REGION
passive current flow
no ion channels"] A["ACTION POTENTIAL
propagation direction"] I["LOCAL CIRCUIT
currents jump
node to node"] N1 -->|local current| INT INT -->|depolarizes| N2 N2 -->|local current| M2 M2 -.->|skips internode| N3 A --> N1 I --> N1 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Synaptic Transmission (Chemical Synapse) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["ACTION POTENTIAL
arrives at
presynaptic terminal"] A2["VOLTAGE-GATED
Ca2+ CHANNELS OPEN
Ca2+ influx"] A3["SYNAPTIC VESICLES
fuse with membrane
exocytosis"] A4["NEUROTRANSMITTER
released into
synaptic cleft 20-40 nm"] A5["BINDING TO
postsynaptic receptors
ligand-gated channels open"] A6["EPSP or IPSP
generated
graded potential"] A7["DEGRADATION or
REUPTAKE of
neurotransmitter"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7 A7 -.->|new AP possible| A1 ``` --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Nerve Impulse Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["STIMULUS INPUT"] S1["Sensory stimulus
mechanical, chemical, thermal"] end subgraph TRANSDUCTION["TRANSDUCTION"] T1["Receptor / Generator potential
graded depolarization"] end subgraph INTEGRATION["INTEGRATION at AXON HILLOCK"] I1["Summation of EPSPs and IPSPs
decision point"] end subgraph INITIATION["INITIATION"] X1["THRESHOLD -55 mV
all-or-none firing"] end subgraph PROPAGATION["PROPAGATION"] P1["Action potential travels
along axon"] P2["Myelinated: saltatory
Unmyelinated: continuous"] end subgraph TRANSMISSION["SYNAPTIC TRANSMISSION"] Y1["Neurotransmitter release
chemical synapse"] end subgraph OUTPUT["RESPONSE"] O1["Postsynaptic potential
muscle contraction / gland secretion / next neuron firing"] end S1 --> T1 --> I1 --> X1 --> P1 --> P2 --> Y1 --> O1 ``` --- ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Ion Channel States During Action Potential ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Resting: Membrane at -70 mV Resting: RESTING
K+ leak open
Na+ channels closed
Vm = -70 mV Resting --> Depolarization: Stimulus reaches threshold Depolarization: DEPOLARIZATION
Na+ channels OPEN
Na+ flows IN
Vm rises to +30 mV Depolarization --> Peak: Na+ channels INACTIVATE
K+ channels OPEN Peak: PEAK
Vm = +30 mV
K+ starts flowing OUT Peak --> Repolarization: K+ efflux dominates Repolarization: REPOLARIZATION
K+ channels open
Vm falls toward -70 mV Repolarization --> Hyperpolarization: K+ channels slow to close Hyperpolarization: HYPERPOLARIZATION
Vm = -80 mV
undershoot phase Hyperpolarization --> RefractoryAbsolute: Na+ channels RECOVER from inactivation RefractoryAbsolute: ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY
No AP possible
Na+ channels recovering RefractoryAbsolute --> RefractoryRelative: Partial recovery RefractoryRelative: RELATIVE REFRACTORY
Strong stimulus can fire AP
K+ channels closing RefractoryRelative --> Resting: Na+/K+ pump restores gradients Resting --> [*] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** Define **Resting Membrane Potential**. State its typical value in a neuron and list the two main ions responsible for maintaining it. **Model Answer:** > **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** The stable, negative electrical potential difference (typically **−70 mV**) existing across the plasma membrane of a neuron at rest, with the inside of the cell negative relative to the outside. > > It is primarily maintained by: > 1. **Potassium (K⁺)** — Higher intracellular concentration; K⁺ leaks out through leak channels, leaving the inside negative. > 2. **Sodium (Na⁺)** — Higher extracellular concentration; the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump actively expels 3 Na⁺ ions for every 2 K⁺ ions imported, contributing to the net negative interior. > > The RMP is governed by the **Nernst equation** for K⁺ and the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** for the overall membrane potential. *(Self-assessment: 1 mark definition + 1 mark value + 1 mark ions explanation)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** What is the **all-or-none principle** of an action potential? What happens if a stimulus is subthreshold? **Model Answer:** > **All-or-None Principle:** Once the membrane potential reaches the **threshold value (−55 mV)**, an action potential of fixed amplitude (~100 mV, peak at +30 mV) is triggered. The action potential does NOT vary in size with stimulus strength — it either fires **completely (all)** or **not at all (none)**. > > If the stimulus is **subthreshold** (does not depolarize the membrane to −55 mV): > - A **local graded potential** is produced > - It **decays passively** with distance > - It **does not propagate** along the axon > - No action potential is generated > > Stronger stimuli increase the **frequency** of action potentials (rate coding), not their amplitude. *(Self-assessment: 2 marks principle + 1 mark subthreshold explanation)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Set A]** **(a) Derive the Nernst Equation and calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ ion at 37 °C, given [K⁺]_in = 140 mM and [K⁺]_out = 5 mM. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the different phases of an action potential with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels. (7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** The Nernst equation gives the membrane potential at which there is **no net ionic flux** for a given ion. It balances the chemical (concentration) gradient against the electrical gradient. **Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):** At electrochemical equilibrium, the work done in moving 1 mole of ion against the concentration gradient equals the work done against the electrical gradient: $$W_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ $$W_{elec} = zF(E_{ion})$$ Setting total work to zero (equilibrium condition): $$RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} - zF E_{ion} = 0$$ Solving for E_ion: $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ **Step 3 — Calculation at 37 °C (T = 310 K) (2 marks):** For K⁺, z = +1, [K⁺]_in = 140 mM, [K⁺]_out = 5 mM: $$E_K = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96485)} \ln \frac{5}{140}$$ $$E_K = 0.0267 \times \ln(0.0357)$$ $$E_K = 0.0267 \times (-3.332)$$ $$E_K = -0.0889 \text{ V} = -88.9 \text{ mV}$$ **Equilibrium potential for K⁺ ≈ −89 mV** (often quoted as −90 mV). --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Action Potential Phases (4 marks):** | Phase | V_m change | Channels | Ion movement | |---|---|---|---| | **Resting** | −70 mV | K⁺ leak open | K⁺ efflux | | **Depolarization** | −70 → +30 mV | Na⁺ channels **open** | Na⁺ influx | | **Peak** | +30 mV | Na⁺ **inactivate**; K⁺ open | K⁺ efflux begins | | **Repolarization** | +30 → −70 mV | K⁺ channels open | K⁺ efflux | | **Hyperpolarization** | Below −70 mV | K⁺ channels slow to close | Excess K⁺ efflux | **Step 2 — Role of Voltage-Gated Na⁺ Channels (1.5 marks):** - **Closed at rest**; **open rapidly** in response to depolarization reaching threshold - Responsible for the rapid **rising phase (depolarization)** - **Inactivate quickly** at the peak → stop Na⁺ influx - Have three states: **closed (resting), open, inactivated** **Step 3 — Role of Voltage-Gated K⁺ Channels (1.5 marks):** - **Closed at rest**; open **slower** than Na⁺ channels - Cause **repolarization** and **hyperpolarization** via K⁺ efflux - Do NOT inactivate rapidly; close slowly → cause undershoot phase - They have only two states: **closed and open** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Set B]** **(a) Explain the propagation of a nerve impulse in myelinated and unmyelinated nerve fibers. Compare their conduction velocities and the underlying mechanisms. (7 marks)** **(b) Describe the mechanism of chemical synaptic transmission with a suitable diagram. List FOUR common neurotransmitters. (7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Step 1 — Unmyelinated Fiber / Continuous Conduction (3 marks):** - No insulating sheath - Voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels distributed **uniformly along the entire axon** - Local circuit currents depolarize the **adjacent membrane** segment by segment - The action potential propagates as a **continuous wave** - Conduction velocity: **0.5 to 2 m/s** (slow) - Found in: C fibers (pain), postganglionic autonomic fibers **Step 2 — Myelinated Fiber / Saltatory Conduction (3 marks):** - Axon wrapped in **myelin sheath** (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) - Myelin is a **lipid-rich electrical insulator** - Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are **clustered only at Nodes of Ranvier** (~1 μm gap every 1–2 mm) - Local circuit currents **jump** from one node to the next - Action potential appears to **"leap"** along the axon (Latin *saltare* = to leap) - Conduction velocity: **up to 120 m/s** (very fast) - More energy efficient — ion pumping only needed at nodes **Step 3 — Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Feature | Unmyelinated | Myelinated | |---|---|---| | Conduction velocity | 0.5–2 m/s | 5–120 m/s | | Mechanism | Continuous | Saltatory | | Energy cost | High | Low | | Na⁺ channel distribution | Uniform | At nodes only | | Examples | C fibers | A-alpha motor fibers | --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Mechanism of Chemical Synaptic Transmission (5 marks):** 1. **Action potential arrival** at the presynaptic terminal 2. **Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open**; Ca²⁺ ions flow into the terminal down their electrochemical gradient 3. **Synaptic vesicles** containing neurotransmitter migrate to the presynaptic membrane 4. Ca²⁺ triggers **exocytosis** — vesicles fuse with the presynaptic membrane and release neurotransmitter into the **synaptic cleft** (20–40 nm wide) 5. Neurotransmitter **diffuses** across the cleft and binds to specific **receptors** on the postsynaptic membrane 6. This opens **ligand-gated ion channels**, causing **EPSP (excitatory, e.g., Na⁺ influx → depolarization)** or **IPSP (inhibitory, e.g., Cl⁻ influx or K⁺ efflux → hyperpolarization)** 7. Neurotransmitter effect is terminated by **enzymatic degradation** (e.g., acetylcholinesterase breaks down ACh) or **reuptake** into the presynaptic neuron **Step 2 — Four Common Neurotransmitters (2 marks):** | Neurotransmitter | Type | Function | |---|---|---| | **Acetylcholine (ACh)** | Excitatory (at NMJ) | Muscle contraction, memory, attention | | **Glutamate** | Major excitatory | Learning, memory (most common in CNS) | | **GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid)** | Major inhibitory | Reduces neuronal excitability, calming | | **Dopamine** | Excitatory/inhibitory | Reward, motor control (loss → Parkinson's) | | (Bonus: Norepinephrine, Serotonin, Endorphins) | | | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors in Nernst Equation:** Students often write `[ion]_in / [ion]_out` instead of `[ion]_out / [ion]_in`. The CORRECT form is **outside over inside**. Marks deducted: 1–2. > > 2. **Confusing E_K and E_Na Values:** Always state **E_K = −90 mV** (resting) and **E_Na = +60 mV** (peak), not the other way around. The peak AP does NOT reach +60 mV in real neurons (it reaches +30 mV) — that's where E_Na is the theoretical limit. > > 3. **Forgetting to Convert T to Kelvin:** Use **T = 310 K (37 °C)**. Using 273 K gives wrong numerical answers. Common: 1 mark lost. > > 4. **Writing "Stimulus causes nerve impulse" without explaining threshold:** Always mention **threshold = −55 mV** and the **all-or-none law**. Vague answers → 1 mark deduction. > > 5. **Mixing up Saltatory with Continuous Conduction:** Saltatory = myelinated = JUMPING at nodes. Continuous = unmyelinated = SMOOTH WAVE. Examiners specifically look for the word "Node of Ranvier." > > 6. **Missing the Role of Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Pump:** Many students forget that the pump restores ion gradients AFTER each action potential. This is essential — without it, neurons would fail to fire repeatedly. > > 7. **Confusing EPSP/IPSP with Action Potential:** EPSP/IPSP are **graded potentials** in the dendrites/soma; they only trigger AP at the **axon hillock** if threshold is reached. > > 8. **Not Labelling Diagram Axes:** When drawing action potential graph, ALWAYS label: **X-axis = Time (ms), Y-axis = Membrane Potential (mV)**. Unlabelled diagrams = 1 mark lost. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **🚀 HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM** **✅ Resting Membrane Potential:** - Value: **−70 mV** (inside negative) - Maintained by: **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in) and **K⁺ leak channels** - Permeability at rest: P_K >> P_Na → V_m approaches E_K **✅ Nernst Equation:** $$E_{ion} = \frac{0.0592}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} \text{ V (at 37 °C)}$$ - E_K = **−90 mV**, E_Na = **+60 mV**, E_Ca = **+120 mV** **✅ Goldman Equation:** Used when membrane is permeable to multiple ions; weights ions by their permeability coefficients. **✅ Action Potential Phases:** Resting → Stimulus → Threshold (−55 mV) → **Depolarization (Na⁺ in)** → Peak (+30 mV) → **Repolarization (K⁺ out)** → **Hyperpolarization (−80 mV)** → Return to resting **✅ Voltage-Gated Channels:** - **Na⁺ channel:** 3 states (closed → open → inactivated); fast - **K⁺ channel:** 2 states (closed → open); slower; delayed rectifier **✅ Threshold & All-or-None:** - Threshold = **−55 mV**; below it = graded potential; at/above it = full AP - Amplitude is FIXED; stimulus strength encoded in **frequency** of firing **✅ Refractory Periods:** - **Absolute (1–2 ms):** Na⁺ channels inactivated; NO AP possible; ensures **unidirectional** propagation - **Relative (3–4 ms):** K⁺ channels still open; only **stronger** stimulus triggers AP **✅ Propagation:** - **Continuous conduction:** unmyelinated, slow (0.5–2 m/s) - **Saltatory conduction:** myelinated, fast (up to 120 m/s); occurs at **Nodes of Ranvier** - Myelin = electrical insulator produced by **Schwann cells (PNS)** or **oligodendrocytes (CNS)** **✅ Conduction Velocity Rule:** v ≈ **6 × d** (m/s, d in μm) for mammalian myelinated fibers **✅ Synaptic Transmission:** - **Electrical synapse:** Gap junctions; very fast; bidirectional; found in cardiac muscle - **Chemical synapse:** Unidirectional; **0.5 ms synaptic delay**; requires Ca²⁺-dependent neurotransmitter release - Neurotransmitter examples: **ACh, Glutamate, GABA, Dopamine, Serotonin, Norepinephrine** - Postsynaptic response: **EPSP (excitatory)** or **IPSP (inhibitory)** **✅ Fiber Classification (Erlanger-Gasser):** - A-alpha (largest, fastest, motor/proprioception) → A-beta → A-gamma → A-delta (fast pain) → B (autonomic preganglionic) → C (smallest, slowest, slow pain) **✅ Clinical Correlations:** - **Multiple Sclerosis (MS):** Demyelination in CNS → slowed/blocked saltatory conduction - **Guillain-Barré Syndrome:** Demyelination in PNS → ascending paralysis - **Local anesthetics (Lidocaine, Procaine):** Block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels - **Tetrodotoxin (pufferfish):** Powerful Na⁺ channel blocker → paralysis **✅ Key Constants to Remember:** - T = **310 K** (37 °C) - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - F = **96,485 C/mol** - RT/F at 37 °C = **26.71 mV** - 2.303 RT/F at 37 °C = **61.5 mV** --- **END OF MODULE 2 — MECHANISMS OF NERVE IMPULSES NOTES** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Synapses and Neuronal Integration. Synaptic Potentials - EPSP and IPSP. Neurotransmitters - Types. Special Senses: Organ of Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synapses and Neuronal Integration. Synaptic Potentials - EPSP and IPSP. Neurotransmitters - Types. Special Senses: Organ of Vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Synapse — The Communication Bridge of the Nervous System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Textbook-Standard)**
> A **synapse** is a specialized functional junction that permits a neuron to pass an electrical or chemical signal to another neuron, gland, or muscle cell (effector). The term was coined by **Sir Charles Sherrington** in **1897** from the Greek words *syn* (together) and *haptein* (to clasp).

Every thought you think, every heartbeat, every blink — is the result of trillions of these microscopic conversations happening between neurons. A single human brain contains an estimated **10¹⁵ (one quadrillion)** synapses, with the **neocortex** alone containing roughly **125 trillion** synaptic connections. This makes the brain the most complex information-processing system known to science, and the synapse is its fundamental computational unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Relay Station"**
> Imagine a synapse as a **post office between two cities** (the pre- and post-synaptic neurons). The "letter" (neurotransmitter molecules) is dispatched from the "sender's counter" (presynaptic terminal), travels across the "highway bridge" (synaptic cleft — about **20–40 nanometers** wide), and is read by the "recipient clerk" (postsynaptic receptors). The clerk then decides whether to forward a positive memo (Excitatory Post-Synaptic Potential — EPSP) or a "do not act" notice (Inhibitory Post-Synaptic Potential — IPSP). Multiple clerks (synapses) read multiple letters at once, and the "manager" (axon hillock) sums up the total mail to decide if the neuron will fire a new message.

---

### 1.2 Synaptic Potentials — EPSP and IPSP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EPSP (Excitatory Post-Synaptic Potential)** — A **transient depolarization** of the postsynaptic membrane that **increases the probability** of the postsynaptic neuron firing an action potential. Typical amplitude: **0.1 mV to a few mV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPSP (Inhibitory Post-Synaptic Potential)** — A **transient hyperpolarization** (or shunting inhibition) of the postsynaptic membrane that **decreases the probability** of the postsynaptic neuron firing an action potential.

| Parameter | EPSP | IPSP |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane potential change | Depolarization (less negative) | Hyperpolarization (more negative) |
| Direction relative to threshold | Toward threshold (≈ **−55 mV**) | Away from threshold |
| Typical ions involved | Na⁺ influx, K⁺ efflux | Cl⁻ influx, K⁺ efflux |
| Common neurotransmitters | Glutamate, Aspartate | GABA, Glycine |
| Effect on neuron | **Excitation** | **Inhibition** |
| Reversal potential (E_rev) | ≈ **0 mV** (for Na⁺-driven) | ≈ **−70 mV** (Cl⁻) or **−90 mV** (K⁺) |
| Summation behavior | Temporal + Spatial | Temporal + Spatial |

---

### 1.3 Neurotransmitters — The Chemical Messengers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neurotransmitters** are endogenous **chemical messengers** synthesized and stored in presynaptic neurons. They are released into the synaptic cleft upon arrival of an action potential and bind to specific receptors on the postsynaptic membrane to elicit a response.

The classical definition by **Sir Henry Dale (1935)** required four criteria: (1) synthesis in the presynaptic neuron, (2) storage in synaptic vesicles, (3) release by Ca²⁺-dependent exocytosis, and (4) reproduction of action by exogenous application. Modern neuroscience recognizes **>100** distinct neurotransmitter substances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Keys and Locks"**
> Neurotransmitters are like **keys**, and postsynaptic receptors are the **locks**. Each key opens only certain locks (specificity), but the same key can open different doors in different rooms of the same building (a single neurotransmitter can have multiple receptor subtypes, e.g., acetylcholine acts on both **nicotinic** and **muscarinic** receptors).

---

### 1.4 Special Senses: Organ of Vision — The Eye

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vision** is the **special sense** by which the brain interprets electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (wavelengths ≈ **400–700 nm**) detected by photoreceptor cells of the retina.

The human eye is a roughly spherical organ of diameter **~24 mm** and mass **~7.5 grams**, functioning as a sophisticated optical instrument analogous to a **camera**:
- **Cornea + Lens** → camera lens system (refractive power ≈ **+60 dioptres**)
- **Iris + Pupil** → aperture diaphragm
- **Retina** → photographic film / CMOS sensor
- **Sclera** → camera body
- **Choroid** → light-absorbing inner coating

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: Students frequently confuse the **organ of Corti** (hearing — Module 2/3 topic in some syllabi) with the **organ of vision** (eye). Remember: vision = photoreceptors (rods & cones); hearing = mechanoreceptors (hair cells of basilar membrane).

---

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters for Biomedical Engineers

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance**
> - **Neural prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs)** — record EPSP/IPSP-like field potentials via EEG/ECoG.
> - **Drug delivery systems** — targeted neurotransmitter agonists/antagonists require understanding of synaptic pharmacology.
> - **Retinal implants (e.g., Argus II)** — restore vision by electrically stimulating the retina, mimicking photoreceptor output.
> - **Biopotential amplifiers** must reject the typical EPSP bandwidth of **0.1 Hz – 5 kHz**.
> - **Synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD)** forms the biological basis of **artificial neural network learning rules** (e.g., Hebbian learning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Chemical Synapse

A chemical synapse comprises three operational regions:

1. **Presynaptic terminal (axon terminal / bouton)** — Contains:
   - **Synaptic vesicles** (diameter ≈ **40–50 nm**) filled with neurotransmitter.
   - **Mitochondria** providing ATP for vesicle recycling.
   - **Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** (P/Q-type, N-type) clustered in "active zones."
   - **SNARE proteins** (Synaptobrevin, Syntaxin, SNAP-25) mediating exocytosis.

2. **Synaptic cleft** — A fluid-filled space of width **20–40 nm** containing:
   - Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and other degradative enzymes.
   - Extracellular matrix proteins (e.g., neurexins, neuroligins).
   - Reuptake transporters (e.g., SERT, DAT, NET).

3. **Postsynaptic membrane** — Contains:
   - **Ligand-gated ion channels (ionotropic receptors)** for fast EPSP/IPSP.
   - **G-protein coupled receptors (metabotropic receptors)** for slow/modulatory responses.
   - **Postsynaptic density (PSD)** — a protein scaffold containing PSD-95, Homer, Shank.

---

### 2.2 Types of Synapses — A Hierarchical Classification

| Type | Subtype | Key Feature | Speed | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Axo-dendritic | On dendrite | 1–5 ms delay | Cortical pyramidal cells |
| | Axo-somatic | On soma | 1–5 ms | Cerebellar Purkinje |
| | Axo-axonic | On axon (presynaptic inhibition) | 1–5 ms | Spinal cord |
| | Dendro-dendritic | Between dendrites | 1–5 ms | Olfactory bulb |
| **Electrical** | Gap junction (Connexin-36) | Direct ionic flow | <0.1 ms | Cardiac muscle, retina |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Contrast** — Electrical synapses transmit **bidirectionally** and are **virtually instantaneous**, while chemical synapses are **unidirectional** and have a characteristic **synaptic delay of ≈ 0.5 ms** (Sherrington, 1906), determined primarily by Ca²⁺-triggered vesicle fusion.

---

### 2.3 The Six-Step Process of Chemical Synaptic Transmission

1. **Action potential arrival** at the presynaptic terminal (AP propagates at 1–120 m/s).
2. **Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** → Ca²⁺ influx (driving force ≈ −60 mV; [Ca²⁺]₀ = **1.2 mM**, [Ca²⁺]ᵢ = **100 nM**, a 10,000:1 gradient).
3. **Ca²⁺ binds synaptotagmin** → triggers SNARE-mediated vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane.
4. **Exocytosis** releases neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft (quantal release; each vesicle = 1 quantum, ≈ **5,000–10,000 molecules** of ACh).
5. **Diffusion across cleft** (t = d²/6D; for d = 30 nm and D = 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s → t ≈ **15 μs**).
6. **Receptor binding** opens postsynaptic ion channels → EPSP or IPSP. Neurotransmitter is then cleared by **reuptake**, **enzymatic degradation**, or **diffusion** (within 1–2 ms).

---

### 2.4 Ionic Basis of EPSPs and IPSPs

#### EPSP Mechanism
- Agonist (e.g., glutamate) binds **AMPA receptor** (a non-selective cation channel).
- Channel opens → **Na⁺ influx** (and some K⁺ efflux).
- Membrane potential **depolarizes** from resting (**−70 mV**) toward EPSP reversal potential (**E_EPSP ≈ 0 mV**).
- Single EPSP amplitude = **0.1–5 mV** — subthreshold by design.

#### IPSP Mechanism
- Agonist (e.g., GABA) binds **GABA_A receptor** (a Cl⁻-selective channel).
- Channel opens → **Cl⁻ influx** (driving force inward at rest, [Cl⁻]ᵢ ≈ 4 mM, [Cl⁻]₀ ≈ 130 mM).
- Membrane potential **hyperpolarizes** toward E_Cl ≈ **−70 mV** (often near or below rest).
- Alternatively, K⁺ channels open (GABA_B via G-protein) → E_K ≈ **−90 mV** → hyperpolarization.

---

### 2.5 Neuronal Integration — The Arithmetic of the Axon Hillock

A typical neuron receives **1,000–10,000 synaptic inputs**. The **axon hillock** acts as the neuron's "summation junction" and **trigger zone** (highest density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels — ~1,000–2,000/μm²).

**Two forms of summation:**

- **Temporal Summation** — Successive EPSPs from the *same* presynaptic neuron add up if they arrive within the **membrane time constant (τ_m ≈ 5–50 ms)**.
- **Spatial Summation** — Simultaneous EPSPs from *different* presynaptic neurons are summed across the dendritic tree.

**Net effect:**

$$V_{hillock}(t) = V_{rest} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j} \text{EPSP}_{i,j} \cdot e^{-(t-t_j)/\tau_m} - \sum_{k} \text{IPSP}_k \cdot e^{-(t-t_k)/\tau_m}$$

If $V_{hillock} \geq V_{threshold}$ (≈ **−55 mV**), an action potential is fired (all-or-nothing principle).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ (Nernst) | $R$ = gas const (**8.314 J/mol·K**); $T$ = temp (**310 K**); $z$ = valency; $F$ = Faraday (**96,485 C/mol**) | Volts (V) | Equilibrium potential of a single ion |
| 2 | $E_{ion} \approx \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ at 37°C | log10 form | mV | Clinical / bedside calculation |
| 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{P_K[E_K] + P_{Na}[E_{Na}] + P_{Cl}[E_{Cl}]}{P_K + P_{Na} + P_{Cl}}$ (GHK) | $P$ = permeability | mV | Resting membrane potential |
| 4 | Synaptic delay $\approx 0.5$ ms | — | ms | One-way chemical transmission |
| 5 | Quantal content $m = \dfrac{\text{Mean EPSP amplitude}}{\text{Unit quantum amplitude}}$ | — | dimensionless | Vesicle release statistics |
| 6 | Membrane time constant $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | $R_m$ = membrane resistance; $C_m$ = capacitance | seconds (s) | Temporal summation window |
| 7 | Cable equation $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | $r_m$ = membrane resistivity; $r_i$ = axial resistivity | m (length constant) | Dendritic signal spread |
| 8 | $I = g \cdot (V_m - E_{rev})$ | $g$ = conductance; $E_{rev}$ = reversal potential | A (amperes) | Synaptic current |
| 9 | Conductance $g = \bar{g} \cdot P_{open}$ | $\bar{g}$ = max conductance; $P_{open}$ = open probability | Siemens (S) | Channel kinetics |
| 10 | Visual acuity $\text{MAR} = \dfrac{1}{60}° \approx 1 \text{ arc-minute}$ | — | degrees (°) | 20/20 vision standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application** — The Nernst and GHK equations are the same mathematical forms used in **electrochemistry, biosensor design, ion-selective electrodes, and pH meters**. The Goldman equation is also fundamental to **Nernst-Planck modelling of drug diffusion through membranes**.

---

### 2.7 Neurotransmitter Classification — Full Taxonomy

#### A. By Chemical Structure

| Class | Members | Receptor Type | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amino acids** | Glutamate, GABA, Glycine, Aspartate | Ionotropic & metabotropic | Excitatory / Inhibitory |
| **Biogenic amines** | Acetylcholine, Dopamine, Norepinephrine, Epinephrine, Serotonin, Histamine | Mostly GPCR; ACh also nicotinic | Mixed |
| **Neuropeptides** | Substance P, Enkephalins, Endorphins, Neuropeptide Y, VIP, Somatostatin | Metabotropic (GPCR) | Slow, modulatory |
| **Purines** | ATP, Adenosine | Ionotropic (P2X) & PPCR (P2Y, A1–A3) | Mixed |
| **Gasotransmitters** | NO (nitric oxide), CO, H₂S | Direct (guanylyl cyclase) | Diffuse retrograde |
| **Lipid messengers** | Anandamide, 2-AG (endocannabinoids) | CB1/CB2 (GPCR) | Retrograde inhibition |

#### B. By Functional Effect (Excitatory vs. Inhibitory)

- **Excitatory**: Glutamate, Aspartate, ACh (at muscle), Substance P, ATP (some).
- **Inhibitory**: GABA, Glycine, Serotonin (mostly), Dopamine (D2), Endorphins.
- **Modulatory** (alter response to other NTs): Dopamine, Serotonin, Norepinephrine, ACh (muscarinic), Neuropeptides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dale's Principle (Modified)** — Originally stated that a neuron releases *one* neurotransmitter. It is now established that neurons can release **multiple transmitters** from the same terminal (**co-transmission**), e.g., ACh + VIP, or Glutamate + Substance P.

---

### 2.8 Anatomy of the Organ of Vision — Layered Structure

#### A. Three Coats (Tunics) of the Eyeball

1. **Outer Fibrous Tunic** — Cornea (anterior 1/6, transparent, refractive power ≈ **+43 D**) + Sclera (posterior 5/6, white, protective).
2. **Middle Vascular Tunic (Uvea)** — Iris (with sphincter & dilator pupillae muscles) + Ciliary body (produces aqueous humor, controls lens shape) + Choroid (vascular supply, absorbs stray light).
3. **Inner Neural Tunic** — Retina (photoreception).

#### B. Optical Components

- **Aqueous humor** — in anterior chamber; nourishes cornea/lens; drains via **trabecular meshwork** → **Schlemm's canal**.
- **Vitreous humor** — in posterior chamber; transparent gel; maintains intraocular pressure (**IOP ≈ 15 mmHg**, range 10–21 mmHg).
- **Lens** — biconvex, avascular, elastic; refractive power ≈ **+19 D**; total eye ≈ **+60 D**.

#### C. Retinal Layers (from inner to outer)

1. Inner Limiting Membrane (ILM)
2. Nerve Fiber Layer (axons of ganglion cells)
3. Ganglion Cell Layer (GCL)
4. Inner Plexiform Layer (IPL)
5. Inner Nuclear Layer (INL) — bipolar, horizontal, amacrine cells
6. Outer Plexiform Layer (OPL)
7. Outer Nuclear Layer (ONL) — rod & cone nuclei
8. Outer Limiting Membrane (OLM)
9. Photoreceptor Layer (rods & cones)
10. Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Fact** — Light passes through ALL the neural layers *before* hitting the photoreceptors (an "inverted" retina), because the photoreceptor outer segments contain the visual pigments (rhodopsin, photopsins). The fovea is the exception — inner layers are pushed aside to maximize acuity.

#### D. Photoreceptors

| Feature | Rods | Cones |
|---|---|---|
| Number per eye | ≈ **120 million** | ≈ **6–7 million** |
| Visual pigment | **Rhodopsin** (retinal + opsin) | **Photopsins** (3 types: S, M, L) |
| Peak sensitivity | ≈ **507 nm** (blue-green) | S: 420 nm, M: 530 nm, L: 560 nm |
| Sensitivity | High (scotopic / dim light) | Low (photopic / bright light) |
| Acuity | Low | High |
| Convergence | High (up to 1000:1) | Low (1:1 in fovea) |
| Color vision | No (achromatic) | Yes (trichromatic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phototransduction Cascade (Brief)**
> 1. Photon hits **11-cis-retinal** → isomerizes to **all-trans-retinal**.
> 2. Retinal activates **opsin → metarhodopsin II (R\*)**.
> 3. R\* activates **transducin (G-protein)**.
> 4. Transducin activates **phosphodiesterase (PDE6)**.
> 5. PDE hydrolyses **cGMP → 5'-GMP**.
> 6. [cGMP] drops → cGMP-gated Na⁺/Ca²⁺ channels **CLOSE**.
> 7. Na⁺/Ca²⁺ influx stops → **hyperpolarization** (from −40 mV to −70 mV).
> 8. Hyperpolarization **decreases glutamate release** onto bipolar cells.
> > Photoreceptors are the only neurons that are **depolarized in the dark** and **hyperpolarized in the light** — a unique feature of the visual system.

---

### 2.9 The Visual Pathway

$$\text{Rods/Cones} \to \text{Bipolar cells} \to \text{Ganglion cells} \to \text{Optic nerve} \to \text{Optic chiasm} \to \text{Optic tract} \to \text{LGN of thalamus} \to \text{Optic radiation} \to \text{Primary visual cortex (V1, Brodmann area 17, calcarine sulcus)}$$

Key relay stations:
- **Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)** — 6 layers; magnocellular (M) layers 1–2 (motion, low acuity) and parvocellular (P) layers 3–6 (color, fine detail).
- **Superior Colliculus** — reflexive eye movements.
- **Suprachiasmatic Nucleus (SCN)** — circadian rhythm entrainment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake** — Damage to the optic chiasm (e.g., pituitary tumor) causes **bitemporal hemianopia** (loss of *temporal* visual fields), NOT nasal hemianopia. This is a **classic KTU/NEET-PG/MBBS exam trap**.

---

### 2.10 Visual Acuity, Field & Errors of Refraction

- **Visual field** — **≈ 200°** (temporal), **≈ 60°** (nasal), **≈ 50°** (superior), **≈ 70°** (inferior). **Blind spot** at **15° temporal** (optic disc — no photoreceptors).
- **Normal acuity** — **6/6** (metric) or **20/20** (imperial) = resolvable angular size of **1 arc-minute (1/60 degree)**.
- **Refractive errors**:
  - **Myopia** — eye too long; image focuses *in front* of retina; corrected with **concave (diverging) lens**.
  - **Hyperopia** — eye too short; image focuses *behind* retina; corrected with **convex (converging) lens**.
  - **Astigmatism** — irregular corneal curvature; corrected with **cylindrical/toric lens**.
  - **Presbyopia** — age-related loss of lens elasticity; corrected with **reading (convex) lens**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Foundation of All Synaptic Potentials)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation explains *why* the EPSP reversal potential is approximately **0 mV** for a Na⁺-dominated channel — a frequently asked KTU question.

The Nernst equation arises from the equality of two thermodynamic driving forces at equilibrium: the **electrical work** required to move an ion across a membrane, and the **chemical work** available from its concentration gradient.

**Step 1** — Consider a membrane permeable only to ion X with charge $z$ and concentrations $[X]_o$ outside and $[X]_i$ inside.

**Step 2** — At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** ($\mu$) is equal on both sides:

$$\mu_o = \mu_i$$

**Step 3** — Electrochemical potential is the sum of chemical potential ($\mu^0 + RT \ln [X]$) and electrical potential ($zFE$):

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_o + zF \cdot E_o = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_i + zF \cdot E_i$$

**Step 4** — The constants $\mu^0$ cancel:

$$RT \ln[X]_o + zF \cdot E_o = RT \ln[X]_i + zF \cdot E_i$$

**Step 5** — Rearrange to isolate the potential difference $E_{ion} = E_i - E_o$:

$$zF \cdot (E_i - E_o) = RT \cdot \ln[X]_i - RT \cdot \ln[X]_o$$

$$zF \cdot E_{ion} = RT \cdot \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$$

**Step 6** — Solve for $E_{ion}$:

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} \text{ (sign-convention variant)}}$$

**Step 7** — Convert from natural log to base 10 using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$$

**Step 8** — At body temperature $T = 310$ K:

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 310}{96{,}485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 9** — Final KTU-ready form:

$$\boxed{E_{ion} \text{ (mV)} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} \quad \text{at } 37°C}$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Application — Nernst Potentials for the Ions Involved in EPSP/IPSP

Using the standard mammalian intracellular/extracellular concentrations:

| Ion | $[X]_o$ (mM) | $[X]_i$ (mM) | $z$ | $E_{ion}$ (mV) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 4.5 | 140 | +1 | $E_K = 61.5 \cdot \log(140/4.5) \approx -90$ | IPSP, resting potential |
| Na⁺ | 145 | 12 | +1 | $E_{Na} = 61.5 \cdot \log(12/145) \approx +60$ | EPSP, action potential upstroke |
| Ca²⁺ | 1.2 | 0.0001 | +2 | $E_{Ca} = (61.5/2) \cdot \log(0.0001/1.2) \approx +123$ | Neurotransmitter release trigger |
| Cl⁻ | 130 | 4 | −1 | $E_{Cl} = -61.5 \cdot \log(4/130) \approx -90$ | IPSP (GABA, glycine) |

**Sample calculation for $E_K$ (row 1, fully worked):**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{140}{4.5} = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}(31.11) = 61.5 \cdot (1.493) \approx 91.8 \text{ mV}$$

The negative sign convention (work done *on* the cell moving from outside to inside) gives $E_K \approx -90$ mV. This matches experimentally measured values within **±2 mV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for synapses** — The reversal potential $E_{rev}$ of a synaptic current is the *weighted* Nernst potential of the permeant ions. For an AMPA-receptor EPSP (Na⁺ + K⁺ permeable), the reversal is closer to **0 mV** (the average of $E_{Na} \approx +60$ and $E_K \approx -90$ weighted by relative permeabilities). For a GABA-A IPSP (Cl⁻ selective), $E_{rev} \approx E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Synaptic Current and Conductance Relationship

The current through a single postsynaptic ion channel obeys **Ohm's law**:

$$I = g \cdot (V_m - E_{rev})$$

- $I$ = ionic current through the channel (amperes, A)
- $g$ = conductance of the channel (siemens, S)
- $V_m$ = current membrane potential (volts, V)
- $E_{rev}$ = reversal potential of the ion(s) the channel passes (volts, V)

When neurotransmitter binds, many channels open simultaneously. The total synaptic current is the **sum of all open channel currents**:

$$I_{syn}(t) = \bar{g}_{syn} \cdot P_{open}(t) \cdot (V_m(t) - E_{rev})$$

**Derivation of the EPSP amplitude** — Treating the postsynaptic membrane as an RC circuit, the synaptic current charges the membrane capacitance $C_m$:

$$I_{syn} = C_m \cdot \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m - V_{rest}}{R_m}$$

For a brief synaptic current pulse (much shorter than $\tau_m = R_m C_m$), the peak voltage change (the EPSP) simplifies to:

$$\Delta V_{EPSP} = \frac{I_{syn} \cdot R_m}{1 + (t_{syn}/\tau_m)} \approx I_{syn} \cdot R_m \quad \text{(for brief input)}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Membrane is modeled as parallel RC (resistor $R_m$ in parallel with capacitor $C_m$).
2. Synaptic current $I_{syn}$ splits between charging $C_m$ and flowing through $R_m$.
3. Steady-state $\frac{dV}{dt}=0$, so all current flows through $R_m$ → $\Delta V = I_{syn} \cdot R_m$.
4. For transient inputs, the effective response is reduced by a factor depending on $\tau_m$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Parallel** — This is identical to the **RC charging equation** in any first-order electronic circuit ($V(t) = V_\infty(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$). A "leaky integrate-and-fire" neuron model is the **direct circuit analog** of synaptic integration.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Spatial Summation — Linear Cable Theory

For a passive dendrite, the steady-state voltage decays exponentially with distance from the synaptic input:

$$V(x) = V_0 \cdot e^{-x/\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **length constant**:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

with $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length (Ω·m) and $r_i$ = axial (intracellular) resistance per unit length (Ω/m).

For multiple simultaneous inputs at different locations $x_i$:

$$V_{soma} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{0,i} \cdot e^{-x_i/\lambda}$$

This is the biophysical basis of **spatial summation**.

> [!TIP]
> A typical cortical pyramidal neuron has $\lambda \approx 0.5$ mm — inputs more than 1 mm from the soma produce negligible EPSP at the hillock when evoked alone, but can sum effectively with other inputs.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Visual Angle (Snellen Acuity Calculation)

A Snellen chart letter subtending **5 arc-minutes** is composed of strokes that subtend **1 arc-minute** — this is the standard for 6/6 (20/20) vision.

$$\text{Visual angle (arcmin)} = \frac{\text{Letter height (mm)}}{57.3 \cdot \text{Distance (m)}} \cdot 60$$

For a letter of height $h$ viewed at distance $d$:

$$\theta \text{ (radians)} = \frac{h}{d} \Rightarrow \theta \text{ (arcmin)} = \frac{h}{d} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot 60 \approx 3438 \cdot \frac{h}{d}$$

**Worked example:** A 9 mm letter at 6 m → $\theta = 3438 \cdot (0.009/6) = 5.16$ arcmin → approximates the 6/6 line.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — EPSP vs. IPSP Decision Table

| Property | EPSP | IPSP |
|---|---|---|
| Ion(s) | Na⁺ (mainly), some K⁺, Ca²⁺ | Cl⁻ (mainly), K⁺ |
| Driving force at rest | $V_m - E_{rev} = -70 - 0 = -70$ mV (inward) | $V_m - E_{rev} = -70 - (-70) = 0$ mV (no net) at E_Cl = -70 |
| Reversal potential | ≈ **0 mV** | ≈ **−70 mV** (Cl⁻) or **−90 mV** (K⁺) |
| Membrane resistance change | Decreases (channels open) | Decreases (shunting) |
| Effect on firing probability | ↑ (closer to threshold) | ↓ (further from threshold) |
| Action on axon hillock | Depolarization | Hyperpolarization / shunting |
| Conductance change | ↑$g_{Na}$ | ↑$g_{Cl}$ or ↑$g_K$ |
| Synaptic integration | Adds algebraically with other inputs | Subtracts from total depolarization |
| Plasticity (LTP/LTD) | Hebbian: correlated firing → strengthening | Can also exhibit LTD |
| Examples in CNS | Cortical glutamatergic synapses | Cerebellar Purkinje (GABAergic) input |
