## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Cardiac Output**. Mention its normal resting value and the formula used to calculate it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Cardiac Output (CO)** is defined as the **volume of blood pumped by each ventricle per minute**. It is calculated using the formula:
$$CO = HR \times SV$$
where HR = Heart Rate (beats/min) and SV = Stroke Volume (mL/beat). The normal resting value of cardiac output in a healthy adult is approximately **5 L/min** (range: 4–6 L/min). It can increase up to **20–25 L/min** during strenuous exercise in trained athletes. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Systemic Circulation** and **Pulmonary Circulation**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Systemic Circulation | Pulmonary Circulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Supplies O₂ and nutrients to body tissues; collects CO₂ and waste | Facilitates gas exchange in the lungs |
| **Pump** | Left ventricle (high pressure ≈ 120 mmHg) | Right ventricle (low pressure ≈ 25 mmHg) |
| **Arteries carry** | Oxygenated blood | Deoxygenated blood |
| **Veins carry** | Deoxygenated blood | Oxygenated blood |
| **Path length** | Long; high resistance | Short; low resistance (≈ 1/6 systemic) |
| **Wall thickness of pump** | LV ≈ 13–15 mm (thick) | RV ≈ 3–5 mm (thin) |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

> **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **anatomy of the human heart** with a neat labeled diagram. Explain the structure and functions of the **four chambers** and the **four valves**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **cardiac conduction system** in detail. Describe the **cardiac action potential** in ventricular myocytes with a suitable waveform diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:

**(a) Anatomy of the Human Heart (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The human heart is a **muscular, four-chambered organ** located in the **mediastinum** of the thoracic cavity, slightly tilted to the left. It weighs approximately **300 g** in adults and is roughly the size of a clenched fist. It functions as a **double pump** that drives the systemic and pulmonary circulations.

**The Four Chambers (3 marks):**

1. **Right Atrium (RA):** Forms the right border of the heart. Receives **deoxygenated blood** from the **superior vena cava** (drains head, neck, upper limbs), **inferior vena cava** (drains lower body), and **coronary sinus** (drains cardiac veins). Lined internally by **pectinate muscles**. Contains the **SA node** (pacemaker) near the SVC opening.

2. **Right Ventricle (RV):** Forms most of the anterior surface of the heart. Pumps deoxygenated blood into the **pulmonary trunk** via the pulmonary semilunar valve. The wall is **3–5 mm thick**. Contains **trabeculae carneae**, the **moderator band**, and **papillary muscles** that anchor the tricuspid valve via **chordae tendineae**.

3. **Left Atrium (LA):** Forms the base of the heart. Receives **oxygenated blood** from **four pulmonary veins** (two from each lung). Mostly smooth-walled; pectinate muscles are confined to the auricle.

4. **Left Ventricle (LV):** Forms the apex and left border. Pumps oxygenated blood into the **aorta** via the aortic semilunar valve. Wall is **13–15 mm thick** (≈ 3× thicker than RV) because it must generate high pressure for systemic circulation. Contains two large **papillary muscles** (anterolateral and posteromedial).

**The Four Valves (3 marks):**

| Valve | Type | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tricuspid valve** | Atrioventricular | RA → RV | Prevents backflow into RA during ventricular systole. Anchored by chordae tendineae and papillary muscles. |
| **Bicuspid (Mitral) valve** | Atrioventricular | LA → LV | Prevents backflow into LA during ventricular systole. Has two cusps. |
| **Pulmonary semilunar valve** | Semilunar | RV → Pulmonary trunk | Prevents backflow into RV during diastole. Has three half-moon cusps. No chordae. |
| **Aortic semilunar valve** | Semilunar | LV → Aorta | Prevents backflow into LV during diastole. Has three half-moon cusps. |

**Conclusion:** The coordinated opening and closing of these valves ensure **unidirectional flow** of blood through the heart. The AV valves close during ventricular systole (producing **S1 "lub"**), while the semilunar valves close during diastole (producing **S2 "dub"**).

---

**(b) Cardiac Conduction System and Action Potential (7 marks):**

**Conduction System (3 marks):**

The heart is **autorhythmic** — it generates its own electrical impulses via specialized cells. The conduction pathway is:

1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node** — located in the RA wall near SVC opening. **Primary pacemaker** at **70–80 bpm**. Initiates each heartbeat by spontaneous depolarization (reaches threshold ≈ **−40 mV** via "funny" Na⁺ channels).

2. **Internodal Tracts (Bachmann, Wenckebach, Thorel)** — rapidly conduct the impulse across both atria, causing simultaneous atrial contraction.

3. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node** — located in the interatrial septum near the coronary sinus. **Delays the impulse by ≈ 0.1 second** (AV nodal delay) to allow complete ventricular filling. Acts as **backup pacemaker at 40–60 bpm**.

4. **Bundle of His** — the only electrical bridge between atria and ventricles, passing through the fibrous skeleton of the heart.

5. **Right and Left Bundle Branches** — descend along the interventricular septum.

6. **Purkinje Fibers** — large, fast-conducting fibers; spread the impulse from the apex upward throughout the ventricular myocardium → causes **apex-to-base contraction** for efficient ejection. Backup pacemaker at **20–40 bpm**.

**Cardiac Action Potential in Ventricular Myocytes (4 marks):**

The ventricular action potential has **5 phases** (numbered 0–4), with a characteristic **plateau (Phase 2)**:

| Phase | Voltage | Ionic Basis | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** — Rapid depolarization | −90 mV → +30 mV | Fast voltage-gated **Na⁺ influx** | ≈ 1–2 ms |
| **1** — Initial repolarization | +30 mV → ≈ 0 mV | **K⁺ efflux** + Na⁺ channel inactivation | ≈ 10 ms |
| **2** — Plateau | ≈ 0 mV (sustained) | **Ca²⁺ influx** (L-type) balances K⁺ efflux | ≈ 200 ms |
| **3** — Repolarization | 0 mV → −90 mV | **K⁺ efflux** (delayed rectifier) | ≈ 100 ms |
| **4** — Resting | −90 mV | K⁺ permeability high; Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase active | — |

**Significance of the Plateau (Phase 2):**
The prolonged Ca²⁺-mediated plateau produces an **absolute refractory period of ≈ 200–250 ms**, much longer than in skeletal muscle. This is a critical safety feature that **prevents tetanic contraction** of the heart, ensuring proper relaxation and filling between beats.

**Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR):** The Ca²⁺ entering during the plateau triggers **massive Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum** via **ryanodine receptors (RyR)**, which initiates the actual mechanical contraction (excitation-contraction coupling).

---

> **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 — ALTERNATIVE]**

**(a)** Draw a **labeled diagram of a normal ECG waveform** and explain the origin and significance of each wave and interval. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Stroke Volume** and **Ejection Fraction**. Derive the **Fick's Principle** for the measurement of cardiac output, and solve: If O₂ consumption is 280 mL/min, arterial O₂ content is 19 mL/dL, and mixed venous O₂ content is 13 mL/dL, calculate the cardiac output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:

**(a) ECG Waveform (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a graphical recording of the **summed electrical activity** of all cardiomyocytes, captured at the body surface using skin electrodes. Standard paper speed: **25 mm/s**; amplitude: **10 mm = 1 mV**.

**Waves and Intervals (6 marks):**

**P Wave:**
- **Origin:** Atrial depolarization (atrial repolarization is hidden).
- **Duration:** ≈ **0.08 s** (≤ 0.10 s).
- **Significance:** Marks the beginning of atrial contraction (atrial systole). Inverted P wave may indicate retrograde conduction.

**PR Interval:**
- **Origin:** Includes atrial depolarization + AV nodal conduction delay.
- **Duration:** **0.12–0.20 s** (3–5 small boxes).
- **Significance:** Measures AV node function. **Prolonged PR (> 0.20 s)** indicates **first-degree AV block**.

**QRS Complex:**
- **Origin:** Ventricular depolarization (atrial repolarization is hidden because ventricular mass is larger).
- **Duration:** **0.08–0.10 s** (≤ 0.12 s).
- **Significance:** Marks the beginning of ventricular contraction. **Widened QRS** indicates bundle branch block.

**ST Segment:**
- **Origin:** All ventricular cells are depolarized (plateau phase of action potential).
- **Duration:** ≈ **0.08 s**.
- **Significance:** Should be isoelectric. **Elevation or depression** indicates myocardial ischemia or infarction.

**T Wave:**
- **Origin:** Ventricular repolarization.
- **Duration:** ≈ **0.16 s**.
- **Significance:** Marks ventricular relaxation. **Tall peaked T waves** → hyperkalemia; **inverted T waves** → ischemia.

**QT Interval:**
- **Origin:** Total ventricular depolarization + repolarization.
- **Duration:** **0.32–0.44 s** (inversely related to heart rate).
- **Significance:** Prolonged QT increases risk of fatal arrhythmias (Torsades de Pointes).

**Calculation of Heart Rate from ECG (additional mark):**
$$
HR = \frac{300}{\text{Number of large boxes between two R-R intervals}}
$$

---

**(b) Stroke Volume, Ejection Fraction, and Fick's Principle (7 marks):**

**Stroke Volume (1 mark):**
**Stroke Volume (SV)** is the volume of blood ejected by **each ventricle per beat**.
$$
SV = EDV - ESV
$$
where EDV = End-Diastolic Volume (≈ **120 mL**) and ESV = End-Systolic Volume (≈ **50 mL**). Normal SV ≈ **70 mL/beat**.

**Ejection Fraction (1 mark):**
**Ejection Fraction (EF)** is the **fraction of end-diastolic volume ejected per beat**:
$$
EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\% = \frac{70}{120} \times 100\% \approx 58.3\%
$$
**Normal EF: 55–70%.** Reduced EF indicates systolic heart failure.

**Fick's Principle Derivation (3 marks):**

Adolf Fick (1870) proposed that the uptake of a substance by an organ equals the blood flow through that organ times the **arteriovenous concentration difference**.

For oxygen, the body consumes $\dot{V}O_2$ mL O₂ per minute. The amount of O₂ delivered per minute equals CO × arterial O₂ content, and the O₂ remaining equals CO × venous O₂ content. Hence:

$$
\dot{V}O_2 = CO \times (CaO_2 - CvO_2)
$$

Solving for CO:

$$
CO = \frac{\dot{V}O_2}{CaO_2 - CvO_2}
$$

Unit conversion: The denominator is in mL O₂/dL blood. Since 1 L = 10 dL:

$$
CO \text{ (L/min)} = \frac{\dot{V}O_2 \text{ (mL/min)}}{[CaO_2 - CvO_2] \text{ (mL/dL)} \times 10 \text{ (dL/L)}}
$$

**Numerical Problem (2 marks):**

Given:
- $\dot{V}O_2 = 280$ mL/min
- $CaO_2 = 19$ mL/dL
- $CvO_2 = 13$ mL/dL

Substitute:

$$
CO = \frac{280}{(19 - 13) \times 10} = \frac{280}{60} = 4.67 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Conclusion:** The cardiac output of the patient is **≈ 4.67 L/min**, which is within the normal range (4–6 L/min).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module (KTU 2024 Examiner's Perspective)**
>
> 1. **Confusing artery/vein by O₂ content:** Pulmonary arteries carry **deoxygenated** blood, pulmonary veins carry **oxygenated** blood. Naming follows **direction of flow**, not oxygenation. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Missing the AV nodal delay in conduction sequence:** The 0.1 s delay at the AV node is **functionally critical** — without it, atria and ventricles would contract simultaneously, defeating efficient filling. Forgetting to mention this = –2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing ECG waves without proper time calibration:** KTU expects you to label the duration of each wave (P ≈ 0.08 s, QRS ≈ 0.08–0.10 s, T ≈ 0.16 s). Just saying "P wave" without scale = –1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting Phase 2 plateau in action potential:** This is the **defining feature** of cardiac action potential. Missing it = –2 marks, as examiners specifically look for it to differentiate from neuronal/skeletal muscle AP.
> 5. **Not distinguishing absolute vs. relative refractory period:** The long **absolute refractory period** (≈ 200–250 ms) is the safety mechanism preventing tetany. The relative refractory period is brief and follows. Confusing them = –1 mark.
> 6. **Writing the SA node as "70 bpm" without specifying "intrinsic rate":** KTU expects precision — write **"intrinsic rate of 70–80 bpm, normally under vagal tone at rest."** Vague answers lose marks.
> 7. **Confusing "Heart Rate" with "Cardiac Output":** CO = HR × SV; these are **different quantities**. (–1 mark if confused)
> 8. **In Pulmonary vs. Systemic question, forgetting wall thickness comparison:** Wall thickness directly reflects workload via Laplace's Law. Include it for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Module 3: Circulatory System & Heart**

#### **Heart Anatomy (Must-Know)**
- ☐ Heart has **4 chambers**: RA, RV, LA, LV.
- ☐ LV wall is **3× thicker** than RV wall (systemic vs. pulmonary pressure).
- ☐ **4 valves**: Tricuspid, Bicuspid (Mitral), Pulmonary Semilunar, Aortic Semilunar.
- ☐ AV valves have **chordae tendineae + papillary muscles**; semilunar valves do NOT.
- ☐ Heart wall has **3 layers**: Epicardium, **Myocardium** (thickest), Endocardium.
- ☐ Pericardium = Fibrous (outer) + Serous (parietal + visceral/epicardium).
- ☐ **S1 "lub"** = AV valve closure; **S2 "dub"** = Semilunar valve closure.

#### **Circulatory Loops (Must-Know)**
- ☐ **Systemic circulation:** LV → Aorta → Tissues → Vena cava → RA. Pressure: **120/80 mmHg**.
- ☐ **Pulmonary circulation:** RV → Pulmonary artery → Lungs → Pulmonary vein → LA. Pressure: **25/8 mmHg**.
- ☐ Pulmonary arteries carry **deoxygenated** blood; pulmonary veins carry **oxygenated** blood (naming follows direction, not O₂).
- ☐ Pulmonary resistance ≈ 1/6 of systemic resistance.

#### **Electrical Conduction (Must-Know)**
- ☐ Pathway: **SA Node → Internodal tracts → AV Node → Bundle of His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers → Ventricular Myocardium**.
- ☐ **SA Node = primary pacemaker** (70–80 bpm); AV Node = backup (40–60); Purkinje = last resort (20–40).
- ☐ **AV nodal delay = 0.1 s** — critical for sequential contraction.
- ☐ Contraction is **apex-to-base** for efficient ejection.
- ☐ Autorhythmic cells: NO fast Na⁺ channels in SA node; depolarization via **"funny" Na⁺ channels + Ca²⁺ influx**.

#### **Cardiac Action Potential (Must-Know)**
- ☐ **5 phases** in ventricular myocytes: 0, 1, 2 (plateau), 3, 4.
- ☐ Phase 0 = **Na⁺ influx**; Phase 2 = **Ca²⁺ influx (plateau)**; Phase 3 = **K⁺ efflux**; Phase 4 = **resting −90 mV**.
- ☐ **Plateau (Phase 2)** is unique to cardiac muscle — produces long absolute refractory period.
- ☐ Absolute refractory period ≈ **200–250 ms** → prevents tetany.

#### **ECG (Must-Know)**
- ☐ **P wave** = Atrial depolarization (≈ 0.08 s).
- ☐ **PR interval** = 0.12–0.20 s (AV delay included).
- ☐ **QRS complex** = Ventricular depolarization (≈ 0.08–0.10 s); atrial repolarization hidden within.
- ☐ **ST segment** = isoelectric (all ventricles depolarized).
- ☐ **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization (≈ 0.16 s).
- ☐ **QT interval** = 0.32–0.44 s.
- ☐ **HR = 300 / (number of large boxes between R-R)**.

#### **Key Formulas (Must-Know)**
- ☐ $CO = HR \times SV$ (L/min, normal ≈ 5).
- ☐ $SV = EDV - ESV$ (mL/beat, normal ≈ 70).
- ☐ $EF = SV/EDV \times 100\%$ (%, normal 55–70).
- ☐ $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ (mmHg).
- ☐ $BP = CO \times SVR$ (mmHg).
- ☐ $CO = \dot{V}O_2 / (CaO_2 - CvO_2)$ (Fick's principle).
- ☐ $R = 8\eta L / (\pi r^4)$ (Poiseuille — 4th power radius dependence).
- ☐ $T = P \cdot r / (2h)$ (Laplace's Law).

#### **Cardiac Cycle (Must-Know)**
- ☐ Atrial systole (0.1 s) → Ventricular systole (0.3 s) → Ventricular diastole (0.4 s) = 0.83 s at 72 bpm.
- ☐ EDV ≈ 120 mL; ESV ≈ 50 mL; SV ≈ 70 mL.
- ☐ During ventricular systole: isovolumetric contraction → ejection.

#### **Biomedical Engineering Connect (Must-Know)**
- ☐ **ECG machine** = surface electrodes + amplifier + display.
- ☐ **Pacemaker** = implantable device for SA node failure; demand mode senses intrinsic activity.
- ☐ **Defibrillator** = high-energy shock (200–360 J) to depolarize entire myocardium.
- ☐ **Heart-lung machine** = extracorporeal CPB used in cardiac surgery.
- ☐ **Fick's principle** is used clinically with thermodilution to measure CO in ICU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Rhythmic Cells - Cardiac Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auto Rhythmic Cells - Cardiac Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Auto Rhythmic (Pacemaker) Cells?

**Auto rhythmic cells** (also called **pacemaker cells** or **autorhythmic cells**) are specialized cardiac muscle cells that possess the unique ability to spontaneously and rhythmically generate electrical impulses (action potentials) without any external neural or hormonal stimulation. They are the natural **biogenerators** of the heartbeat, ensuring the heart beats continuously from embryonic development through the last moment of life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Auto rhythmic cells are non-contractile cardiac cells characterized by the absence of a stable resting membrane potential. Instead, they exhibit a continuously drifting membrane voltage known as the Pacemaker Potential, which gradually depolarizes to threshold, triggering a self-generated action potential responsible for the rhythmic contraction of the heart.*

### 1.2 Key Anatomical Locations (The Cardiac Conduction System)

The auto rhythmic cells are concentrated in the specialized **Cardiac Conduction System (CCS)**, which directs the flow of electrical activity through the heart in a precise sequence:

| Component | Location | Intrinsic Rate (beats/min) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SA Node** (Sino-Atrial Node) | Right atrium near SVC opening | **70–75** | Primary Pacemaker |
| **AV Node** (Atrio-Ventricular Node) | Base of right atrium | 40–60 | Delay & Relay |
| **AV Bundle (Bundle of His)** | Interventricular septum | 40–60 | Conduction Highway |
| **Bundle Branches (Left & Right)** | Septum → Apex | 30–40 | Distribute impulse |
| **Purkinje Fibers** | Ventricular myocardium | 20–30 | Final trigger to ventricles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Pacemaking:** The cell group with the **fastest intrinsic rate** (SA Node) dominates. This is called **"Overdrive Suppression"** — faster pacemakers reset slower ones, ensuring a unified rhythm.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dripping Tap & the Bucket Brigade"

Imagine a city water distribution system:

- **SA Node = The Main Pump House** that turns on automatically every second, pushing water into the system.
- **AV Node = A Sluice Gate** that momentarily holds back the water, then releases it — this delay lets the "upper tanks" (atria) fill before the "lower tanks" (ventricles) receive water.
- **Purkinje Fibers = The Final Sprinkler Network** that spreads water evenly to the entire field (ventricles) for uniform contraction.

If the main pump fails, the sluice gate (AV Node) can act as a backup pump at a slower rate — this is why patients with SA-node failure (Sick Sinus Syndrome) can survive because the heart has **redundant pacemaker capacity**.

### 1.4 Why Are Auto Rhythmic Cells Critical for Biomedical Engineers?

- **Cardiac Pacemakers (Implantable Devices):** These electronic devices replace or override malfunctioning SA/AV nodes by delivering precisely timed electrical pulses (typically 0.5–7.5 V, 0.1–1.5 ms duration).
- **ECG/Defibrillators:** Understanding the cellular origin of ECG waves (P, QRS, T) requires knowledge of pacemaker potentials.
- **Drug Design & Bioelectric Modeling:** Hodgkin-Huxley-type models of pacemaker cells drive the simulation of arrhythmias.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Resting threshold of SA Node:** approximately **−40 mV**
- **Maximum diastolic potential (MDP) of SA Node:** approximately **−60 mV**
- **Peak overshoot of SA Node AP:** approximately **+10 mV**
- **Intrinsic SA Node firing rate:** approximately **100 beats/min** (in vitro without vagal tone) but **~70 beats/min** at rest in humans due to vagal dominance.
- **Av Node conduction delay:** approximately **100 ms** (0.1 s) — vital for atrial-ventricular sequencing.
- **Heart rate range:** **60–100 beats/min** is the KTU-defined normal sinus rhythm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** The SA Node is called the **"Pacemaker of the Heart"** because it has the **fastest rate of spontaneous depolarization** among all cardiac cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phases of the Cardiac Pacemaker Action Potential

Unlike skeletal muscle or contractile cardiac cells, the auto rhythmic cell action potential has **no stable resting phase** and no true plateau. It is conventionally divided into **4 phases**, with Phase 4 being the unique defining feature.

### Phase 4 — The Pacemaker Potential (Slow Diastolic Depolarization)

This is the **signature phase** of auto rhythmic cells. After repolarization, the membrane potential does **not** return to a stable negative resting value. Instead, it **spontaneously drifts toward threshold** due to a net inward (depolarizing) current.

**Key ionic players in Phase 4:**

1. **Funny Current (I_f) — "If" or "HCN channels"**
   - Activated by **hyperpolarization** (unusual — most channels activate on depolarization).
   - Carries **mixed Na⁺ and K⁺** inward (Na⁺ dominates → depolarization).
   - Activated at membrane potentials more negative than **−40 mV**.
   - Blocked by **Cesium (Cs⁺)** — used experimentally to identify the current.
   - Encoded by **HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated)** genes.

2. **T-type Calcium Channels (Caᵥ3.1, Caᵥ3.2)**
   - Activated when membrane reaches approximately **−50 mV**.
   - Carry **inward Ca²⁺** current → further depolarization.
   - Inactivated by the time threshold is reached.

3. **Decreasing K⁺ Outflow**
   - K⁺ efflux channels (delayed rectifier) gradually **close** during diastole.
   - Reduced K⁺ exit → less hyperpolarizing influence → net depolarization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net effect of Phase 4:** The inward currents (I_f + I_Ca-T) progressively exceed the outward K⁺ current, producing a **gradual, self-sustaining depolarization** that brings the cell to threshold (~ −40 mV).

### Phase 0 — Depolarization (Upstroke)

- Once threshold is reached, **L-type Ca²⁺ channels (Caᵥ1.2)** open.
- These are **slow, long-lasting** voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels.
- The **Ca²⁺ influx** drives rapid depolarization toward **+10 mV**.
- Note: This is **fundamentally different** from neurons/skeletal muscle, which use fast **Na⁺ channels** for Phase 0.
- The slow Ca²⁺-mediated upstroke (~ **2–7 V/s** vs. 1000 V/s for Na⁺) explains the **slow conduction velocity** in nodal tissue (0.05 m/s in AV node vs. 4 m/s in Purkinje).

### Phase 3 — Repolarization

- **L-type Ca²⁺ channels inactivate.**
- **Delayed Rectifier K⁺ channels (I_K)** open → K⁺ efflux.
- Membrane potential returns toward the Maximum Diastolic Potential (~ −60 mV).
- This phase is **NOT preceded by a Phase 1 or Phase 2 plateau** — nodal cells lack the plateau.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY DIFFERENCE FROM CONTRACTILE CELLS:**
> Contractile ventricular myocytes show **5 phases** (0, 1, 2 plateau, 3, 4 stable resting). Pacemaker cells show only **3 effective phases** (4, 0, 3) with NO plateau and NO stable resting state.

### Phase Summary Table

| Phase | Name | Dominant Ionic Event | Channel | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **4** | Pacemaker Potential | Funny current (Na⁺ in) + T-type Ca²⁺ in + K⁺ out ↓ | HCN, Caᵥ3.x, I_K closing | Slow depolarization |
| **0** | Depolarization | L-type Ca²⁺ influx | Caᵥ1.2 | Rapid upstroke |
| **3** | Repolarization | K⁺ efflux | I_K (delayed rectifier) | Return to MDP |

## 2.2 The Slope of Phase 4 — The Master Variable of Heart Rate

The **steepness (slope)** of the pacemaker potential is the single most important determinant of heart rate. Anything that makes the slope **steeper** → reaches threshold faster → **higher heart rate (tachycardia)**. Anything that makes it **shallower** → reaches threshold slower → **lower heart rate (bradycardia)**.

### Autonomic Modulation

| Parameter | Sympathetic (↑ HR) | Parasympathetic (↓ HR) |
|---|---|---|
| Neurotransmitter | Norepinephrine | Acetylcholine (ACh) |
| Receptor | β₁-adrenergic | Muscarinic (M₂) |
| Second Messenger | ↑ cAMP | ↓ cAMP (via Gi) |
| Effect on I_f | **Increases** (more depolarizing current) | **Decreases** (less depolarizing current) |
| Effect on I_Ca-L | Increases | Decreases |
| Effect on I_K | Decreases K⁺ exit | Increases K⁺ exit (via I_KACh) |
| Net Slope of Phase 4 | **Steeper** | **Shallower** |
| Heart Rate | **Increases** | **Decreases** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vagal Tone Dominance:** Even at rest, the parasympathetic vagus nerve fires continuously, keeping HR at ~70 bpm. If vagal input is blocked (atropine), HR rises to ~100 bpm (intrinsic SA rate).

## 2.3 Engineering Relevance — The Hodgkin-Huxley Pacemaker Model

For a biomedical engineer, the pacemaker cell is modeled by coupled differential equations describing ionic currents. The **simplified Noble (1962) model** captures the essence:

$$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -I_{ionic} = -(I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Ca} + I_{leak})$$

Where:
- $C_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit area (~ **1 μF/cm²**)
- $V$ = membrane potential
- $I_{ionic}$ = sum of ionic currents per unit area

The **modern Luo-Rudy model** (1991) for ventricular cells and the **Fabbri-Fantini-Pellegrini model** for SA node cells are the KTU-relevant references for cardiac electrophysiology simulation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars use $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression / Value | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membrane Time Constant | $\tau = R_m \cdot C_m$ | seconds (s) | Time for V to reach 63% of steady state |
| 2 | Conduction Velocity (AV node) | $v \approx 0.05$ | m/s | Slow conduction = protective delay |
| 3 | Conduction Velocity (Purkinje) | $v \approx 4.0$ | m/s | Fast spread for coordinated contraction |
| 4 | SA Node Intrinsic Rate (no vagal tone) | $f_0 \approx 100$ | beats/min | Maximum natural pacemaker rate |
| 5 | Resting Heart Rate (with vagal tone) | $f \approx 70$ | beats/min | Normal clinical value |
| 6 | Maximum Diastolic Potential (MDP) | $V_{MDP} \approx -60$ | mV | Most negative value during cycle |
| 7 | Threshold Potential (SA Node) | $V_{th} \approx -40$ | mV | Triggers Phase 0 |
| 8 | Peak Overshoot | $V_{peak} \approx +10$ | mV | Maximum positive value |
| 9 | AV Nodal Delay | $\Delta t_{AV} \approx 100$ | ms | Critical for AV sequencing |
| 10 | Cycle Length (Rest) | $T = \frac{60}{HR}$ | seconds | $\approx 0.857$ s at 70 bpm |
| 11 | Nernst Equation (K⁺) | $E_K = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}$ | mV | $E_K \approx -90$ mV at body temp |
| 12 | Nernst Equation (Ca²⁺) | $E_{Ca} = \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i}$ | mV | $E_{Ca} \approx +120$ mV |
| 13 | Pacemaker Slope Effect on Rate | $\frac{dV}{dt}\bigg\vert_4 \uparrow \Rightarrow f \uparrow$ | qualitative | Steeper slope → faster HR |
| 14 | Phase 0 Upstroke Velocity (SA Node) | $\frac{dV}{dt}\bigg\vert_0 \approx 2$ to $7$ | V/s | Slow compared to Na⁺-driven APs |

## 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

1. **Implantable Pacemakers (IPG — Implantable Pulse Generator):**
   - Sense intrinsic atrial/ventricular activity.
   - When natural rate falls below the lower rate limit (LRL, typically **40–60 bpm**), deliver a pacing pulse of **2.5–5 V, 0.4–0.5 ms** through a lead.
   - Modern pacemakers use **rate-responsive** algorithms (accelerometers, minute ventilation sensors) that mimic the autonomic nervous system — effectively **replacing the SA node electronically**.

2. **ECG Interpretation:**
   - The **P wave** = SA node firing + atrial depolarization.
   - The **PR interval** (~ 120–200 ms) = AV nodal delay.
   - The **QRS complex** = ventricular depolarization via His-Purkinje system.
   - Pathology: Absent P wave → SA node dysfunction; prolonged PR → AV block.

3. **Defibrillators (ICD):**
   - Deliver high-energy shocks (up to **360 J** biphasic) to reset the entire myocardial electrical activity, then allow the SA node to re-establish normal rhythm.

4. **Antiarrhythmic Drug Classes:**
   - **Class II (β-blockers):** Reduce sympathetic slope of Phase 4 → slow HR.
   - **Class IV (Ca²⁺ channel blockers — verapamil, diltiazem):** Block L-type Ca²⁺ in Phase 0 of AV node → slow conduction.
   - **Class III (Amiodarone):** Block K⁺ channels → prolong repolarization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Cycle Length vs. Phase 4 Slope

The cycle length $T$ of a pacemaker cell is the time from one threshold crossing to the next. If we approximate Phase 4 as a **linear ramp** (a standard biomedical engineering assumption), we can derive $T$ analytically.

### Step 1 — Define the Boundary Conditions of the Ramp

Let:
- $V_{MDP}$ = Maximum Diastolic Potential (start of ramp)
- $V_{th}$ = Threshold potential (end of ramp)
- $S$ = Slope of the ramp (i.e., $\frac{dV}{dt}$ during Phase 4)

### Step 2 — Express the Voltage Excursion

The total voltage change required during Phase 4:

$$\Delta V = V_{th} - V_{MDP}$$

For the SA Node:

$$\Delta V = (-40) - (-60) = +20 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Phase 4 Duration

The time spent in Phase 4 is the voltage change divided by the slope:

$$T_4 = \frac{\Delta V}{S} = \frac{V_{th} - V_{MDP}}{S}$$

### Step 4 — Account for the Time Spent in Phases 0 and 3

Let $T_0$ be the duration of Phase 0 (depolarization) and $T_3$ the duration of Phase 3 (repolarization). The total cycle length:

$$T_{cycle} = T_4 + T_0 + T_3$$

### Step 5 — Apply SA Node Typical Values

For a resting human SA node, $T_0 \approx 30$ ms, $T_3 \approx 100$ ms, $\Delta V = 20$ mV.

If HR = 70 bpm, then:

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{70} = 0.857 \text{ s} = 857 \text{ ms}$$

Therefore:

$$T_4 = 857 - 30 - 100 = 727 \text{ ms}$$

And the slope:

$$S = \frac{20 \text{ mV}}{727 \text{ ms}} \approx 0.0275 \text{ mV/ms} = 27.5 \text{ μV/ms}$$

### Step 6 — Effect of Sympathetic Stimulation

Under sympathetic activation, suppose the slope doubles ($S' = 2S = 0.055$ mV/ms). Then:

$$T_4' = \frac{20}{0.055} \approx 364 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle}' = 364 + 30 + 100 = 494 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow HR' = \frac{60000}{494} \approx 121 \text{ bpm}$$

This corresponds to **sympathetic tachycardia** — exercise response.

### Step 7 — Effect of Vagal Stimulation

Under vagal dominance, slope might drop to $S'' = 0.015$ mV/ms:

$$T_4'' = \frac{20}{0.015} \approx 1333 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle}'' = 1333 + 30 + 100 = 1463 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow HR'' = \frac{60000}{1463} \approx 41 \text{ bpm}$$

This corresponds to **vagal bradycardia** — the rest-and-digest response.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 1:1 inverse relationship between slope $S$ and heart rate is the **single most tested relationship** in KTU exams for this topic. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Nernst Equation for K⁺ at 37°C

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential for an ion. For K⁺:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute the Universal Constants

- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 310$ K (body temperature, 37°C)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $z = +1$ for K⁺

### Step 2 — Convert Ln to Log₁₀

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_K = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Constant at 37°C

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 310}{1 \cdot 96{,}485} = \frac{5954}{96485} \approx 0.0617$$

So at body temperature:

$$E_K \approx 61.7 \cdot \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i} \text{ mV}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Typical K⁺ Concentrations

$[K^+]_i = 150$ mM, $[K^+]_o = 4.5$ mM:

$$E_K = 61.7 \cdot \log_{10} \frac{4.5}{150} = 61.7 \cdot \log_{10}(0.03) = 61.7 \cdot (-1.523) \approx -94 \text{ mV}$$

This is the **Nernst potential for K⁺** — a critical reference for understanding why the membrane wants to sit near −90 mV.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The GHK (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz) Voltage

When multiple ions are permeant, the resting membrane potential is given by the **GHK equation**:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

Typical permeabilities in cardiac nodal cells at MDP: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1.0 : 0.05 : 0.45$. This is why the **MDP is ~−60 mV** (closer to $E_K$ but shifted by Na⁺ leak).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SA Node vs. AV Node vs. Purkinje

| Parameter | SA Node | AV Node | Purkinje Fiber | Ventricular Contractile |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Diastolic Potential** | −60 mV | −65 mV | −90 mV | −90 mV |
| **Threshold Potential** | −40 mV | −40 mV | −70 mV | −65 mV |
| **Phase 0 Upstroke Ion** | Ca²⁺ (slow) | Ca²⁺ (slow) | Na⁺ (fast) | Na⁺ (fast) |
| **Phase 0 Velocity (dV/dt)** | 2–7 V/s | 5–15 V/s | 100–500 V/s | 200–400 V/s |
| **Conduction Velocity** | 0.05 m/s | 0.05 m/s | 2–4 m/s | 0.3–1 m/s |
| **Intrinsic Rate** | 70–100 bpm | 40–60 bpm | 20–40 bpm | None (does not fire) |
| **Phase 4 Slope (Rest)** | Steep | Moderate | Shallow | Flat (no auto) |
| **Plateau (Phase 2)?** | NO | NO | NO | YES |
| **Stable Resting V?** | NO | NO | NO | YES |
| **Apparent Function** | Initiates | Delays & relays | Rapid distribution | Contracts |

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step — Generation of the Pacemaker Potential

Let us trace the membrane voltage from one beat to the next in an SA node cell:

**Step 1 — End of previous repolarization (start of new cycle):** Membrane at $V_{MDP} \approx -60$ mV. HCN channels (I_f) start activating because V is hyperpolarized.

**Step 2 — Early Phase 4 (V from −60 to −55 mV):**
- I_f becomes significant, carrying Na⁺ in.
- K⁺ efflux via I_K is decreasing.
- Net inward current ≈ 1–2 pA/pF.
- dV/dt ≈ 0.02 mV/ms.

**Step 3 — Mid Phase 4 (V from −55 to −50 mV):**
- T-type Ca²⁺ channels begin to activate.
- I_f still active but approaching saturation.
- Net inward current rises to ≈ 3–4 pA/pF.
- dV/dt ≈ 0.05 mV/ms.

**Step 4 — Late Phase 4 (V from −50 to −40 mV):**
- T-type Ca²⁺ channels are now strongly activated.
- I_f is saturated.
- K⁺ efflux continues to drop.
- Net inward current ≈ 5–8 pA/pF.
- dV/dt accelerates — the curve becomes noticeably steeper.

**Step 5 — Threshold reached (V ≈ −40 mV):**
- L-type Ca²⁺ channels open abruptly.
- Phase 0 begins: rapid Ca²⁺-driven depolarization.

**Step 6 — Phase 0 (V from −40 to +10 mV):**
- Inward Ca²⁺ current peaks.
- Cell reaches overshoot.

**Step 7 — Phase 3 (V from +10 to −60 mV):**
- L-type Ca²⁺ channels inactivate.
- Delayed rectifier K⁺ channels open.
- K⁺ efflux repolarizes the cell.
- Cycle restarts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Pearl:** A pacemaker cell can be modeled as a **relaxation oscillator**. The slow charging (Phase 4) followed by a rapid discharge (Phase 0–3) is mathematically identical to a **neural integrate-and-fire model** or an **astable multivibrator circuit** in electronics.

## 3.6 Mathematical Model of the Pacemaker (van der Pol–type oscillator)

A simplified 2-variable model used in cardiac electrophysiology:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{1}{C_m}(I_{ionic}(V, w) + I_{stim})$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \frac{(w_\infty(V) - w)}{\tau_w(V)}$$

Where $w$ is a **recovery variable** (representing K⁺ channel activation state). This is the **FitzHugh-Nagumo** analogue for cardiac tissue — directly useful for biomedical engineers designing arrhythmia simulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pacemaker Action Potential — Phases and Ion Channels

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start of Cycle: V at MDP -60 mV] --> B[Phase 4 Pacemaker Potential]
    B --> B1[Funny Current I_f: Na Influx]
    B --> B2[T-type Ca Channel: Ca Influx]
    B --> B3[K Efflux Decreasing]
    B1 --> C[Threshold Reached -40 mV]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Phase 0 Depolarization]
    D --> D1[L-type Ca Channels Open]
    D1 --> E[Overshoot +10 mV]
    E --> F[Phase 3 Repolarization]
    F --> F1[Ca Channels Inactivate]
    F --> F2[K Efflux via Delayed Rectifier]
    F1 --> A
    F2 --> A
```

## 4.2 Cardiac Conduction System — Sequence of Impulse Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SA[SA Node Right Atrium 70 to 100 bpm] -->|Internodal Pathways| AV[AV Node Base of Right Atrium]
    AV -->|Bundle of His Delay 100 ms| BH[Bundle of His Interventricular Septum]
    BH --> LBB[Left Bundle Branch]
    BH --> RBB[Right Bundle Branch]
    LBB --> PLF[Purkinje Fibers Left Ventricle]
    RBB --> PRF[Purkinje Fibers Right Ventricle]
    PLF --> VM1[Ventricular Myocardium Contracts]
    PRF --> VM2[Ventricular Myocardium Contracts]
```

## 4.3 Autonomic Modulation of Phase 4 Slope

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SympatheticPathway
        S1[Sympathetic Nerve] --> S2[Norepinephrine]
        S2 --> S3[Beta-1 Adrenergic Receptor]
        S3 --> S4[Increase cAMP]
        S4 --> S5[Increase I_f and I_Ca]
        S5 --> S6[Steeper Phase 4 Slope]
        S6 --> S7[Higher Heart Rate]
    end
    subgraph ParasympatheticPathway
        P1[Parasympathetic Vagus Nerve] --> P2[Acetylcholine]
        P2 --> P3[Muscarinic M2 Receptor]
        P3 --> P4[Decrease cAMP Increase K Efflux]
        P4 --> P5[Shallower Phase 4 Slope]
        P5 --> P6[Lower Heart Rate]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pacemaker Cell to Heartbeat

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CellularLevel
        C1[Pacemaker Cell SA Node] --> C2[Pacemaker Potential Generation]
        C2 --> C3[Threshold Detection]
        C3 --> C4[Action Potential Firing]
    end
    subgraph TissueLevel
        T1[Action Potential Propagates] --> T2[Atrial Muscle Depolarization]
        T2 --> T3[AV Nodal Delay]
        T3 --> T4[Ventricular Conduction System]
        T4 --> T5[Ventricular Myocyte Depolarization]
    end
    subgraph OrganLevel
        O1[Atrial Systole Blood into Ventricles] --> O2[Ventricular Systole Blood to Lungs and Body]
        O2 --> O3[Diastole Refill]
        O3 --> O4[Next Cycle Initiated by SA Node]
    end
    C4 --> T1
    T5 --> O1
    O4 --> C1
```

## 4.5 Comparison: Pacemaker vs. Contractile Action Potential

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PacemakerCell
        P1[Phase 4: Gradual Depolarization Pacemaker Potential] --> P2[Phase 0: Slow Ca Driven Upstroke]
        P2 --> P3[Phase 3: K Efflux Repolarization]
        P3 --> P1
    end
    subgraph ContractileCell
        K1[Phase 4: Stable Resting -90 mV] --> K2[Phase 0: Fast Na Upstroke]
        K2 --> K3[Phase 1: Brief Na Inactivation]
        K3 --> K4[Phase 2: Ca Plateau]
        K4 --> K5[Phase 3: K Repolarization]
        K5 --> K1
    end
```

## 4.6 Pacemaker Waveform — Voltage vs. Time Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[T0 MDP -60 mV] --> T1[T0 plus 700 ms Threshold -40 mV]
    T1 --> T2[T0 plus 730 ms Overshoot plus 10 mV]
    T2 --> T3[T0 plus 830 ms Return to MDP -60 mV]
    T3 --> T0
```

| Time Point | Membrane Voltage | Phase | Key Event |
|---|---|---|---|
| T0 | −60 mV | 4 start | MDP — I_f begins |
| T0 + 700 ms | −40 mV | 4 end | Threshold — L-type Ca opens |
| T0 + 730 ms | +10 mV | 0 peak | Overshoot |
| T0 + 830 ms | −60 mV | 3 end | Cycle restart |

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — July 2024]
**Define auto rhythmic cells. Why is the SA node called the pacemaker of the heart?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Auto rhythmic cells are specialized cardiac muscle cells capable of generating rhythmic action potentials spontaneously without external stimulation, due to their unstable resting membrane potential called the pacemaker potential (1 mark).*

*The SA node is called the pacemaker of the heart because:*
- *It possesses the fastest intrinsic rate of spontaneous depolarization (~70–100 bpm) among all cardiac cells (1 mark).*
- *Through overdrive suppression, the SA node dominates and resets slower pacemakers (AV node, Purkinje), making it the origin of the normal sinus rhythm (1 mark).*

**Total: 3 marks**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the "funny current" (I_f) in the generation of the pacemaker potential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*The funny current I_f is an inward current carried primarily by Na ions through HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channels during Phase 4 of the pacemaker action potential (1 mark).*

*It is unusual because it is activated by hyperpolarization (more negative than −40 mV), rather than by depolarization. This current is the primary driver of the slow diastolic depolarization that brings the SA node from its Maximum Diastolic Potential (~−60 mV) to threshold (~−40 mV) (1 mark).*

*The I_f current is modulated by autonomic input: sympathetic stimulation (cAMP) increases I_f (steeper slope → higher HR), while parasympathetic (ACh) decreases I_f (shallower slope → lower HR) (1 mark).*

**Total: 3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the ionic basis and phases of the action potential in SA node auto rhythmic cells. Compare it with the ventricular contractile cell action potential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the autonomic nervous system modulates the heart rate through changes in the slope of the pacemaker potential. Add a note on the engineering significance of this modulation in implantable pacemakers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**1. Pacemaker Action Potential Phases (3 marks):**

The SA node action potential has three effective phases:

- **Phase 4 — Pacemaker Potential (Slow Diastolic Depolarization):** The membrane spontaneously depolarizes from the Maximum Diastolic Potential (~−60 mV) toward threshold (~−40 mV). This is driven by:
  - The **funny current (I_f)**: inward Na⁺ via HCN channels, activated by hyperpolarization (1 mark).
  - **T-type Ca²⁺ channels**: inward Ca²⁺ current, activated near −50 mV (0.5 marks).
  - **Decreasing K⁺ efflux** via delayed rectifier channels (0.5 marks).

- **Phase 0 — Depolarization (Upstroke):** At threshold, L-type Ca²⁺ channels (Caᵥ1.2) open, causing rapid Ca²⁺-driven depolarization to overshoot (~+10 mV). The upstroke is slow (2–7 V/s) compared to Na⁺-driven APs (0.5 marks).

- **Phase 3 — Repolarization:** L-type Ca²⁺ channels inactivate, and delayed rectifier K⁺ channels open, repolarizing the cell back to MDP (0.5 marks).

**2. Comparison with Ventricular Contractile Cell (4 marks):**

| Feature | SA Node | Ventricular Myocyte |
|---|---|---|
| Resting V | None (unstable MDP) | Stable at −90 mV |
| Phase 0 Ion | Ca²⁺ (slow) | Na⁺ (fast) |
| Conduction Velocity | 0.05 m/s | 0.3–1 m/s |
| Plateau (Phase 2) | Absent | Present (Ca²⁺-driven) |
| Function | Initiate rhythm | Contract |

Ventricular cells have a **stable resting potential** maintained by high K⁺ permeability, exhibit a **fast Na⁺-driven upstroke** and a **Ca²⁺-dependent plateau** (Phase 2) that sustains depolarization for ~250 ms — essential for long, powerful contractions (1 mark).

Pacemaker cells **lack Phase 1 and Phase 2** because they have a negligible density of fast Na⁺ channels and minimal inward Ca²⁺ balance during the action potential — making them non-contractile and specialized for rhythmicity (1 mark).

**Total for Part A(a): 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**1. Sympathetic Modulation — Increasing Heart Rate (3 marks):**

- Norepinephrine released from sympathetic nerve terminals binds **β₁-adrenergic receptors** on SA node cells.
- This activates **Gs protein → ↑ adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP**.
- cAMP directly binds HCN channels, **increasing I_f** (funny current).
- cAMP-dependent PKA also **phosphorylates and increases L-type Ca²⁺ channels**, increasing Phase 0 inward current.
- The net effect is a **steeper Phase 4 slope** → threshold is reached faster → **shorter cycle length → higher heart rate (tachycardia)** (1 mark for mechanism + 1 mark for outcome).

**2. Parasympathetic Modulation — Decreasing Heart Rate (2 marks):**

- ACh released from vagus nerve binds **M₂ muscarinic receptors**.
- M₂ activates **Gi protein → ↓ cAMP** (opposite of sympathetic).
- Gi also opens a special G-protein-coupled K⁺ channel (**I_KACh**) → **increased K⁺ efflux** → membrane hyperpolarizes.
- Result: **shallower Phase 4 slope** → longer time to threshold → **slower heart rate (bradycardia)** (1 mark each for mechanism and outcome).

**3. Engineering Significance — Implantable Pacemakers (2 marks):**

Modern pacemakers (e.g., Medtronic, Boston Scientific devices) use **rate-responsive pacing algorithms** to mimic autonomic control (1 mark):
- **Accelerometer-based sensors** detect body movement → increase pacing rate (mimicking sympathetic input).
- **Minute ventilation sensors** measure respiratory rate → adjust rate to metabolic demand.
- **Closed-loop systems** (e.g., Biotronik CLS) use **right ventricular impedance** to estimate cardiac contractility and adjust rate in real-time.
- The pacemaker essentially becomes an **artificial SA node** that mathematically replicates the Phase 4 slope changes seen in the natural pacemaker (1 mark).

**Total for Part A(b): 7 marks**

**GRAND TOTAL FOR QUESTION A: 14 marks**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the four phases of the ventricular contractile cell action potential and the ionic basis of each phase. Explain why pacemaker cells do not exhibit a stable Phase 4 resting potential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the AV nodal delay — its ionic basis, duration, and physiological significance. Explain how this delay is reflected in the ECG and what happens if it is pathologically prolonged. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**1. Ventricular Action Potential Phases (5 marks):**

| Phase | Name | Ionic Basis | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Rapid Depolarization | Fast Na⁺ influx via voltage-gated Na⁺ channels | 1–2 ms |
| **1** | Initial Repolarization | Na⁺ channel inactivation + transient K⁺ efflux (I_to) | 10–20 ms |
| **2** | Plateau | Ca²⁺ influx via L-type Ca²⁺ channels balanced by K⁺ efflux | 100–200 ms |
| **3** | Repolarization | K⁺ efflux via delayed rectifier (I_Kr, I_Ks); Ca²⁺ channels close | 50–100 ms |
| **4** | Resting Potential | High K⁺ permeability → V_m ≈ E_K ≈ −90 mV | Sustained |

**Phase 0 (1 mark):** Rapid depolarization driven by regenerative Na⁺ influx. Threshold ~ −65 mV, overshoot ~ +30 mV, dV/dt up to 400 V/s.

**Phase 1 (0.5 mark):** Brief notch due to Na⁺ channel inactivation and a transient outward K⁺ current (I_to).

**Phase 2 (1.5 marks):** The **plateau** — a unique feature of cardiac muscle. L-type Ca²⁺ channels (Caᵥ1.2) open and carry sustained inward Ca²⁺ current. This inward Ca²⁺ current is balanced by outward K⁺ current (I_K), producing a near-zero net current and a flat plateau. The plateau **prevents tetanus** (a critical property of cardiac muscle) and provides sustained Ca²⁺ entry for **calcium-induced calcium release (CICR)** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, triggering contraction.

**Phase 3 (1 mark):** Repolarization occurs as L-type Ca²⁺ channels inactivate and K⁺ efflux dominates. The delayed rectifiers I_Kr (rapid) and I_Ks (slow) drive the membrane back to ~ −90 mV.

**Phase 4 (1 mark):** Stable resting potential maintained by high K⁺ permeability (inward rectifier K⁺ channels, I_K1) and the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle).

**2. Why Pacemaker Cells Lack Stable Phase 4 (2 marks):**

Pacemaker cells have a **low density of I_K1 inward rectifier channels**, which is the primary channel responsible for clamping the membrane near $E_K$ in ventricular myocytes (1 mark). Additionally, the persistent activation of the **funny current I_f** at hyperpolarized potentials creates a continuous depolarizing influence. As a result, the membrane cannot stabilize at −90 mV — instead it drifts upward from the MDP (~ −60 mV) due to the net imbalance of inward (I_f, I_Ca-T) and outward (I_K) currents (1 mark).

**Total for Part B(a): 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**1. Ionic Basis of AV Nodal Delay (2 marks):**

The AV node is composed of **small-diameter cells** with **fewer gap junctions** than ventricular myocytes. The Phase 0 upstroke is mediated by **slow L-type Ca²⁺ channels** rather than fast Na⁺ channels, resulting in a **low conduction velocity (~0.05 m/s)** (1 mark). The small cell diameter and sparse connexin-43 expression further slow intercellular current flow (1 mark).

**2. Duration and Physiological Significance (2 marks):**

The AV nodal delay is approximately **100 ms (0.1 second)** (0.5 marks). This delay allows the **atria to fully contract and complete ventricular filling** before the ventricles are activated. Without this delay, atrial and ventricular contractions would occur simultaneously, severely reducing cardiac output (1 mark). It also serves as a **protective filter** — limiting the rate of ventricular activation in case of rapid atrial rhythms (e.g., atrial flutter at 300 bpm → ventricular rate ~75 bpm after AV filtering) (0.5 marks).

**3. ECG Reflection (2 marks):**

The AV nodal delay is reflected in the **PR interval** of the ECG, measured from the start of the P wave (atrial depolarization) to the start of the QRS complex (ventricular depolarization) (1 mark). Normal PR interval = **120–200 ms** at a heart rate of 60–100 bpm. A PR interval > 200 ms indicates **First-Degree AV Block**. Higher degrees include:
- **Second-Degree AV Block (Mobitz I / Wenckebach):** Progressive PR prolongation until a beat is dropped.
- **Second-Degree AV Block (Mobitz II):** Constant PR with sudden dropped beats.
- **Third-Degree (Complete) AV Block:** Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS — the ventricles are driven by an escape pacemaker (junctional ~40 bpm or ventricular ~20–30 bpm) (1 mark).

**4. Clinical/Biomedical Engineering Relevance (1 mark):**

Patients with complete AV block require **implantable pacemakers** (typically **DDD mode** — dual-chamber pacing, dual-chamber sensing, dual response). The device restores AV synchrony by sensing atrial activity (P wave) and, after a programmed AV delay (mimicking the natural 100 ms), delivering a ventricular pacing pulse.

**Total for Part B(b): 7 marks**

**GRAND TOTAL FOR QUESTION B: 14 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Phase 0 ion:** Many students wrongly state that SA node Phase 0 is Na⁺-driven. It is **Ca²⁺-driven** — this is the single most common error, and it costs **2 full marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Forgetting the plateau difference:** SA node cells DO NOT have a Phase 2 plateau. Stating that they do will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong unit for conduction velocity:** Always express in **m/s**, not m/sec or cm/s.
> 4. **Forgetting HCN channel name:** HCN = **Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated** — not just "funny channel."
> 5. **In ECG questions,** the PR interval is measured from the **START of P to START of QRS**, not end-to-end.
> 6. **Sympathetic/Parasympathetic confusion:** β₁ receptors are **sympathetic**; M₂ receptors are **parasympathetic**. Mixing these will cost 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Auto Rhythmic Cells & Cardiac Action Potentials**

- ✅ **SA node** is the primary pacemaker (70–100 bpm); it is the fastest auto rhythmic cell.
- ✅ The **pacemaker potential (Phase 4)** is the **signature feature** — slow spontaneous depolarization from MDP (~−60 mV) to threshold (~−40 mV).
- ✅ **Phase 4 ionic drivers:** I_f (Na⁺ in via HCN) + I_Ca-T (Ca²⁺ in) + decreasing I_K (less K⁺ out).
- ✅ **Phase 0 ion in pacemaker cells = Ca²⁺** (NOT Na⁺) via L-type Ca²⁺ channels.
- ✅ Pacemaker cells have **NO Phase 2 plateau** and **NO stable Phase 4 resting potential**.
- ✅ **AV nodal delay ≈ 100 ms** — reflected as PR interval (120–200 ms) on ECG.
- ✅ Conduction velocities: SA/AV node ~0.05 m/s, His-Purkinje ~4 m/s, Ventricular muscle ~0.3–1 m/s.
- ✅ **Sympathetic ↑ cAMP → ↑ I_f → steeper Phase 4 → ↑ HR**.
- ✅ **Parasympathetic (Vagus) → ↓ cAMP + ↑ I_KACh → shallower Phase 4 → ↓ HR**.
- ✅ At rest, **vagal tone dominates**, keeping HR at ~70 bpm (vs. intrinsic ~100 bpm).
- ✅ **Implantable pacemakers** replace SA node function — sensing intrinsic activity, delivering 2.5–5 V, 0.4–0.5 ms pulses when needed.
- ✅ **Class II antiarrhythmics (β-blockers)** reduce HR by reducing Phase 4 slope.
- ✅ **Class IV antiarrhythmics (Ca²⁺ blockers)** slow AV node conduction by blocking Phase 0 Ca²⁺ entry.
- ✅ Nernst potential for K⁺ at 37°C ≈ **−94 mV** (using 2.303 RT/F = 61.7 mV).
- ✅ Cycle length $T = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{HR}$ — at 70 bpm, T ≈ 0.857 s.
- ✅ **Overdrive suppression** ensures the fastest pacemaker (SA) controls the rhythm.
- ✅ The pacemaker cell action potential resembles an **astable multivibrator** in electronics — useful analogy for biomedical engineering students.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT205 - BASIC ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS (ELECTRONICS AND BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cardiac Cycle (ECG)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Cardiac Cycle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardiac Cycle (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **cardiac cycle** is the complete sequence of mechanical and electrical events that occurs in the heart during one full heartbeat, encompassing the alternating phases of **contraction (systole)** and **relaxation (diastole)** of both the atria and ventricles, coordinated by precisely timed electrical impulses originating from the **SA (Sino-Atrial) node**.

For a healthy adult at a normal resting heart rate of approximately **72 beats per minute (bpm)**, the duration of one complete cardiac cycle is:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{72 \text{ beats}} \approx 0.833 \text{ seconds per beat}
$$

This brief 0.833 seconds contains a tightly choreographed series of pressure changes, volume changes, valve openings/closings, and electrical depolarizations/repolarizations.

## 1.2 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG) — KTU Standard Definition:**
> An **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive diagnostic recording that graphically represents the **time-varying electrical potentials** generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle fibers, captured via surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest, and displayed as a voltage-versus-time waveform.

The standard clinical ECG paper speed is **25 mm/s** with vertical calibration of **10 mm = 1 mV**. Therefore:
- **1 small box (1 mm)** = 0.04 seconds
- **1 large box (5 mm)** = 0.20 seconds

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making the Heart's Rhythm Intuitive

### Analogy 1: The Cardiac Cycle as a Two-Stage Water Pump
Imagine the heart as a **two-stage water pump** with two filling chambers (atria) that act as **priming reservoirs** and two powerful pumping chambers (ventricles) that eject water (blood) into a pipe network. 

- The **atria** first "prime" the pumps by squirting a small amount of water into the ventricles (atrial systole).
- Then the **ventricles** contract powerfully to push water out to the body and lungs (ventricular systole).
- Finally, everything **relaxes** and refills for the next cycle (diastole).

This continuous cycle happens roughly **once every 0.8 seconds** at rest, and faster when you exercise.

### Analogy 2: ECG as a "Stadium Wave" in the Heart
Think of the ECG as the **electrical "wave" that ripples through a stadium** (where the audience = cardiac muscle cells). When the wave passes through a section, those people stand up (depolarization = contraction). As it moves on, they sit back down (repolarization = relaxation). The ECG machine, mounted "above the stadium," records the net crowd movement at any instant — translating the wave into the familiar P-QRS-T trace.

## 1.4 Standard Clinical Metrics (Bold Highlight)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Resting Heart Rate** | **60–100 bpm** (clinical norm: 72 bpm) |
| **Cardiac Cycle Duration** | **≈ 0.8 s** at 72 bpm |
| **Atrial Systole Duration** | **≈ 0.1 s** |
| **Ventricular Systole Duration** | **≈ 0.3 s** |
| **Diastole (Total Relaxation)** | **≈ 0.4 s** |
| **Normal ECG Paper Speed** | **25 mm/s** |
| **ECG Voltage Calibration** | **10 mm = 1 mV** |
| **Cardiac Output (Rest)** | **≈ 5 L/min** |
| **Stroke Volume (Rest)** | **≈ 70 mL/beat** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Halves of the Cardiac Cycle

The cardiac cycle is structurally divided into two main mechanical phases:

### A. Systole (Contraction Phase)
- **Atrial Systole** (~0.1 s): Atria contract, pushing the final ~30% of blood into ventricles.
- **Ventricular Systole** (~0.3 s): Ventricles contract, ejecting blood into the aorta and pulmonary artery.
  - *Isovolumetric contraction* (ventricles build pressure, all valves closed)
  - *Ventricular ejection* (semilunar valves open, blood ejected)

### B. Diastole (Relaxation / Filling Phase)
- **Ventricular Diastole** (~0.5 s): Ventricles relax and refill from the atria.
  - *Isovolumetric relaxation* (all valves closed, pressure drops)
  - *Rapid filling* (AV valves open, blood rushes in)
  - *Reduced filling (diastasis)* (slow passive flow)
  - *Atrial systole* completes the filling (active topping-up)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** At a normal heart rate of 72 bpm, the heart spends **more time in diastole** (~0.5 s) than systole (~0.4 s combined). This is critical because **coronary perfusion of the left ventricle occurs primarily during diastole**. When heart rate increases (e.g., 180 bpm), diastole shortens disproportionately, which can compromise coronary blood flow — a frequently tested concept.

## 2.2 Detailed Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

| Phase | Duration | Valves State | Volume/Pressure Event | ECG Correlation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Atrial Systole** | 0.1 s | AV valves OPEN; SL valves CLOSED | Atria contract → push blood into ventricles (atrial "kick") | Follows P wave |
| **2. Isovolumetric Contraction** | ~0.05 s | All 4 valves CLOSED | Ventricular pressure rises sharply; volume constant | QRS complex (early) |
| **3. Ventricular Ejection** | ~0.25 s | SL valves OPEN; AV valves CLOSED | Blood ejected into aorta (70 mL SV) | ST segment / T wave onset |
| **4. Isovolumetric Relaxation** | ~0.08 s | All 4 valves CLOSED | Ventricular pressure falls sharply; volume constant | End of T wave |
| **5. Rapid Ventricular Filling** | ~0.11 s | AV valves OPEN; SL valves CLOSED | Blood passively rushes from atria to ventricles | T-P segment |
| **6. Reduced Filling (Diastasis)** | ~0.19 s | AV valves OPEN; SL valves CLOSED | Slow passive flow, almost in equilibrium | T-P segment |
| **7. Atrial Systole** (loop back) | 0.1 s | AV valves OPEN; SL valves CLOSED | Atrial kick completes ventricular filling | After next P wave |

## 2.3 Heart Sounds and Their Correlation

| Sound | Cause | Timing in Cycle | Frequency | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1 ("Lub")** | Closure of mitral & tricuspid (AV) valves | Beginning of ventricular systole | 25–45 Hz | 0.14 s |
| **S2 ("Dub")** | Closure of aortic & pulmonary (semilunar) valves | Beginning of ventricular diastole | 50 Hz | 0.11 s |
| **S3** (pathological if adult) | Rapid ventricular filling vibration | Early diastole | Low | Brief |
| **S4** (pathological) | Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle | Late diastole (atrial systole) | Low | Brief |

## 2.4 The ECG Waveform — Component-by-Component Analysis

### The P Wave
- **Origin:** Atrial depolarization (begins at SA node, spreads through both atria).
- **Duration:** ≤ **0.12 s** (3 small boxes); ≤ **0.11 s** is more precise clinically.
- **Amplitude:** ≤ **2.5 mm** (0.25 mV) in lead II.
- **Shape:** Smooth, rounded, upright in lead II (because depolarization travels toward the positive electrode of lead II, which is on the left leg).

### The PR Interval
- **Definition:** From the **beginning of the P wave** to the **beginning of the QRS complex**.
- **Represents:** Atrial depolarization + AV nodal conduction delay.
- **Normal Duration:** **0.12 – 0.20 s** (3–5 small boxes).
- **Significance:** Reflects the time taken for the impulse to travel from atria through the AV node to the ventricles. The **AV nodal delay (~0.1 s)** is a critical protective feature that allows complete ventricular filling before contraction.

### The QRS Complex
- **Origin:** Ventricular depolarization (and simultaneous atrial repolarization, which is hidden).
- **Components:**
  - **Q wave:** First negative deflection before any positive wave. (Septal depolarization, left-to-right.)
  - **R wave:** First positive deflection after the Q. (Main ventricular depolarization.)
  - **S wave:** Negative deflection following the R wave.
- **Normal Duration:** **0.06 – 0.10 s** (1.5–2.5 small boxes).
- **Amplitude (Lead II):** R wave typically **9–11 mm** (0.9–1.1 mV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the QRS larger than the P wave?**
> Because the ventricles have **much more muscle mass** than the atria (ventricles must pump blood to the entire body and lungs; atria only prime the ventricles). Greater mass → greater electrical vector magnitude.

### The ST Segment
- **Definition:** From the **end of the S wave (J point)** to the **beginning of the T wave**.
- **Represents:** The plateau phase (phase 2) of the ventricular action potential — all ventricular myocytes are depolarized (the "isoelectric" period).
- **Significance:** **ST segment elevation** is a hallmark of acute myocardial infarction (heart attack). **ST depression** indicates myocardial ischemia.

### The T Wave
- **Origin:** Ventricular repolarization.
- **Shape:** Asymmetric, rounded, usually upright in lead II.
- **Duration:** ~0.16 s.
- **Important:** Repolarization travels in the **opposite direction** to depolarization, yet the T wave is **upright** — this is because repolarization proceeds from the **epicardium to endocardium** (the reverse of depolarization), so the net vector points toward the positive lead.

### The QT Interval
- **Definition:** From the **beginning of the QRS complex** to the **end of the T wave**.
- **Represents:** Total time for ventricular depolarization + repolarization (i.e., the entire ventricular action potential).
- **Normal Duration:** **0.35 – 0.44 s**, but **rate-dependent** (slower at lower heart rates).
- **Corrected QT (QTc) Formula (Bazett's):**

$$
QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}
$$

where RR is the R-R interval in seconds. Normal QTc is **≤ 0.44 s (men)** or **≤ 0.46 s (women)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students incorrectly equate a long QT interval with "slow heart rate." A **prolonged QTc > 0.50 s** is a dangerous substrate for **Torsades de Pointes**, a potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia.

### The U Wave
- **Origin:** Believed to represent repolarization of the **Purkinje fibers** or papillary muscles (still debated).
- **Shape:** Small, rounded deflection following the T wave.
- **Amplitude:** < 1–2 mm; usually in V2–V4.
- **Clinical significance:** A prominent U wave may indicate **hypokalemia** (low blood potassium).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cardiac Cycle Duration** | $T = 60 / HR$ | seconds (s) | At 72 bpm → T ≈ 0.833 s |
| 2 | **Heart Rate from R-R Interval** | $HR = 60 / RR$ | beats per minute (bpm) | RR of 0.8 s → HR = 75 bpm |
| 3 | **Cardiac Output** | $CO = SV \times HR$ | L/min | At rest: 70 mL × 72 bpm ≈ 5 L/min |
| 4 | **Stroke Volume** | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | mL/beat | Normal ≈ 70 mL/beat |
| 5 | **Ejection Fraction** | $EF = SV / EDV \times 100\%$ | % | Normal: 55–70% |
| 6 | **Mean Arterial Pressure** | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | mmHg | Normal: 70–100 mmHg |
| 7 | **ECG Time per Small Box** | $1 \text{ mm} = 0.04$ s | s | 25 mm/s paper speed |
| 8 | **ECG Time per Large Box** | $5 \text{ mm} = 0.20$ s | s | 5 × 0.04 s |
| 9 | **ECG Voltage Calibration** | $10 \text{ mm} = 1$ mV | mV | Standard gain |
| 10 | **Corrected QT (Bazett)** | $QT_c = QT / \sqrt{RR}$ | s | QTc ≤ 0.44 s (men) |
| 11 | **PR Interval (Normal)** | $0.12 - 0.20$ s | s | Reflects AV conduction |
| 12 | **QRS Duration (Normal)** | $0.06 - 0.10$ s | s | Ventricular depolarization |
| 13 | **QT Interval (Normal)** | $0.35 - 0.44$ s | s | Rate-dependent |
| 14 | **Cardiac Axis (Normal)** | $-30°$ to $+100°$ | degrees | Determined from lead I and aVF |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of the ECG

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for a Biomedical Engineer (KTU 2024 Outcome-based focus):**

1. **ECG Machine Design:** Biomedical engineers design analog front-ends (instrumentation amplifiers like AD620, INA128), filters (0.5–150 Hz bandpass, 50/60 Hz notch), and ADCs (≥ 12-bit, ≥ 500 Hz sampling) to capture the ECG.
2. **Pacemaker Design:** Knowledge of the PR interval, QRS duration, and rate-responsive algorithms is essential to building demand pacemakers.
3. **Defibrillators:** Engineers design biphasic waveforms timed to the R wave to avoid the vulnerable T-wave period (which can trigger VF).
4. **Holter Monitors & Wearables:** Smart watches (Apple Watch, Fitbit) use single-lead ECG derived from photoplethysmography + dry electrodes.
5. **HRV Analysis:** For stress, sleep, and autonomic nervous system assessment, engineers compute time-domain and frequency-domain HRV metrics from the R-R intervals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cardiac Cycle Duration from Heart Rate

### Problem Setup
A patient has a heart rate of 75 bpm. Find the duration of one complete cardiac cycle and identify the time spent in systole vs. diastole.

### Step 1: Define the relationship
The duration of one cardiac cycle ($T$) is the reciprocal of the heart rate expressed in beats per second.

$$
T = \frac{1}{\text{Heart Rate (in Hz)}} = \frac{1}{HR/60}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the given values
Given: HR = 75 bpm.

$$
T = \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{75 \text{ beats/min}}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify
Divide 60 by 75:

$$
T = \frac{60}{75} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \text{ seconds per beat}
$$

### Step 4: Determine the fraction of time in systole vs. diastole
At rest, systole occupies roughly 1/3 of the cardiac cycle and diastole occupies 2/3.

$$
T_{systole} = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.8 \text{ s} \approx 0.267 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{diastole} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.8 \text{ s} \approx 0.533 \text{ s}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Total cycle:** 0.8 s
- **Systole:** ≈ 0.27 s
- **Diastole:** ≈ 0.53 s

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heart Rate from an ECG Strip (R-R Method)

### Problem Setup
On an ECG strip recorded at the standard 25 mm/s, the distance between two consecutive R waves is measured as 20 mm. Calculate the heart rate.

### Step 1: Convert distance to time
At 25 mm/s, the time interval for 20 mm is:

$$
RR \text{ interval} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Paper speed}} = \frac{20 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

### Step 2: Compute

$$
RR = \frac{20}{25} = 0.8 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3: Convert RR interval to heart rate (bpm)
Heart rate (in bpm) is calculated as:

$$
HR = \frac{60}{RR} = \frac{60}{0.8}
$$

### Step 4: Final result

$$
HR = 75 \text{ bpm}
$$

### Alternative "Large Box" Quick Method
At 25 mm/s, each large box = 0.20 s. The number of large boxes between two R waves divided into 300 gives the heart rate:

$$
HR = \frac{300}{\text{Number of large boxes between R-R}}
$$

For 4 large boxes: HR = 300 / 4 = 75 bpm ✓ (matches our derivation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: QT Interval Correction (Bazett's Formula)

### Problem Setup
A patient's ECG shows a measured QT interval of 0.40 s, and the R-R interval is 0.8 s. Calculate the corrected QT (QTc) and determine if it is normal.

### Step 1: Apply Bazett's formula

$$
QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
QT_c = \frac{0.40}{\sqrt{0.8}}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the square root

$$
\sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944
$$

### Step 4: Divide

$$
QT_c = \frac{0.40}{0.8944} = 0.447 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 5: Clinical interpretation
For an adult male, the upper limit of normal QTc is **0.44 s**. A QTc of 0.447 s is **borderline prolonged** and warrants follow-up.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume

### Problem Setup
A patient has an end-diastolic volume (EDV) of 120 mL and an end-systolic volume (ESV) of 50 mL. Heart rate is 70 bpm. Calculate stroke volume, cardiac output, and ejection fraction.

### Step 1: Stroke Volume

$$
SV = EDV - ESV
$$

$$
SV = 120 \text{ mL} - 50 \text{ mL} = 70 \text{ mL/beat}
$$

### Step 2: Cardiac Output

$$
CO = SV \times HR
$$

$$
CO = 70 \text{ mL/beat} \times 70 \text{ beats/min} = 4900 \text{ mL/min}
$$

Convert to liters:

$$
CO = 4.9 \text{ L/min}
$$

### Step 3: Ejection Fraction

$$
EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%
$$

$$
EF = \frac{70}{120} \times 100\% = 58.3\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
- SV: **70 mL/beat** (normal)
- CO: **4.9 L/min** (normal resting range: 4–8 L/min)
- EF: **58.3%** (normal range: 55–70%)

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)

### Problem Setup
A patient has a systolic BP of 120 mmHg and diastolic BP of 80 mmHg. Calculate MAP.

### Step 1: Apply formula

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(120 - 80)
$$

### Step 3: Simplify inside parentheses

$$
MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(40)
$$

### Step 4: Multiply

$$
MAP = 80 + 13.33
$$

### Step 5: Final result

$$
MAP \approx 93.3 \text{ mmHg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1/3?** The diastolic phase occupies approximately 2/3 of the cardiac cycle, and systole occupies 1/3. Therefore, MAP weighs DBP twice as heavily as the pulse pressure (SBP - DBP).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — ECG Waveform Origins

| Wave / Segment | Physiological Origin | Mean Vector Direction | Duration | Amplitude (Lead II) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | SA node fires → atria depolarize | Inferior-left (downward-left) | 0.06–0.12 s | < 2.5 mm (0.25 mV) |
| **PR segment** | AV nodal delay (slow conduction) | Isoelectric (no net vector) | 0.02–0.10 s | 0 mm |
| **Q wave** | Septal depolarization (L→R) | Leftward (small, normal) | < 0.04 s | < 25% of R wave |
| **R wave** | Main ventricular depolarization | Leftward-inferior | — | 9–11 mm (0.9–1.1 mV) |
| **S wave** | Late basal LV depolarization | Rightward-superior | — | Variable |
| **J point** | End of QRS, start of ST | Junction point | — | — |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (phase 2 AP) | Isoelectric | 0.08–0.12 s | ± 0.5 mm |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization (epi→endo) | Leftward-inferior | 0.16 s | < 5 mm (0.5 mV) |
| **U wave** | Purkinje/papillary repolarization | Same as T | 0.04–0.08 s | < 1–2 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The ECG Signal Acquisition Chain

This diagram maps the complete flow of ECG signal acquisition — a critical concept for biomedical engineers designing ECG machines, Holter monitors, and wearable ECG devices.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heart: Electrical Activity] --> B[Body Surface Potentials: 0.5 to 5 mV]
    B --> C1[Lead 1: Right Arm to Left Arm]
    B --> C2[Lead 2: Right Arm to Left Leg]
    B --> C3[Lead 3: Left Arm to Left Leg]
    C1 --> D[Electrode-Skin Interface]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Protection Network: ESD and Defib Protection]
    E --> F[Instrumentation Amplifier: Gain 1000]
    F --> G[High-Pass Filter: 0.05 Hz Cutoff]
    G --> H[Low-Pass Filter: 150 Hz Cutoff]
    H --> I[Notch Filter: 50 Hz or 60 Hz]
    I --> J[Main Amplifier: Adjustable Gain]
    J --> K[ADC: 12 to 16 bit, 500 Hz Sampling]
    K --> L[Microcontroller: DSP Processing]
    L --> M1[Display: Real Time Waveform]
    L --> M2[Storage: SD Card or Flash]
    L --> M3[Wireless: BLE WiFi Transmission]
    L --> M4[Analysis: HRV Arrhythmia Detection]
```

## 4.2 Cardiac Cycle Phases Flow Diagram

This flowchart maps the mechanical and electrical sequence of one complete heartbeat, correlating valve states, pressure changes, and ECG waveforms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: SA Node Fires]) --> Pwave[P Wave: Atrial Depolarization]
    Pwave --> PRseg[PR Segment: AV Nodal Delay 0.1 s]
    PRseg --> AtrialKick[Atrial Systole: Atria Contract]
    AtrialKick --> QRS[QRS Complex: Ventricular Depolarization]
    QRS --> IsoC[Isovolumetric Contraction: All Valves Closed]
    IsoC --> Eject[Ventricular Ejection: SL Valves Open]
    Eject --> S1valve[S1 Heart Sound: AV Valves Close]
    S1valve --> T[T Wave: Ventricular Repolarization]
    T --> IsoR[Isovolumetric Relaxation: All Valves Closed]
    IsoR --> S2valve[S2 Heart Sound: SL Valves Close]
    S2valve --> RapidFill[Rapid Ventricular Filling: AV Valves Open]
    RapidFill --> Diastasis[Reduced Filling: Diastasis]
    Diastasis --> Pwave
```

## 4.3 ECG Waveform Anatomy Diagram

This block diagram details each component of the ECG waveform with timing and clinical significance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[P Wave] --> B[PR Segment]
    B --> C[PR Interval]
    C --> D[Q Wave]
    D --> E[R Wave]
    E --> F[S Wave]
    F --> G[J Point]
    G --> H[ST Segment]
    H --> I[T Wave]
    I --> J[U Wave]
    J --> K[QT Interval]
    K --> L[Next P Wave]

    A -.- A1[0.06 to 0.12 s: Atrial Depolarization]
    C -.- C1[0.12 to 0.20 s: Atria to Ventricles]
    E -.- E1[0.06 to 0.10 s Total QRS: Ventricular Depolarization]
    H -.- H1[Isoelectric: Ventricular Plateau]
    I -.- I1[0.16 s: Ventricular Repolarization]
    K -.- K1[0.35 to 0.44 s: Total Ventricular Activity]
```

## 4.4 Conduction System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SA Node: 60 to 100 bpm Pacemaker] --> B[Bachmann Bundle: Atrial Conduction]
    B --> C[AV Node: 0.1 s Delay]
    C --> D[Bundle of His]
    D --> E1[Right Bundle Branch]
    D --> E2[Left Bundle Branch]
    E1 --> F1[Purkinje Fibers Right]
    E2 --> F2[Purkinje Fibers Left]
    F1 --> G[Ventricular Myocardium]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Mechanical Contraction: Systole]
```

## 4.5 Wiggers Diagram Conceptual Block

A simplified block representation of the Wiggers diagram — the classic teaching tool that correlates ECG, heart sounds, ventricular pressure, and valve states.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time Axis: 0.8 seconds per cycle]
        direction LR
        T1[0.0 s: SA fires] --> T2[0.1 s: Atrial systole]
        T2 --> T3[0.15 s: QRS start]
        T3 --> T4[0.2 s: S1, Isovolumetric contraction]
        T4 --> T5[0.3 s: Ventricular ejection]
        T5 --> T6[0.45 s: T wave, Isovolumetric relaxation]
        T6 --> T7[0.5 s: S2, Rapid filling]
        T7 --> T8[0.8 s: Cycle complete]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEBT205]**

**Define the cardiac cycle. Mention the normal duration of one cardiac cycle in a healthy adult.**

#### Model Answer:
The cardiac cycle is the complete sequence of mechanical and electrical events occurring in the heart during one full heartbeat, comprising alternating phases of contraction (systole) and relaxation (diastole) of the atria and ventricles. In a healthy adult with a resting heart rate of 72 bpm, the duration of one cardiac cycle is approximately **0.8 seconds** (calculated as 60/72).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEBT205]**

**List the standard ECG waves and intervals in their correct order. State the normal duration of the PR interval.**

#### Model Answer:
The standard ECG waveform components in order are:
**P wave → PR interval (PR segment) → QRS complex (Q, R, S waves) → ST segment → T wave → U wave**

The normal PR interval duration is **0.12 to 0.20 seconds** (3 to 5 small boxes on standard ECG paper at 25 mm/s), representing the time for the electrical impulse to travel from the atria through the AV node to the ventricles.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCEBT205, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the conduction system of the heart. Add a note on the significance of the AV nodal delay.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the various waves, intervals, and segments of a normal ECG waveform. Mention their normal durations and clinical significance.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Conduction System of the Heart (7 Marks)**

The conduction system of the heart is a specialized network of cardiac muscle cells that generates and conducts electrical impulses, ensuring coordinated contraction. The components in order are:

1. **Sino-Atrial (SA) Node** — Located in the right atrial wall near the SVC opening. It is the natural cardiac pacemaker, generating impulses at 60–100 bpm. **(1 mark)**
2. **Atrial Conduction Pathways (Bachmann's Bundle, internodal tracts)** — Spread the impulse across both atria, causing simultaneous atrial depolarization. **(1 mark)**
3. **Atrio-Ventricular (AV) Node** — Located in the lower interatrial septum. It introduces a critical **0.1 s delay** before relaying the impulse to the ventricles. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Bundle of His** — The only electrical connection between atria and ventricles, traveling through the fibrous skeleton. **(0.5 marks)**
5. **Right and Left Bundle Branches** — Conduct the impulse down the interventricular septum. **(1 mark)**
6. **Purkinje Fibers** — Spread the impulse rapidly through the ventricular myocardium from endocardium to epicardium, ensuring synchronized ventricular contraction. **(1 mark)**

**Significance of the AV Nodal Delay (1 mark):**
